responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association: 2, 1954, No. 1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

2, 1954, No. 1

Erik Homburger Erikson. 'The Dream Specimen of Psychoanalysis.'

Freud's 'Irma dream' is taken to illustrate those aspects of dream interpretation which to-day are considered essential to an exhaustive analysis. The manifest and latent dream thoughts are presented as revealed by Freud in The Interpretation of Dreams. Further details regarding the dreamer's current life situation are added. Emphasis is laid on the manifest content of the dream. The manifest dream is more than a useless shell to be thrown aside in favour of a more worthwhile kernel. The author postulates a style of representation in addition to the dream's striving for representability. This style is a reflection of the individual ego's peculiar time space and frame of reference for its defences, compromises and achievements. A scheme entitled 'Outline of Dream Analysis' is presented. This has four sub-headings: (1) manifest configurations; (2) links between manifest and latent dream material; (3) analysis of latent dream material; (4) reconstruction. A thorough assimilation of part one of such an inventory should allow the observer to realize that the dream life of some persons is characterized by, e.g. a greater clarity of the experience of spatial extension and motion; of others by clear somatic sensations; a rich interpersonal dream life, etc.

The 'Irma dream' is then subjected to analysis with the use of such a chart. The first item is verbal configurations. The numerous mistranslations from German to English are used to analyse further certain latent themes which are withheld in The Interpretation of Dreams. 'Interpersonal configurations in the Dream Population' are next studied. The dreamer's activities are initially described as intrusive, related to phallic behaviour. Later the dreamer appeals for help from higher authority with the ultimate formation of a group with a common conviction. A parallel is drawn between these interpersonal dream configurations and the religious rites of conversion or confirmation. This interpretation does not run counter to Freud's interpretation (the wish for revenge) but complements it. The id supplies the wish making the dream possible, but without appropriate ego measures the dream would fail. In sleep the healthy ego retraces its steps from earliest years. Reference is then made to psycho-social criteria which indicate the extent of this backward movement.

responseSet.title: News, Notes and Comments
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

News, Notes and Comments

19th INTERNATIONAL PSYCHO-ANALYTICAL CONGRESS

The 19th Congress of the International Psycho-Analytical Association will be held from 24 to 28 July, 1955, at Geneva, Switzerland, under the auspices of the Swiss Psycho-Analytical Society.

Membership of the Congress is open to the following persons only:

a. Those included in the list of members of the International Psycho-Analytical Association, published in the 108th Bulletin (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., volume 35, Part 4, 1954).

b. Those non-members of the International Association, whose application has been countersigned by the Secretary of his or her local Psycho-Analytical Society, and has been approved by the Central Executive of the International Association.

c. Candidates (students in training) of Psycho-Analytical Institutes, on the recommendation of their local Institute or Society.

d. Husbands or wives and adult children of members of the Congress.

The Congress Administrative Committee (Chairman: Dr. Raymond de Saussure) has its office at: Tertasse 2, Geneva, Switzerland. All those intending to attend the International Congress are urged to make their arrangements as early as possible.

Provisional Scientific Programme: Sunday, 24 July: Registration and Reception by the Swiss Psycho-Analytical Society. Monday, 25 July: morning: Opening Remarks by the President, Dr. Heinz Hartmann. Scientific Session: four papers to the entire meeting; afternoon: two sections of five papers each with discussion from the floor. Tuesday, 26 July: morning: Discussion of 'The Problem of Transference' (summary of current views, four prepared discussions, discussion from the floor); afternoon: two or more sections of four papers each, with discussion from the floor. Wednesday, 27 July: morning: Panel on Perversions (General Theory of Perversions, several papers dealing with special aspects of perversions, discussion from the floor); afternoon: four papers to the whole meeting. Thursday, 28 July: morning: Business Meeting of the International Psycho-Analytical Association.

responseSet.title: Slips of the Tongue in the Norse Sagas
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Slips of the Tongue in the Norse Sagas

Clarence Seyler

Clarence Seyler

Clarence A. Seyler, D.Sc.
LEATHERHEAD, SURREY

Clarence Seyler  

Two cases of errors in speech are recorded in the Orkneyinga Saga (Icelandic Sagas, Vol. III, Rolls Series 34). I quote from Sir G. W. Dasent's translation. In the Earl's Saga it is related that Earl Rögnvald had fallen out with his kinsman Earl Thorfinn, and the two erst-while friends made bitter warfare on each other. Rögnvald had burnt Thorfinn's house over his head, and Thorfinn escaped unknown. 'No man thought anything else but that Earl Thorfinn had lost his life there', says the Saga. However, as his body was not found it is evident that Rögnvald must have been haunted by the repressed dread that his enemy was still alive. The Saga then continues, 'Earl Rögnvald sat in Kirkwall and drew thither the stores he needed to have for his winter quarters. He had a great band of men and much good cheer. But a little before Yule, Earl Rögnvald fared with a great following into a little Papey to fetch malt to be brewed for Yule. And at even as they were on the isle they sate long over a roasting fire, and he who made up the fire said that the firewood began to fall short. Then the Earl made a slip of the tongue, and these were the words he spoke, "Then are we full old when these (fires) are burnt out." But he meant to have spoken that they would then be full warmed. And as soon as ever he found it out he said: "I have not made a slip of the tongue before this, so that I call it to mind. It comes into my mind what King Olaf my foster-father said at Sticklestead when I took him up for a slip of the tongue: If ever it happens that I made a slip of the tongue I might make up my mind that I should then have but a short time unlived. Maybe that my kinsman Thorfinn is still alive."' Then Thorfinn suddenly appears and sets fire to the house. Rögnvald escapes but is pursued and slain.

Reference to the Icelandic original might be expected to throw light upon the word associations that contributed to the slip. The actual words are: 'Tha maelti jarl mis-macli, ok tok til ortha. Tha eru ver full-gamlir, er thessir (eldir) eru brunnir. En hann vildi that maelti hafa, at tha vaeri their full- bakathir.

responseSet.title: Drives, Affects, Behavior: Contributions to the Theory and Practice of Psycho-Analysis and its Application.  Edited by Rudolph M. Loewenstein.  (New York:  International Universities Press, 1953.  Pp. 398.  $7.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Drives, Affects, Behavior

Contributions to the Theory and Practice of Psycho-Analysis and its Application. Edited by Rudolph M. Loewenstein. (New York: International Universities Press, 1953. Pp. 398. $7.50.)

E. Gostynski

E. Gostynski

E. Gostynski  

As a very obvious sequel to Freud's reformulated theory of instincts, psycho-analysis has been occupied with the interrelation of the two primal drives. Freud's views have been elaborated further in the researches of H. Hartmann, E. Kris, and R. M. Loewenstein, who analyze in detail the general function of theory in psycho-analysis. The fruitful stimulus provided by their theoretical presentations is reflected in the writings of the following authors: R. A. Spitz applies their views on aggression to his own findings in infancy. He traces the differentiation and eventual fusion of the drives through several stages, which are paralleled by corresponding stages of ego development and of establishment of object relationships. The results of damming-up and of defusion of the drives are illustrated on the clinical syndrome of anaclitic depression. M. Schur discusses the theory of anxiety. Stages of ego development are described in which a hierarchy of (danger) situations is correlated with a hierarchy of ego responses, of which 'somatization' and 'desomatization' at the respective ends of the scale are significant concepts for the understanding of psychosomatic phenomena. J. Lampl-de Groot uses the depressive states as a clinical basis for stating her views on the aims of the aggressive drive and on the interrelationship of the primal drives in the development of the ego and of object relationships. Relevant conclusions are drawn to the therapeutic possibilities with depressive patients. D. Burling-ham gives clinical examples of states of enforced or voluntary motor restraint in children and in adults. Her observations bear out the close connection between the muscular system and energy discharges derived from both instinctual drives. H. Flournoy emphasizes the role of aggression against the rival brother in homosexual attraction. The eminence of the two famous inverts of antiquity, Socrates and Plato, is shown to be the result of repression and sublimation. M. Schlumberger confirms Freud's formulation of the structure of the epileptic fit as a condensation of the drives of Oedipus. The death of his patient in a fit is interpreted as an unconscious suicide', a turning of the aggressive drive against the self. K. R. Eissler discusses in a detailed biographical study the moral masochism operative in Eckermann's relationship to Goethe. The gratification of Eckermann's self-destructive tendencies was the condition upon which his literary achievement, the writing of the famous 'Conversations', was based in transcendence of his general mediocrity. The importance of masochism in artistic creativeness is pointed out.

responseSet.title: August Stärke (1880–1954)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

August Stärke (1880–1954)

J. Spanjaard

J. Spanjaard

J. Spanjaard
Harrlem.

J. Spanjaard  

On 16 September, 1954, August Stärke died after a prolonged illness. In him the Dutch Psycho-Analytical Society lost one of its founders and one of its most original members. A few years before his death the Society showed its appreciation of his work by granting him Honorary Membership.

In looking through Stärke's works on psycho-analysis one is struck by the valiant and heroic quality of his penetrating mind and by his determination to understand the fundamental laws of human psychology. They contain thoughts of unmistakable genius which are only marred—as he himself admitted—by a too lapidary style and by a speculative trend which is sometimes hard to follow. All his work shows the signs of a strong revolutionary tendency. Freud's writings, of which On Dreams (1901) was the first to reach him, made a profound impression on him as here at last he found the hypocrisy of the traditional psychology of the conscious mind unmasked. From then on he became a fervent supporter of psycho-analytical theory which to him was often a vehicle for his revolt against the ethical and aesthetic prejudices that dominate science.

Stärke had no less than 146 publications to his name, his early papers being neurological and psychiatrical, the latter mainly psycho-analytical. From 1926 on he wrote a great number of papers on myrmecology; this change of subject may be explained by the sociological content of many of his papers.

Stärke was not a psycho-analyst in the usual sense; he spent his life as a psychiatrist in a mental hospital and one has the impression that psycho-analysis as a therapeutic technique was not the central point of his interest. His most important contributions to psycho-analysis concern the psychoses: 'Comments on Paranoia' (1919 and 1935), in which he located the real persecutor in the scybalum, and Psycho-Analysis and Psychiatry (1921), for which he received the Freud Prize. In the latter he made profound observations on the differences between psychotic and so-called normal or neurotic individuals. The normal person has his delusions too, but they are well integrated; the psychotic differs from him only insofar as the regression of his 'ego instinct' makes it impossible for him to postpone satisfaction of his impulses until their expression becomes socially acceptable or gives secondary gain. The regression of the 'ego instinct' is more important than that of the libido which also occurs in the normal and neurotic individuals. Stärke formulated a number of stages of ego impulses which are an extension of Ferenczi's views on the development of the sense of reality but which are formulated with greater reference to biology and the concept of repetition compulsion.

responseSet.title: Linkage Fantasies and Representations
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Linkage Fantasies and Representations11

Bortimer Ostow M.D.

Bortimer Ostow

Bortimer Ostow, M.D.
NEW YORK

Bortimer Ostow  

SUMMARY

The instinctual need to be united first with mother and later with a substitute object may be expressed by fantasies of linkage with the object. For these fantasies the notion of linkage of the child with its mother by the umbilical cord is paradigmatic. Representations of such linkage include pipelike carriers of fluid, electric cords, hoses, tubes, and clasped hands. It is not necessary to make the incredible assumption that there is infantile knowledge of the umbilical cord. It is more reasonable to assume that when the knowledge of the umbilical cord is acquired in later years, it is used as a paradigm to label (and screen) linkage fantasies which existed much earlier. They themselves come into being when actual experiences of such linkage in infancy act upon the neural constitutional basis for learning and employing the technique of linkage.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953: Martha Wolfenstein, Ph.D.  'Children's Understanding of Jokes', pp. 162–173.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Martha Wolfenstein, Ph.D. 'Children's Understanding of Jokes', pp. 162–173.

Some elements in a child's ability to understand and participate in the convention of a punning joke

77

are studied in a group of fifty-five children aged from six to seventeen. While comprehension of the joking situation has a definite relation to age, intelligence, and freedom of the learning process, the acquisition seems a particularly unstable one. It varies in a single child with his ability to withstand strong feelings aroused by the latent meaning of sometimes purely incidental elements in the joke. For example a joke about morons upset the child's ability to respond by punning, or even to understand what was demanded of him, because of castration anxiety. Some children become expert in the use of jokes to control the environment and deal with the problem of hostility. Others find it intolerable for the adult to abandon his role as supporter of the reality principle by propounding jokes.

78

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953: Margaret Mahler, M.D., and Paula Elkisch, Ph.D.  'Some Observations on Disturbances of the Ego in a Case of Infantile Psychosis', pp. 252–261.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Margaret Mahler, M.D., and Paula Elkisch, Ph.D. 'Some Observations on Disturbances of the Ego in a Case of Infantile Psychosis', pp. 252–261.

A case is reported of a six-year-old boy who manifested a remarkable memory for small details of situations which had been highly emotionalized in his infancy; so much so that he was constantly distressed by meeting such a detail as a purely adventitious element in later experience. This failure in selective forgetting came to the fore during treatment. Desperate crying over stories or pictures connected with babies was a feature of his symptomatology. At one stage he showed catatonia-like states of excitement around the pictures, alternating with a listless stuporous condition. He seemed deliberately to seek out the sweeping excitations inherent in the pictures and the activities which they induced in him to ward off a threatened dissolution of the boundaries of the self. Desperate crying had been an element—which the parents had tried to prevent—in two passively perceived experiences of his first year; one a painful prolonged bodily state, the other prolonged feeding difficulties. In treatment he mimicked and excitedly fed a crying 'picture baby'. The feeding was not meant to assuage the baby's crying, but expressed a reactivated memory entity in which 'crying-baby-pain-feeding' were irreversibly connected. The baby activities corresponded to a quest for restitution in terms of the primary process and its mechanisms, and enabled him to relegate his crying to an image with whom he playfully identified. His unstructured ego could neither modulate its affects nor function in the secondary process by selective perception, selective recall, and selective forgetting. The chronic distress connected with the enduring character of the early painful experiences had defeated the functioning of repression. The irreversible nature of his composite memory patterns prevented the development of a capacity for abstraction, and his extraordinary memory was an inexpedient substitute for the ego's ability to execute repression.

78

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953: Anna Maenchen, Ph.D.  'Notes on Early Ego Disturbances', pp. 262–270.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Anna Maenchen, Ph.D. 'Notes on Early Ego Disturbances', pp. 262–270.

The case of a four-year-old boy is described with a view to assessing the contribution made by maternal deprivation to his severe ego disturbances. Both parents were withdrawn and the area of traumatic handling embraced all the early milestones. Restriction of movement and enforced loneliness were features. Symptoms of echolalia, awkwardness, confusion of pronouns, and inability to establish relationship fitted into the picture of schizophrenia, but there were significant symptomatic omissions.

The introjection of the vague withdrawn mother seems to have been a factor in producing a reality-shy ego. In the child's almost objectless world, an increase in primary narcissism with primary aggression partly turned against the self were inevitable features. The author offers the speculation that internalized aggression may have taken as its object the non-autonomous ego functions and handicapped the process of synthesis.

The retarded speech, still at an imitative level at four years, contained suppressed oral and anal elements beyond which the child had not progressed. An anal regression, activated during treatment, revealed an early fear of abandonment. A secondary aggression (anal spite)

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Annemarie Weil, M.D. 'Certain Severe Disturbances of Ego Development in Childhood', pp. 271–287.

Following on the work of Kanner, Rank, Geleerd, and Mahler, the author describes in detail the clinical characteristics and dynamics of a group of children with severe ego disturbances of a non-neurotic type characterized by arrested development. Immature object relationship with features of omnipotence and faulty and defective reality testing arising from inadequate defensive structuring are typical. This group represents the pool from which some acute and subacute psychoses and a variety of less dramatic, non-regressive narcissistic disturbances emerge. There is a résumé of the literature connecting this type of child with clinical entities found amongst adult patients, and the writer describes the progress and outcome of treatment in a girl patient whom she followed from her tenth to her eighteenth year.

79

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Gregory Rochlin, M.D. 'Loss and Restitution', pp. 288–309.

The treatment is described of a boy of four years suffering from social isolation and incomplete ego differentiation. The history showed repeated separations from the mother, beginning at three months and extending up to the time of treatment. A total change of surroundings was repeatedly involved.

A central symptom which was repeated in the treatment was the child's need to retreat into a cupboard, under stress, and rub his face on his mother's fur coat. The treatment revealed a generalized tendency of the infantile ego to avoid loss by fixing upon a part of the object which it could retain and control. The child's primitive stereotyped patterns of behaviour disclosed a parallel effort to immobilize his own involuntary functions whose autonomous activity threatened him by stimulating phantasies of loss. The child's efforts to keep his objects by incorporation, control, and immobilization involved an almost complete sacrifice of essential reality.

The case is cited as representative of an important group of children who suffer severe object loss at a predominantly narcissistic stage of libido development, prior to the development of object constancy. The ego's need for an object cannot be met by a mere increase in narcissism, and the object is restored in inanimate form at some cost to the reality sense. The disorder described differs in degree and pervasiveness from the normal phenomenon of children solacing themselves with a doll or a piece of fur, which does not result in arrested development.

79

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Berta Bornstein. 'Fragment of an Analysis of an Obsessional Child. The First Six Months of Analysis', pp. 313–332.

This presentation of the case of an eight-year-old girl suffering from insomnia and fear of death was made to facilitate discussion of divergent techniques among a group of analysts. The death fear was fed by the intensity of the child's destructive impulses against mother and sister, and represented a punishment by the superego. With the decrease of the death fear, libidinal aspects of the relation to the father emerged in the form of the 'death feeling', and a frightening pleasurable sensation called 'the creeps', expressing the struggle against masturbation and the fear of self-injury. The interrelatedness of insomnia, death fear, and superego development was discernible in this early phase of the treatment. Seventeen drawings illustrate the case presentation.

79

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Augusta Alpert, Ph.D., and Sylvia Krown. 'Treatment of a Child with Severe Ego Restriction in a Therapeutic Nursery', pp. 333–354.

A four-and-a-half-year-old boy was entered at the Council of Child Development Centre's Therapeutic Nursery in company with his mother for observation of severe anxiety states which made it impossible for him to separate and which seemed connected with traumatic events in his third year. Closer observation revealed severe early impairment of ego functioning characteristic of atypical children. He was given an exclusive relationship with a teacher through which he was enabled to relive correctively steps in object formation. The educational treatment included persistent stimulation to prevent withdrawal and regression. As independent functioning developed group contacts were tolerated and extended. Experiments with guided regression to early fixations following cues given by the child became a productive later feature. 'Continual infusions of borrowed ego strength' have resulted in a mitigation of the demand for symbiotic union, some growth in independent activity, and the development of new defences. Work with both parents went on simultaneously and psycho-analytic treatment of the child is now envisaged. The psychopathology reveals many of the features, both extrinsic and intrinsic, typical or early autistic disturbances.

79

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Emma Plank, M.A. 'Memories of Early Childhood in Autobiographies', pp. 381–393.

The author has come upon a certain type of early memory showing the quality of genuine recollection of significant experiences, which are distinct in its qualities from screen memories. These creative writers seem to have undertaken the recording of their lives under some intense emotional stress and when they were advanced in years. Mostly they describe not memories of events but emotional relationships and experiences, feelings of anxiety, despair, bliss, and early instinctual gratifications, reaching back to the end of the first year of life. The writers were men showing pathological traits but with a high degree of creative integration.

80

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas  

Three examples are offered, two from child and one from an adult analysis, to indicate the clinical significance of the rhyme. 'The rhyme itself is a simple dramatization of the second catastrophic trauma a child experiences (if birth is the first), viz. the arrival of a sibling. Humpty Dumpty then symbolizes both the first- and second-born; and the fall, the sibling's birth, the first-born's hostile wish that the same disaster befall the sibling.' 'The wall represents the mother, mother love, parental esteem, and the narcissistic omnipotence of the first-born.

[[RunningHead]]

Adrenal Cortex

Transactions of the Fourth Conference, 12, 13, and 14 November, 1952. Editor: Elaine P. Ralli, Associate Professor of Medicine, New York University College of Medicine. (New York: Josiah Macy, Jr., Foundation, 1953. Pp. 165. $4.00.)

S. Lucas

S. Lucas

S. Lucas  

This is a record of informal discussions between experts and research workers in this field and is, as would be expected, of a highly technical biochemical and physiological quality. The members, with the exception of Professor Hans Selye of Montreal, work at universities and other centres in the U.S.A., and there are two guests from Great Britain.

In an introduction, Dr. Frank Fremont-Smith stresses the importance of the multi-discipline approach and of free informal discussions, and warns against difficulties which may be partly semantic and partly psychological.

Professor Selye discusses mechanisms through which the adrenal cortex can produce qualitatively different effects. He explains his now well-known theory of stress and 'disease of adaptation'. He describes his experimental work on rats, and there are illustrations of his air pouch techniques in the skin of rats where experiments can be accurately observed and measured.

Selye believes that the adrenal cortex plays an essential part in causing the bodily changes which occur during adaptation to stress (e.g. to cold, infections, injuries, nervous strain, etc.). A stimulus from the target area provokes the pituitary gland to liberate A.C.T.H. The latter causes the adrenals to release mineralo-corticoids (which stimulate inflammation) and gluco-corticoids (which are anti-inflammatory). These are in proportion, but when this is disturbed, diseases of adaptation are produced. S.T.H. (growth hormone of the pituitary) also stimulates inflammation. Selye describes conditioning of these effects at various levels.

Dr. Ingle contends that the adrenals may play only a permissive rôle and that the adreno-cortical steroids may not themselves be the cause of the changes, and that some responses to stress are extra-adrenal. He describes his studies.

Dr. Sayers argues that the concept of diseases of adaptation is very difficult to prove or disprove, and that advances in methodology are necessary rather than to 'juggle concepts'.

Psychiatric Treatment. Proceedings of the Association for Research in Nervous and Mental Diseases

Vol. XXXI. (London: Bailliere, Tindall and Cox, 1953. Pp. xiv + 451. 49 illus. 68 s. 6 d.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This volume covers all aspects of psychiatric treatment except long-term psycho-analysis. Four of the eight sections are devoted to psychological individual and group therapy. They are of considerable interest to the psycho-analyst because they survey most of the work which is taking place in the vast borderland between psycho-analytic and clinical psychiatry. The problem of therapeutic goals and of the evaluation of therapeutic results receives due attention. The rest of the volume is devoted to the present state of physical treatments. Each section consists of four or five papers and is followed by a summary presented by a prominent expert and by extracts from the ensuing discussions. The standard of the contributions is almost invariably high.

141

The Initial Interview in Psychiatric Practice (With Phonograph Records)

By Merton Gill, Richard Newman, Fredrick C. Redlich. (New York: International Universities Press, 1954. Pp. 423. $6.00. Records separate $4.60.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

In recent years an increasing number of psycho-analysts have concerned themselves with the detailed study of the therapist-patient interaction. Recording of interviews with modern techniques has been employed with the aim of obtaining objective data. Many psycho-analysts regard the taking of notes during treatment as harmful to the therapeutic process. The idea of a complete sound recording of sessions, which at the same time are being watched through the one-way mirror, must appear to them a major offence against basic therapeutic principles.

141

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

23, 1953, No. 3

Ida MacAlpine and Richard A. Hunter. 'The Schreber Case. A Contribution to Schizophrenia, Hypochondria and Psychomatic Symptom-Formation.'

Investigating intractable pruritus ani by means of psychotherapy the authors were struck by the similarity of the hypochondriacal symptoms of which their patients complained with those of Schreber. They found their origin in primitive fantasies of intestinal procreation. Though these had secondary homosexual implications for men, and carried a castration threat, they were equally incompatible with mature sexual functioning in men and women.

Schreber's memoirs are also filled with the mysteries of the origin of life—birth, death, resurrection, the migration of souls—and his extensive knowledge of these subjects from every aspect shows the unconscious determination of his thoughts before he became ill. His first psychotic experience—the hallucination of a crackling noise in the wall as though a mouse were there—was a transparent reference to parturition, and the authors show how frequent are unconscious references to periods of nine in his description and the timing of his illness. Moreover, it was his hypochondriacal symptoms, with their similarities to various myths of birth, which caused him his most acute suffering in the catatonic years when his illness was at its height.

They believe that it was Freud's desire to maintain paranoia as an independent clinical entity not necessarily related to schizophrenia which accounted for his emphasis on homosexual mechanisms as primary. Had he concentrated on the hypochondriacal symptoms he would have seen Schreber's doubt about his sex—a frequent phenomenon with schizophrenics—as necessitated by them. Freud himself admitted that no theory of paranoia could be complete which failed to explain the accompanying hypochondria. Moreover, had the hope of being turned into a woman been the most pathogenic factor it is difficult to see how Schreber could have recovered retaining this idea.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

Z. Alexander Aarons. 'Effect of the Birth of a Sister on a Boy in his Fourth Year.'

It is often difficult to reconstruct the psychological effect that the birth of a sibling has in a child's life. The author therefore describes the case of a boy whose symptoms of continuously holding his penis developed six weeks after the birth of his sister, and its resolution in a short analysis, as 'further direct evidence that such an event may precipitate anxiety of psycho-pathological proportions'. He leaves open the problems of the exhaustive genesis of the anxiety and whether the treatment should have been continued.

218

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

I. Peter Glauber. 'A Deterrent in the Study and Practice of Medicine.'

The author examines the neurotic countertransference to their patients of five doctors or medical students. Their difficulties resulted from a confluence of oedipal identifications determining their choice of profession with unsublimated part-object identifications. Typically the part-object was the breast, and ablation of the breast, representing the separation of the self from the mother, was the typical therapy. Its aim was the cathexis of the ego. The oepidal identifications could not function adequately, partly because they took the form of inhibitions, and partly because the solution of the regressive conflict was prerequisite. The abortion of the capacity for identification resulted from trauma at the narcissistic object-finding stage. It was noteworthy that in most of these patients feeding was experienced simultaneously as a libidinal gratification and as an aggressive attempt at reincorporation. The fear of reincorporation mobilized further aggression in the shape of a defensive increase in oral activity, scoptophilia, and general motility, the motility being equated with the emergence of a differentiated self, or birth. In so far as identification presupposes relinquishment or transformation of aggressive energy the capacity for it was still further impeded.

This conflict of ego regression and progression was reflected in necrophilic ruminations. The reattachment of the libido to the mother was represented by the image of the clutching cold mother, projected on to inanimate nature in the form of liquids and semi-solids, e.g. quicksand, water. The other side of the conflict was represented by the image of the 'dead' mother who gives birth to, or liberates, the child, and was projected as solids, e.g. the elation of loitering amid ruins.

218

'Cézanne's Technique and Scoptophilia.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly: Joseph Weiss.  'Cézanne's Technique and Scoptophilia.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

Joseph Weiss. 'Cézanne's Technique and Scoptophilia.'

The critics have adopted strangely contradictory attitudes towards Cézanne's portrayal of mass and depth. Perhaps there was an ambiguity in Cézanne's own vision to account for this. There is evidence that he suffered from a severe scoptophilic conflict. The author knows of a painter whose difficulty in delineating perspective was due to a scoptophilic fear, and it is therefore conceivable that its ambiguous treatment by Cézanne was also due to one. If so, then his concentration on his sensations rather than the object, his disregard of real forms, and his technique of laying on pigment in such a way as to pull the attention continually back and forth be-between surface and depth would all be well suited to maintain detachment in looking and thus minimize such an anxiety. Perhaps one might speculate that this technique also diminishes the observer's anxiety, enabling him to look more intensely.

219

responseSet.documentRefHTML: (1955) The Psychoanalytic Quarterly: 22, 1953, No. 4. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 36:219

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly: 22, 1953, No. 4
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

22, 1953, No. 4

Adelaide M. Johnson. 'Factors in the Etiology of Fixations and Symptom Choice.'

Collaborative work in the simultaneous treatment of parents and children has convinced the author of the importance of the unconscious complicity of the parents in determining the level of fixation and symptom formation in the child. Fixation occurs at the point where the significant parent's regressive impulses are least successfully integrated. The parent projects his or her own guilt on to the child, thus giving it a double burden to bear, but at the same time unconsciously sanctions the expression of the guilt-laden impulse. The parent thus obtains an important vicarious satisfaction. Soon the sharing of guilt and satisfaction alike become necessary for the symbiosis of parent and child. It follows that successful treatment of the child is often impossible without treatment of the parent; while, equally, successful treatment of the child may make the treatment of the parent imperative. Johnson believes that most of the rigidly fixed symptoms of childhood are formed in the way described.

219

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly: William Needles.  'A Note on Orgastic Loss of Consciousness.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

William Needles. 'A Note on Orgastic Loss of Consciousness.'

Freud and other analysts have spoken of a transitory loss of consciousness during intense sexual orgasm. This has therefore sometimes been construed as a criterion of complete orgasm, with implications for the aims of analysis. While agreeing that orgasm produces an alteration in consciousness, Needles believes that careful investigation of accounts of loss of consciousness will reveal (1) that it is a very rare phenomenon, and (2) that it may be an indication of psychopathology rather than of normality.

219

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly: Henry Alden Banker.  'A Dream of an Inhibited Writer.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

Henry Alden Banker. 'A Dream of an Inhibited Writer.'

The dream of this inhibited writer revealed the autarkic wish to be at the same time the suckling mother and the sucking baby, which, following Bergler, the author believes to be characteristic of writers. The patient's associations showed that writing was equated with the giving of milk, so that the inhibition of writing was due to displaced guilt at using writing to dispense with the mother. Such an autarkic repudiation of the mother represents a defence against a masochistic attachment to the pre-oedipal mother.

219

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly: William G. Barrett.  'Mark Twain's Osteopathic Cure.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

William G. Barrett. 'Mark Twain's Osteopathic Cure.'

Late in life Mark Twain was temporarily cured of numerous long-standing ailments by osteopathic treatment. Barrett surveys his life and writings to conclude that his character was dominated by passive and masochistic reaction-formations of strong unconscious sadistic drives. His osteopathic 'cure', with its suggestion of a derivation in such passive satisfactions, is the point of departure for these observations.

220

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

William N. Evans. 'Evasive Speech as a Form of Resistance.'

Every patient has his own method of evasive speech. Evans describes a few varieties and shows how each type of evasion may be related to the context of behaviour in which it first manifested itself and how this type of evasion pervades every aspect of the patient's activities.

Menninger.  'Psychological Aspects of the Organism Under Stress.  Part 1:  The Homeostatic Regulatory Function of the Ego.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association: Karl A. Menninger.  'Psychological Aspects of the Organism Under Stress.  Part 1:  The Homeostatic Regulatory Function of the Ego.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Karl A. Menninger. 'Psychological Aspects of the Organism Under Stress. Part 1: The Homeostatic Regulatory Function of the Ego.'

This work has been partly motivated by a dissatisfaction with current psychiatric nosologies and the conviction of the usefulness of Freudian concepts in clinical practice. The author believes that the concept of homeostasis helps to provide a better understanding of human behaviour. In general, applications of the homeostatic principle to psychology have followed biological and physical systems rather slavishly. Such a conception is too narrow. Freud utilized the concept of homeostasis as a basic principle in his metapsychology in The Interpretation of Dreams. Twenty-five years later in Beyond the Pleasure Principle he reconsidered this and advanced a more complex hypothesis. The work of von Bertalanffy is quoted and points of similarity with Freud's theories considered. Menninger conceives of homeostasis as operating at many levels of integration. In addition to its biological implications it must be seen as an index of the current balance of the life and death forces which are reconciled and bound within each new homeostatic balance of the personality system. Five topics are discussed in relation to the homeostatic function. (1) Instinct theory; the usefulness of the life and death instinct theory is commented upon. The difficulties which biologists have in understanding the integrative versus destructive processes as expressions of instinctual trends are due to the nature of the material with which psychoanalysts and psychiatrists work. The author discusses the theoretical question whether the destructive trends are the derivatives of an instinctual drive originally self-directed. He believes that the acceptance of a destructive drive but not the death instinct undermines the basic premises upon which the hypothesis is based. (2) Ego functions; while the ego may be defined as a controlling mechanism it may also be conceived of as an organized expression and product of the constructive, synthetic impulses which characterize the life instinct. The ego has to reconcile the conflicting demands of both instincts and it can be looked upon as a cybernetic mechanism. (3) The somatic ego; it is artificial to exclude from the ego functions the needs of the body for oxygen, water, etc. The maintenance of the physio-psycho-sociological balance is the chief function of the ego. (4) Ego stresses; the ego is continually 'under fire'. It has to adjust to demands emanating from internal and external sources. If the ego is regarded as a complex homeostatic regulatory mechanism failure to adjust takes on a wider meaning. All 'illness' falls somewhere in a range of ego system decompensation. The author illustrates the regulatory function of the ego with an example of an incident which stimulates an aggressive response. (5) Normal minor emergency devices; a number of such devices are mentioned, including: (a) touch; (b) soft voices; (c) efforts to establish human contacts, etc. These devices may be sufficient to resolve tension. If the threat to the personality becomes greater the regulatory functions of the ego are increasingly strained and are forced to resort to more disadvantageous measures.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association: Leo Angelo Spiegel.  'Acting Out and Defensive Instinctual Gratification.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Leo Angelo Spiegel. 'Acting Out and Defensive Instinctual Gratification.'

Features from the analyses of two patients are presented to illustrate how exhibitionistic and masochistic impulses may be allowed gratification while essentially serving a defensive purpose, i.e. to avoid the narcissistic injury arising from non-fulfilment of infantile genital wishes. In both cases there was a common denominator in the affective sphere. Both patients were constantly being humiliated and betrayed. This had occurred in the past and in the present both within and outside the analysis. The transference was permeated by sadomasochistic influences. Spiegel lays emphasis on the necessity to interpret not the id derivatives now expressed regressively but the defence against the injury to narcissism.

221

responseSet.documentInfoXML: <artinfo arttype="ABS" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ABS" id="IJP.036.0223B" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1955</artyear><artvol actual="36">36</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">223</artpgrg><artvar>B</artvar><arttitle> Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association</arttitle><artsub>Edith Jacobson.  'Contribution to the Metapsychology of Psychotic Identifications.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association: Edith Jacobson.  'Contribution to the Metapsychology of Psychotic Identifications.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Edith Jacobson. 'Contribution to the Metapsychology of Psychotic Identifications.'

Jacobson suggests that the study of psychotic identifications will be furthered by a closer enquiry into the differences between normal ego and superego identifications and the corresponding mechanisms in psychosis. Ego identifications differ from early infantile identification mechanisms in that the former are realistic in so far as they result in changes within the ego, while the latter are essentially magical, based on phantasies, or the temporary belief of being one with or becoming the object, regardless of reality. In psychotic states normal object relationships disintegrate and are replaced by such pre-oedipal magic identification mechanisms. Normally these magic identifications find some expression in the ego-ideal and the superego. The core of the ego-ideal consists of idealized parental images blended with archaic, aggrandized images of the self. There is normally an awareness of this unrealistic element in the superego and this is reflected in the recognition of the discrepancies which exist between ego and superego standards. An account of a manic-depressive and a schizophrenic patient is given to illustrate that in manic depressives the magic identifications aim at and succeed in maintaining a dependence of the self on a powerful superior love object. In the schizophrenic the regressive processes go much further. The ego and superego systems deteriorate to a dangerous extent, with a dissolution of the superego and its decomposition into threatening parental figures. The need of the manic-depressive to cling on to the object at any price is shown in the tendency of the patient to treat himself as if he were the love object. The schizophrenic behaves as if he were or believes himself to be the object because of his inclination to destroy and replace the object by the self or let the self be annihilated and replaced by the object.

223

[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Sylvan Keiser. 'Orality Displaced to the Urethra.'

Three patients are described who had a compulsive need to believe themselves to be and to appear normal. This need became so dominating as to influence every sphere of life. In fact these patients suffered with numerous symptoms which they denied. In each case there was an exaggerated oral drive which spread to all the orifices including the urethra. The oral greed was replaced by a fear of anal and urethral penetration. All the three patients were found to have: (1) orally dependent mothers who had had gastrectomies for peptic ulcers; (2) fathers who were successful business men but castrating parents; (3)

responseSet.title: Steps in Psychotherapy: By J. Dollard, F. Auld, and A. M. White.  Edited by Dael Wolfle.  (New York:  Macmillan, 1953.  Pp. 222.  $3.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Steps in Psychotherapy

By J. Dollard, F. Auld, and A. M. White. Edited by Dael Wolfle. (New York: Macmillan, 1953. Pp. 222. $3.50.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

The authors describe the treatment of a patient who was suffering from attacks of depression, a feeling of hopelessness, and much weeping; in short, a neurotic depressive reaction. The therapy is stated to have been conducted under the two stars of Freudian psychopathology and reinforcement-learning-theory. The former provided the power and the latter the precision of the procedure. It was a short-term technique, designated by the authors brief psychotherapy, which, according to their definition, is treatment lasting from six to thirty hours.

The authors do more, however, than describe the treatment, as they show exactly how a student psychotherapist was supervised and trained by an experienced teacher and colleague. The title of the book is an apposite one, as indeed the procedure was conducted by steps, the left foot forward being the student therapist's session with the patient, and the right the supervision session. At each such interview a sound recording of all that was said by the patient and student therapist was played over in the presence of student and supervisor. Such supervision, conducted in a precise and tight-reined way, imposes considerable stress upon the student therapist, as it threatens him with criticism which might well paralyze his initiative. In this case, however, the helpful approach of the supervisor and the stability of the student therapist combine to produce steady progress.

Though the technique of free association was used, the therapy was pushed forward by 'labelling', a term used by the authors to describe the providing of words and sentences by the therapist, and the linking of them to the vague but intense, inarticulate feelings of the patient. Psycho-analysis is much more comprehensive than the treatment described, but in such treatment there was the same emphasis upon the study of problems brought forward in the session, in the light of their origins. In other words due importance was given to the present as the re-enactment of the past.

responseSet.title: The Sexually Adequate Female: By Frank S. Caprio.  (New York:  The Citadel Press, 1953.  Pp. 219.  $3.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Sexually Adequate Female

By Frank S. Caprio. (New York: The Citadel Press, 1953. Pp. 219. $3.00.)

M. Dale

M. Dale

M. Dale  

In this book the physical and emotional causes of sexual inadequacies, especially frigidity, in women are discussed; the measures to correct them are described, and suggestions are made and advice given on how to maintain happiness in the marital relationship.

With pleasant frankness, in readable style, the author fills a need in the literature of marital relations for laymen and doctors alike. He draws on a wide medical and psychiatric experience and analytical literature of the various schools of thought. Although Freud's ideas are given some respect the term 'Oedipus-complex' or '-problem' is strictly avoided. 'Electra-complex' is substituted for it, and it is a pity that the author has not made more use of Freud's Three Essays on the Theory of Sex. The excellent case material and the large variety of symptoms would have made a more coherent, more closely knit and clearer pattern.

responseSet.title: News, Notes and Comments
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

News, Notes and Comments

A Bust of Sigmund Freud in the Arkadenhof of the University of Vienna

On 4 February, 1955, a bust of Sigmund Freud, the donation of Dr. Ernest Jones, was unveiled. Preceding this ceremony a congregation took place in the Auditorium Maximum of the University. After an opening speech by the Rector, Professor Dr. Hans Radon, the Dean of the Medical Faculty, Dr. Franz Brücke, brilliantly presented Freud's connection with the University. Dr. Hans Hoff, Professor of Neurology and Psychiatry, who inaugurated the erection of this memorial, described Freud's revolutionary influence on modern psychiatry and on the sciences in general. He was followed by Dr. E. Krapf and by Dr. Leo Bartemeier, who spoke on behalf of the American Medical Association, the American Psychiatric Association, and the American Psychoanalytic Association. Finally, Dr. Alfred Winterstein, President of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Association, recalled to the congregation how Freud, the man and the scientist, came to make those far-reaching discoveries which have become the foundation of the science of psycho-analysis.

The bust bears the inscription:

ostaklein' ainigmat' edeikai kratistoshnaner (Greatest of men, he held the key to the deepest mysteries.)

On the evening preceding the unveiling a banquet took place, arranged by the Vienna Psycho-Analytic Society. Dr. Alfred Winterstein, who presided, welcomed the delegates from abroad, the head of the University Clinic of Neurology and Psychiatry, Dr. Hans Hoff, and the Director of the Museum of Art, Dr. E. Buschbeck. Messages of congratulation were received from the psychoanalytic organizations of Switzerland (Dr. Philip Sarasin), Germany (Dr. Carl Müller-Braunschweig), Great Britain (Dr. Sylvia Payne), Italy (Professor Perotti), Belgium (Dr. Lechat), France (Princesse Marie Bonaparte). Dr. Leo Bartemeier replied to the toast.

The Third International Congress on Criminology will be held in London from 12 to 18 September this year, under the chairmanship of Dr. Denis Carroll. Aspects of Recidivism (Statistics, Description, Causes, Prognosis, and Treatment) will be the main point of discussion. Address: 28 Weymouth Street, London, W.1, England.

responseSet.title: Konversion Und Reversion Klinischer Neurosen: By Hans Rehder.  (Köln:  Artze Verlag, 1953.  Pp. 232.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Konversion Und Reversion Klinischer Neurosen

By Hans Rehder. (Köln: Artze Verlag, 1953. Pp. 232.)

Lajos Levy

Lajos Levy

Lajos Levy  

This book has made it its task to demonstrate that behind all neuroses the primary driving force is 'fear'. In many very interesting case-histories which all testify to the very suggestive personality and excellent healing capacities of the author, there has been constructed a new theory of neuroses which, compared to the tenets of psycho-analysis, reveals itself as superficial and most incompre-hensible, particularly by a very peculiar nomenclature.

The writer has expanded the original conception of conversion and has attempted to define the different forms of neuroses as always induced by 'conversion'. According to him there are various sorts of 'conversion'; he distinguishes a total, a partial, an inverting, a passive, an active sort of conversion and at last a 'Konversionsahnliche Disharmonie'. According to these assumptions there are various forms of neuroses which are viewed as simple conversion-acts of 'fear'. But the 'fear' has many grades, as for instance, exaltation, pavor, terror, horror, etc. The task of the therapy is to induce a 'reversion'. Now there is presented a theoretical approach to his really remarkable results which is hardly understandable by the reader. Why the author's mystic theory should give better directives for seeing the proper structure of the neuroses than the concepts of psycho-analysis, remains a sheer puzzle for us. The jargon of his theoretical explanations is a word-salad indeed.

All in all, this book is again striking evidence for the old fact that in the science and art of medicine there are many wide, often unbridgeable gaps between theory and practice. One can be a very excellent physician, a very efficient gastro-intestinal specialist, but fail totally as a theoretical surveyor of one's own successful practical experiences.

399

responseSet.title: Studies in Scientific Hypnosis: By Jerome M. Schneck, M.D.  Nervous and Mental Disease Monographs.  No. 84.  (New York:  Coolidge Foundation, 1954.  Pp. xvi + 333.  $6.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Studies in Scientific Hypnosis

By Jerome M. Schneck, M.D. Nervous and Mental Disease Monographs. No. 84. (New York: Coolidge Foundation, 1954. Pp. xvi + 333. $6.50.)

E. Gostynski

E. Gostynski

E. Gostynski  

This volume is mainly made up of papers previously published elsewhere. Nearly all the hypnotic techniques described, in current use by the author and others, are known and do not need separate review. The exploration by hypnosis of particular problems (audiometry, vocational guidance), and the combination with psychological test material, is of interest.

Further application is demonstrated in the method of hypno-analysis, in which hypnotic suggestions are given with a broad psycho-analytic orientation. This newer approach claims wider goals; it aims at insight as well as at direct symptom relief. While it is only to be expected that with this procedure the hypnotist should encounter resistances and transference, the divergence between the psycho-analytic and the hypno-analytic methods becomes obvious at this point: the hypnotist uses his technique to undercut resistances and transference and to analyse them only if necessary, the analyst uncovers deeper layers by constant analysis of resistance and transference; the hypnotist aims predominantly at remembering, the analyst at operating Freud's classical triad: remembering, repeating, and working through.

These points emerge on reading the presented case material which covers a wide variety of symptomatology, perhaps with some preponderance of hysterical conditions: phobias, conversions, fugues, anxiety states, amnesias. Owing to the fragmentary nature of the case reports it cannot always be ascertained to what extent the author's hypno-analytic method results in wider insight on the part of the hypnotist or on that of the patient or both. Perhaps the eclectic adoption of psycho-analytic findings is also responsible for a lack of clarity on this issue. It is indeed to be regretted that the author has not used his evidently wide experience with hypnosis for a metapsychological study of the changes brought about in the patient's mental economy by this form of treatment. The self-hypnotic dreams, for instance, illuminate well the introjection of the hypnotist by the patient and the rôle of the introjected hypnotist in the patient's mental economy. Critical research of this kind might have lent more weighty support to the author's sanguine plea for the wider application of hypnosis in general and of hypno-analysis in particular.

responseSet.title: Psychotherapy of Psychosis: By Gustav Bychowski.  (New York:  Grune and Stratton, 1952.  Pp. 328.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy of Psychosis

By Gustav Bychowski. (New York: Grune and Stratton, 1952. Pp. 328.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

It is sometimes assumed among psycho-analysts that extensive hospital experience in clinical psychiatry is not an advantage in the psycho-analytical understanding of the psychoses. This book proves that any such generalization would be mistaken. The author is both a clinical psychiatrist and a psycho-analyst of great experience and erudition. His views on the origin of the psychoses and their treatment are catholic, and he does not adhere to a strictly psychogenic theory. He regards physical treatments as beneficial in certain psychotic states. He is nevertheless firmly convinced of the value of psycho-analytical psychotherapy in the psychoses. The author has a distinguished record as a student of schizophrenia, especially of its regressive features, about which he wrote an excellent monograph in 1923. In his therapeutic approach he stands nearer to Federn than to any other writer. Therapeutic failures in schizophrenia are in his view often due to the indiscriminate use of the method of free association on the one hand, and to the disregard of narcissistic defences on the other hand. Fromm-Reichmann's and Rosen's methods are critically reviewed and Sechehaye's observations are fully and sympathetically discussed. The author believes that in the majority of depressive states we must assume a combination of psychological and biological, i.e. exogenous and endogenous factors. He does not accept Klein's views on mental mechanisms in early infancy. He expects progress in therapy from a combination of biological and psychological methods. This is an unusually well balanced presentation of the state of psycho-analytical psychotherapy of the psychoses. The book can be highly recommended to everybody working in this difficult field.

400

responseSet.title: Trends of Life: By F. Wood Jones, F.R.S. (London:  Edward Arnold, 1953.  Pp. 191.  10 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Trends of Life

By F. Wood Jones, F.R.S. (London: Edward Arnold, 1953. Pp. 191. 10 s. 6 d.)

John Pratt

John Pratt

John Pratt  

In as short a compass as 180 excellently written pages the author has set out to convey 'in the plain language of everyday use … a summary of the conclusions derived from many years spent in the quest of the problems of life and the baffling manifestations of its activities in the sequence of living creatures.' Professor Wood-Jones, F.R.C.S., F.R.S., was latterly, until his recent demise, the Sir William Collins Professor of Human and Comparative Anatomy, and Conservator of the Anatomical Museum of the Royal College of Surgeons. He was also the author of a number of highly interesting and valuable publications of which some of the best known are especially concerned with examples of the indivisibility of function and of anatomical structure, e.g. on the curious pseudo-thumb of the giant Panda that in a bamboo-eating animal has been evolved from a modified sesamoid bone and a displaced muscle attachment in a hand that has been already highly specialized in a different direction. (Proc. Zool. Soc. Lond. B. 109; 113.) Wood-Jones has also elsewhere given original examples of the various structural adaptations of the teeth, claws, beaks, etc., in various animals to the function, highly important for survival, of fur and feather toilet. His assertion that the rough tongue of the cat tribe is an adaptation to toilet habits has been accepted by Julian Huxley. His argument that the very early appearance of the peculiar squatting facets on the os astragalus and tibia of the infant Australian aborigine constitutes a self-evident demonstration of Lamarckian inheritance is probably well known. In the present volume he has illustrated his arguments with a number of impressive examples of the more highly adaptive features of living organisms. For any reader (e.g. a simpleminded mechanist) who still needs to be impressed in this respect, this and all other popular works of this author are to be recommended. A more sophisticated reader might however regard these same examples as so much bait to the hook of the late Professor's 'conclusions', which to this reviewer appear to be not so much conclusions as examples of anthropomorphic thinking, made possible by a disregard of current views of the nature and function both of scientific theory and of its concepts. The conclusions in question are expressed throughout the book, which is a special plea for Vitalist, Lamarckist, and teleological explanations of the nature of life and of the evolutionary process. The second, third and fourth chapter headings read as follows: 'The concept of vitalism', 'The history of thought on vitalism', 'The concept of teleology'. In the course of his Lamarckist observations Wood-Jones frequently had occasion to refer to Charles Darwin; but it is interesting to note that in this (albeit brief and popular and partisan) book, there is not a single reference to Haldane, Julian Huxley, or Fisher; and it is even more interesting to note that although the word 'marvellous' is employed on no less than four separate occasions, the word 'mutation' is not used even once. As a vitalist, the Professor is certainly to be numbered among a distinguished company that has in the past included Buffon, the anatomist Hunter, Erasmus Darwin, Lamarck, and even Von Uexkull: to say nothing of a literary personage, Samuel Butler, whom he quotes more than once, as well as various religious persons, whom he does not: and the philosopher Bergson. However, the employment of the anthropomorphic concept of 'force' or of 'power' is usually nowadays admitted to be permissible only as an operational concept and is as an explanation usually agreed to be unsatisfactory.

responseSet.title: The Emotional Problems of Childhood: By H. Joseph and G. Zern.  (New York:  Crown Publishers, 1954.  Pp. ix + 310.  $3.75.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Emotional Problems of Childhood

By H. Joseph and G. Zern. (New York: Crown Publishers, 1954. Pp. ix + 310. $3.75.)

E. Anthony

E. Anthony

E. Anthony  

The enormous success enjoyed by Spock's little pocket-book in America has reminded the publishers of a vast public eagerly waiting to be told just how to bring up their children. A spate of parent guides must be expected to reach us within the next few years.

The Joseph-Zern guide to normal and abnormal development has a psycho-analytic background which, compared with Spock, is less smoothly incorporated into the popular style and text, the consequence being at times a curious juxtaposition of the superficial and the dry, the platitudinous and the profound. However, the information and advice given is usually sober and sensible and reflects current mental hygiene views. There are no delightful drawings, as in Spock, to enliven the text, and some of the examples chosen to illustrate the themes have a cultural loading which may confuse the non-American.

404

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Problems of Infancy and Childhood

Transactions of the Sixth Conference, 1952, edited by Milton J. E. Senn. (New York: Josiah Macy, Jr. Foundation, 1953. Pp. 160. $2.50.)

E. Anthony

E. Anthony

E. Anthony  

The multi-disciplined approach sponsored by the Foundation is expressly designed 'to promote effective communication across departmental walls'. With this laudable aim in view, a representative group of scientific workers is invited to meet around a particular topic and exchange their ideas. The proceedings are informal and the language conversational. This makes for easy reading, but at its less edited and more banal moments, the intention can be quite exasperating.

On the whole this volume is much more to the point than some of its predecessors, and good chairmanship keeps the red herring from trailing too blatantly across its pages. Escalona gives a balanced account of the phenomenology of babyhood as she feels it. Her eclectic approach contrives to bring in and interrelate a mass of developmental data. She refers to her method as 'phenomenology by inference' which attempts to scale the dimensions of infantile experience, and find a frame of reference which would make it possible to understand each child's idiosyncratic habit of perceiving the world and responding to it. The unconscious dimension is necessarily omitted.

Wolf reports on a research project at the Child Study Centre at Yale dealing with the longitudinal investigation of infants from the period of pregnancy onwards. With psycho-analytic theory as a basis, the project aims at testing the capacity to predict the course of development; for example (a successful prediction) that specific fears with toys would develop with a certain child.

Steward describes excessive crying in infants taking into account the mother-child relationship at one pole and such investigations as barium meals, nasal smears, eosinophil counts and skin reactions at the other pole. It is worth noting that a span of this size is almost a commonplace to-day. Also worth noting, as a refreshing change, is the fact that the father and his feelings are given a prominent part in the production and prevention of a syndrome occurring in the first three months of life.

responseSet.title: Understanding the Japanese Mind: By James Clark Moloney.  (New York:  Philosophical Library, 1954.  Pp. 252.  $3.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding the Japanese Mind

By James Clark Moloney. (New York: Philosophical Library, 1954. Pp. 252. $3.50.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

A description is given of the social pressures to which every home-bred Japanese is subjected during his development. The author attributes the low incidence of schizophrenia among Japanese to the secure and satisfying breast-feeding, mother-child relationship which is the lot of nearly all Japanese. Also, the vantage point of a child, slung securely on the mother's back, looking over her left shoulder, gives intimacy of bodily contact, and a moving panoramic world full of variation and interest. From then on the period of training in cleanliness is quite different, being both harsh and rigorous. Thereafter social pressures upon the child are relentless, and all the rewards are for conformity, social sanctions being brought to bear against deviants from the prescribed pattern of behaviour. Because the social disciplines are so formalized and rigid, so authoritarian and so all-embracing, individuality is restricted. The price of this conformity at the expense of individuality is paid by the Japanese in their tendency to develop psychosomatic illnesses and to some extent in the peculiar socially sanctioned ritual cruelties to the self or to others.

With the Emperor at the apex, the hierarchical stratified structure of Japanese society is tightly knit and definite. The author states that the lack of aggressive behaviour, in other words the tendency to conformity even in Japanese psychotics, is remarkable. Not unexpectedly in view of this type of society, the Japanese tend to identify themselves with the enemy (the aggressor). The Japanese look for a precedent for any of their actions and the author suggests, rather gloomily, that the precedent of dropping atomic bombs on Japan may well have an unpleasant sequel in the future.

Regarding psycho-analysis and the Japanese, the author brings evidence to show that Japanese do not accept or practise psycho-analysis in the Western sense of the term. He considers the dilemma which confronts Japanese psycho-analysts, namely whether to regard the freeing of the individual as the desirable goal, or to regard the rendering of the person more able to conform to the restrictions of society as a more desirable one. That these aims are incompatible is beyond doubt. That there should be a dilemma for Japanese analysts is inevitable in view of their own social conditioning, and in view of the fact that very few of them have been analysed by Western analysts. Even so, as a result of their early conditioning, Japanese psycho-analysts tend to adopt the camouflage of conformity with psycho-analysis as we know it, in their writings for external consumption, while in their writings for internal consumption they conform to the demands of their own social milieu and adapt and modify psycho-analysis to fit those demands. The author discusses their modification of psycho-analytic concepts to suit their own socio-political ideology, and calls the process by which they do it, Syncretization.

responseSet.title: Psychology and Worship: By R.S.Lee.  (London:  S.C.M. Press, 1955.  Pp. 110.  7 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychology and Worship

By R.S.Lee. (London: S.C.M. Press, 1955. Pp. 110. 7 s. 6 d.)

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones  

This book consists of six lectures, the Burroughs Memorial lectures for 1953. The author is the Vicar of the University Church of St. Mary's in Oxford. As he showed a few years ago in his excellent book Freud and Christianity, he has a thorough knowledge and understanding of psycho-analysis, the findings of which he fully accepts. Here, as in his former book, he is concerned to reconcile those findings with the teachings of Christianity, and, moreover, to ascertain what modifications of the latter may be desirable.

Starting from the premise that the idea of God first develops out of three elements—the super-ego, the ego-ideal, and the infantile father-image (p. 65)—he admits that many of the various attitudes towards God later are still influenced by those childhood sources, and his aim is to purify them in the adult conception of God. It is as if the child on growing up discovers that there really is a God, thus confirming his early expectations, needs, and desires, but he now has to adopt a more mature attitude towards the Deity. The same thesis holds good for other religious beliefs and rituals. There are deep motives urging towards the belief in immortality, but its objective truth is demonstrated by the historical fact of Christ's resurrection from the dead.

407

responseSet.documentRef: Stengel, E. (1955) Clinical Psychiatry: By William Mayer-Gross, Eliot Slater and Martin Roth.  (London:  Cassell, 1954.  Pp. 652.  50 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis  36:414
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Stengel, E. (1955) Clinical Psychiatry: By William Mayer-Gross, Eliot Slater and Martin Roth. (London: Cassell, 1954. Pp. 652. 50 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 36:414
responseSet.documentRefXML: Stengel, E. (1955) Clinical Psychiatry: By William Mayer-Gross, Eliot Slater and Martin Roth. (London: Cassell, 1954. Pp. 652. 50 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 36:414

responseSet.title: Clinical Psychiatry: By William Mayer-Gross, Eliot Slater and Martin Roth.  (London:  Cassell, 1954.  Pp. 652.  50 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Psychiatry

By William Mayer-Gross, Eliot Slater and Martin Roth. (London: Cassell, 1954. Pp. 652. 50 s.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

There is an urgent need for an up-to-date textbook of psychiatry. The authors of this impressive volume have attempted to fill this gap. They aim at a multidimensional approach which would give each factor contributing to mental illness its due place. They are very critical of the psycho-dynamic orientation of American and part of British psychiatry and have set their minds on stemming this trend. Of American psychiatry they present an incomplete picture. They quote extensively from a short and provocative article by Lidz written for German psychiatrists. (A book such as Dynamic Psychiatry, edited by Alexander and Ross, gives a much truer picture of the American psychiatry scene.) In discussing psycho-analysis they lean too heavily on critics who do not think much of its contribution to psychiatry. With all due respect for Jaspers, the Allgemeine Psychopathologie is not a reliable guide for those who want to make up their own minds on this issue. Nevertheless, the psychiatric reader will get good value from this book, provided he realizes that the time has gone by when one could expect any single writer, or team of writers sharing the same orientation, to present all the main currents in psychiatry fairly and authoritatively. It would be a pity if, as the result of its strong bias, the considerable merits of Clinical Psychiatry should not be appreciated by those readers who do not share the authors' views about recent trends in Western psychiatry. This reviewer has often emphasized that the descriptive-phenomenological, genetic, and psycho-dynamic approaches are not only compatible but complementary. It is to be hoped that this book will not be quoted in refutation of this opinion. Apart from the weaknesses inherent in a presentation such as this, the standard of the book is high, but the level of excellence is highest in the sections dealing with the subjects on which the authors are experts, and these are many. There is a useful list of references covering 35 pages.

responseSet.title: The Appraisal of Intelligence: By A. W. Heim.  (London:  Methuen, 1954.  Pp. 171.  12 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Appraisal of Intelligence

By A. W. Heim. (London: Methuen, 1954. Pp. 171. 12 s. 6 d.)

S. Lindsay

S. Lindsay

S. Lindsay  

This work is refreshing and stimulating, and should be read by all who are interested in the psychology of intelligence. The main theme, which is clearly stated throughout, is a plea for 'appraisal' of intelligence, in contrast to 'measurement' or 'subjective judgement'. Dr. Heim criticizes the tendency in many testers to supplant older methods of investigation rather than develop them, and rejects the presupposition that there is some one attribute 'intelligence' which is capable of strictly quantitative measurement. Factor analysis is considered to be sterile when treated as an end in itself, and suggestions are offered for research along non-factorist lines. She asserts that a test may become too 'pure' and too 'reliable' to be any longer a valuable instrument. Proposals are made for a new approach to the appraisal of intelligence, based on the indivisibility of the subject's cognitive and orectic processes—a compromise between the formal intelligence test and the comparatively unstructured projective test of personality. However, one feels that the elucidation of the unconscious dynamics of intelligent activity by a psycho-analytic approach would enrich this type of work, which goes so far to perfect the assessment of intelligence for practical purposes.

418

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1: Otto Isakower.  'Spoken Words in Dreams.  A Preliminary Communication.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1

Otto Isakower. 'Spoken Words in Dreams. A Preliminary Communication.'

In 1939 the author pointed to the auditory sphere as the nucleus of the superego and hinted that a further investigation of dreams with this idea in mind might be profitable. Here he states what he considers to be the logical conclusion of his argument—that speech elements in the manifest content of dreams are a direct contribution from the superego. From supporting analytic material he suggests that the nearer the sleeper is to wakefulness the clearer become the superego's comments on the dream, as though casting the mental eye on the dream during the waking process assisted in reinstating reality. He then traces adumbrations of his theory in Freud.

421
