Bacon.  'Psycho-Analytic Observations on Cardiac Pain.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1: Catherine L. Bacon.  'Psycho-Analytic Observations on Cardiac Pain.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1

Catherine L. Bacon. 'Psycho-Analytic Observations on Cardiac Pain.'

Cardiac pain, frequently radiating down the left arm, has been observed in several patients in analysis during a conflict of receptive and aggressive drives. It is known that oral receptive strivings stimulate the parasympathetic and aggressive strivings the sympathetic nervous systems. The physiology of the heart is such that the combined stimulation of the two could easily result in cardiac anoxia. Now oral receiving often induces a sense of obligation and obligation a sense of defiance. If such a patient revolts against the donor (parent) he feels fearful, if he does not, he feels outraged at the loss of his freedom. In either event, if the conflict is severe and occurs with the patient in the vagal phase, cardiac pain may ensue from this concurrent stimulation of the sympathetic. This is theoretically much less likely to occur if tension can be reduced in either system. Interpretation should therefore be directed (a) towards diminishing the patient's oral guilt and anxiety arising from hostility, and (b) towards slowing down his reaction-time to oral-receptive stimuli and thus allowing the necessary time for psychic and physical digestion.

421

'The Structure of Homosexual Acting Out.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1: Gustav Bychowski.  'The Structure of Homosexual Acting Out.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1

Gustav Bychowski. 'The Structure of Homosexual Acting Out.'

The analysis of homosexuals is characterized by alternating phases of working through and acting out. Bychowski suggests the following explanations of the acting out. (1) The prerequisite is an archaic, narcissistic ego-structure. (2) The choice of partners is therefore based on narcissistic projection—it is to this that they owe their high if transient value. (3) The ego cannot bind libidinal excitations sufficiently not to be overwhelmed by discharges along primitive lines. As in the traumatic neurosis the ego is striving not merely for pleasure but for mastery of the libidinal and aggressive impulses and of the archaically cathected objects. (4) The ego is filled with conflicting identifications with parental images. Since each identification is dethroned as soon as it is made, considerable counter-cathexis is required to maintain them. As a result of this further weakening of the ego, object-cathexis comes to be felt as ego-loss. (5) Because of the low potential of free ego libido there is a lack of the neutralized mental energy indispensable for control. Therefore even identification cannot be maintained on a purely intrapsychic level, so that acts of genital and motor discharge become necessary for the un-bound and un-neutralized libidinal energies.

422

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1: Joseph G. Kepecs.  'Observations on Screens and Barriers in the Mind.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1

Joseph G. Kepecs. 'Observations on Screens and Barriers in the Mind.'

Mental states described as blankness, or by such phrases as 'There is nothing in my mind', may be investigated by requesting the subject to describe the experience. Often this will arouse memories or associations which indicate that these states contain visual memories which have lost their outlines and distinctness. The memory has become a homogeneous part of the perceptual field. Many of the memories are of the maternal breast. Others are memories of later screens which have served to prevent the child from carrying out some action or satisfying some curiosity. They function as barriers between what is conscious and what is unconscious, and therefore serve the forces leading to repression. They continue to function internally as they once functioned externally, to exclude painful feelings and thoughts from consciousness.

422

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1

Henry P. Laughlin. 'King David's Anger.'

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

The prophet Nathan recounted to David a parable against a rich man which kindled the king's anger. He then showed David that the rich man was himself. The author brings several examples of patients whose rapid dislike of someone was similarly formed on the perception of a resemblance with a disapproved segment of their own personality. He suggests the hypothesis that 'our attention is selectively directed to those qualities in others over which we have greatest concern or conflict in ourselves'.

422

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 2: Jule Eisenbud.  'Behavioural Correspondences to Normally Unpredictable Future Events.'  Part 1.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 2

Jule Eisenbud. 'Behavioural Correspondences to Normally Unpredictable Future Events.' Part 1.

This is a paper about precognition. Though the author prefers to avoid the word there is no need to do so in a short abstract. He sets out to describe what on the face of it would be a somewhat dubious precognitive experience; only analysis could reveal the numerous coincidences which give it evidential value.

The manifest content of a patient's dream contained what looked like a reference to an event which occurred at least an hour after he had woken up and which was reported in a newspaper on the following day. Eisenbud treats this event as an ordinary day residue—i.e. as though it had occurred on the day before the dream instead of the day after. An intricate relationship to the manifest content of the dream is then revealed. Not only does this enable him to make an efficient dream construction, it also shows reference to a current emotional problem of the analyst—a phenomenon usually regarded as a sine qua non of psi-conditioned dreams.

The paper is to be continued.

423

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 2: Ralph R. Greenson.  'About the Sound "Mm …"'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 2

Ralph R. Greenson. 'About the Sound "Mm …"'

A patient in euphoric mood felt as if he were humming the sound 'Mm …'. Analysis showed that this stood for the fantasy of being at the mother's breast. 'Mm …' is the only sound which it is possible to make while still keeping something safely in the mouth. The word Mama—like many other words for mother and breast—is built round it. The sound also duplicates the pleasant labial sensations associated with the act of nursing.

423

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 2: S. S. Feldman.  Motives of "Minor Offence" in Two Dreams.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 2

S. S. Feldman. Motives of "Minor Offence" in Two Dreams.'

'The dreams of two adult male patients contained the expression, "minor offence". (1) The offence was "minor" to the child, but to his parents it was a major offence. (2) The offence took place when the dreamer was a minor. (3) The phrase refers to sexual relations with minors. (4) The "minor" is a disguise for the mother. (5) The offence was felt in childhood to be a major one, but there was a wish to minimize the offence and deny the guilt.' (Author's summary.)

Feldman thinks it probable that this phrase indicates the same latent thoughts in all dreams.

423

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 2

Morris Factor. 'A Woman's Psychological Reaction to Attempted Rape.'

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

An attempted rape accelerated the eroticization of a woman's behaviour in the transference. The author thinks that (1) the fact of a real attack diminished her guilt over her unconscious complicity; (2) her superego could be appeased by being shown her suffering. But these defences could not prevent a guilt-laden dream when she was confronted with her assailant in court.

423

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 3: Mortimer Ostow.  'A Psychoanalytic Contribution to the Study of Brain Function.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 3

Mortimer Ostow. 'A Psychoanalytic Contribution to the Study of Brain Function.'

1. The Frontal Lobes

Experimental stimulation and ablation of the premotor frontal regions has indicated that we can assign to them no specific function. Nevertheless human beings and animals deprived of them display a uniform impairment in strength and consistency of motivation. The author attempts to explain strength of motivation as a function of the ability to construct derivatives of instinctual drives and unconscious fantasies and to transfer psychic energy from these to conscious processes. Impairment of consistency of motivation he explains as a consequence of impairment of function of the device that regulates the orderly procession of fantasies so as to maintain continuity of motivation without rigidity. It therefore seems to him reasonable to assign to the premotor frontal region the functions of devising and energizing derivatives of instinctual drives and unconscious fantasies and of regulating the rate and sequence in which unconscious fantasies determine day to day behaviour. He thinks that if one accepts the formulation that neurosis or psychosis is caused by incomplete or unsuccessful repression, then one can understand that damage to the frontal lobe can relieve an individual of neurotic symptoms by depriving him of the power to form them and—what is the same thing—the power to create, express, and enjoy himself as a human being.

(Based on Author's Summary.)

423

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 3

Jule Eisenbud. 'Behavioural Correspondences to Normally Unpredictable Future Events.' Part 2.

The author continues his paper with another example of a dream in which the multiple interrelations of its latent thoughts could be construed as suggesting 'precognitive' and telepathic knowledge. The dream seemed to occur under the stress of intense rivalry with another patient—telepathically intuited—and to refer to it.

Eisenbud does not consider that 'precognition' necessarily implies the existence of hitherto unsuspected dimensions of time. It might imply instead unsuspected dimensions of mind; and these, he indicates, may provide the first steps towards bridging many of the discontinuities in natural scientific theory, such as the conflict between indeterminacy in some fields and determinacy in others.

424

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 3: Jerome Richfield.  'An Analysis of the Concept of Insight.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 3

Jerome Richfield. 'An Analysis of the Concept of Insight.'

No logically successful clarification of the concept of insight exists in psycho-analytic theory which will (1) differentiate between the various uses of the term, and (2) relate these uses theoretically to therapeutic aims. Current definitions of the various types of insight are linguistically confused. The author attempts a clarification by applying Bertrand Russell's concepts of knowledge by description and knowledge by acquaintance in order to be able to define insights according to the manner in which they were achieved. Both methods are necessary for successful treatment.

424

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4: Mark Kanzer.  'Observations on Blank Dreams with Orgasms.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4

Mark Kanzer. 'Observations on Blank Dreams with Orgasms.'

The forgotten dream has been interpreted by Lewin in oral terms as a concrete object which has been lost. Kanzer suggests that the oral elements may sometimes be a defence against phallic and anal sexuality, particularly when the forgetting is associated with blank dreams with orgasm.

He reports two sets of three dreams. In each set the first dream dealt with the upper regions of the body, or world, from which there was a fall, the third with the lower regions. In the middle, and associated with orgasm, was in one case a blank dream and in the other a dream which 'slipped away'. The middle dream clearly referred to the middle, or genital, region of the body. The dream which 'slipped away' was associated both with a severed phallus and with faeces which slipped away. The blank dreams in Kanzer's patient had their origin in adolescent dreams with seminal emission in which both vision and dreaming were arrested at the moment of penetrating the vagina. He suggests that the blankness represented the nothingness of the vagina, and quotes Freud's interpretation of a 'gap' in a dream in the same sense. The interest in the oral zone associated with the complex breast and dream screen may therefore sometimes be due to an upward displacement from the maternal vulva, serving the interests of regression. The breasts may moreover play a rôle in relation to the downward sweep of the eyes towards the blankness of the vulva similar to that which the hair or garment plays with the fetishist. The orgasm of the blank dream is evidence of the repressed voyeuristic impulse and testifies, moreover, to the oedipal basis of these psychic mechanisms; furthermore, by ridding the body of tension, the regression supplements the visual block as a defence against the perpetuation of dangerous instinctual excitement.

The picture of the feeding breast represents a further defence by turning from active to passive, from mother-identification to child-identification, as well as from phallic to oral. This is a pantomime of sexual intercourse, relieving castration anxiety.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4: Phillip H. Starr.  'Psychoses in Children:  Their Origin and Structure.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4

Phillip H. Starr. 'Psychoses in Children: Their Origin and Structure.'

Just as the Oedipus complex is nuclear for the neuroses so the 'symbiotic union' of mother and infant is nuclear for the psychoses. The symbiotic union—a normal phase—is achieved approximately between the ages of six months and a year. It is characterized by healthy give and take between the partners resulting in the satisfactory psycho-physiological regulation of the child. It is preceded by a phase in which the child reaches out for the mother and is resolved at about eighteen months as the ego, after weaning, disengages itself and struggles towards autonomy. The healthy achievement of the symbiotic union depends on the congenital activity type of the infant and on the emotional availability of the mother. Low object seeking potential in the infant favours autism, a low degree of activity parasitism. The resulting disturbances in the development of ego, of object relationship and of affectivity culminate in shaping the symbiotic union and are the true determinants of psychoses and the other more severe psychiatric disturbances in the same way as the infantile sexuality, which shapes the Oedipus complex, is the true determinant of the neuroses.

The paper ends by describing how prognosis and therapeutic technique must be based on an estimate of all these factors. Technique of treating mothers and indications for institutionalization are also discussed.

426

[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Charles Fisher. 'Dreams and Perception.'

This paper reports a repetition of Poetzl's work on dreams with the aim of integrating the results into the psycho-analytic theory of dreams. Eighteen patients and six doctors acted as subjects. Each subject was told to fix his eyes on a screen on to which a picture was projected for 1/200 second. The subject was told to describe what he had seen and then to make a drawing of it. He was also instructed to write down any dreams he might have that night. No order to dream was given.

426

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3: Martin Wangh.  'Day Residue in Dream and Myth.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Martin Wangh. 'Day Residue in Dream and Myth.'

The presence of dwarfs in the manifest content of a dream of a patient whose father-in-law had just died provides the clinical material of this paper. The dream successfully mastered the anxiety, principally arising from oral problems, which the father-in-law's death had aroused. The comforting thought the dream provided was 'You can rest, you don't need to worry, the little people will protect you and work for you.' Squirrels had supplied the day residue for the dream. The author suggests that the myth of the little people so widespread in northern and middle European countries is due to a denial and transformation of the anxiety caused by different varieties of rodents who were a constant danger to the food supply. The myth turns the hated and feared creatures into helpful and friendly little beings. This material alone could not form the myth. While the fear of rodents both in the dream and in the myths formed the preconscious stimulus (the day residue) the unconscious element arises from the ambivalent relationship towards ancestors and children. This conflict was given prominence by the death of the patient's father-in-law.

427

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3: Victor Calef.  'Colour in Dreams.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Victor Calef. 'Colour in Dreams.'

When a patient reports colour in a dream the immediate response is one of surprise and resistance. The latent content consists frequently of elements from the scoptophilic-exhibitionistic life of the patient. The author suggests that the black and white imagery of most dreams is an indication that the resistance has succeeded in camouflaging the latent dream thoughts and wishes. Colour is removed in the service of repression. The threat of the return of the repressed stimulates colour in dreams, particularly if the repressed is connected with exhibitionistic-scoptophilic impulses. Colour in dreams represents a further regression in the direction of pictorial representation calling forth the reproduction of sensory images or symbolic distortions of these images.

427

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3: Louis Linn.  'Colour in Dreams.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Louis Linn. 'Colour in Dreams.'

Visual experiences contribute to the development of the superego in as important a way as auditory impressions. The author quotes Freud's 'non vixit' dream and a dream of one of his own patients to support this theory. While colour in dreams is often associated with exhibitionism and scoptophilia, this is not invariable. Different instinctual strivings may find expression through colour. The colours which appear in dreams can often be traced to specific experiences in the environment rather than to pre-existing colour elements present constitutionally in the physiological visual apparatus.

427

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3: Alexander Grinstein.  'The Convertible as a Symbol in Dreams.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Alexander Grinstein. 'The Convertible as a Symbol in Dreams.'

This paper discusses the use of a particular model of motor car as a dream element. The speed of a car and the type of journey is often

427

used to portray the progress of an analysis. More specifically a car may represent the entire body or parts of it. It may represent the genitals or their functioning. The author emphasizes that the car is a bisexual symbol. Five dreams are presented from the analyses of four male patients where the type of car dreamt of was a 'convertible'. This type of car was chosen because it could represent the idea of the patient being converted or transformed into a woman.

428

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3: Louis S. Lipschutz.  'The Written Dream.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Louis S. Lipschutz. 'The Written Dream.'

A patient who wrote down his dreams but did not look at his notes in the analytical session was asked to continue with his associations and then finally to read his written account. Four of these dreams, verbal and written, are compared in this paper. The written dream is a 'much overdetermined typical piece of acting out'. It can have several meanings at different levels. These include a gift of faeces, the wish to have an anal baby, a defence against castration fear. The written dream is always a transference dream designed to please, placate, or confuse the analyst. Greater distortion is possible in the written dream. It should be considered as an expression of resistance and treated as such.

428

[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Samuel A. Guttman. 'A Note on Morning Depression.'

This note discusses two points concerning morning depressions. First, a theoretical consideration of depressed affect after waking, and second, affect as a consequence of thoughts and affects while sleeping. In morning depression the affect associated either recalled or unrecalled is carried into the waking state.

428

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3: George Devereux.  'Primitive Genital Mutilations in a Neurotic's Dream.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

George Devereux. 'Primitive Genital Mutilations in a Neurotic's Dream.'

That which is repressed in one culture may be overt in another culture and vice versa. This paper reports the dream of a female patient in which occurred all but one of the many phallic mutilations known to anthropologists. The patient's dream and associations is followed by an account of an imaginary ailment encountered in parts of South-East Asia and known as koro. The patient fears that his penis will retract into the abdomen. A Dutch psychiatrist has suggested that the practice of inserting a small rod (ampallang) into a hole pierced through the glans penis is to prevent the retreat of the penis into the abdomen. The author reports that amongst the Mohave Indians jokes are made about male transvestites who would like to push the penis into the abdomen and so produce a vagina. The feminization phantasy which in Indonesia and South China causes anxiety is turned into a joke by the Mohave Indians. The patient's dream, while referring to other genital mutilations, omitted any reference to the ampallang. The author suggests that the patient unconsciously believed that she was already in a state of koro. Her penis had already 'withdrawn' into the vagina. This could never have happened if her phantasied penis had been provided with an ampallang in the first place. The patient knew of circumcision, sub-incision, and castration, but knew nothing of other mutilations such as encrustation which did appear in her dream or of the ampallang which did not appear in the dream. In this case phantasy material which in certain areas is culturally standardized and implemented by means of formal and traditional acts and emotions appeared in a patient's dream as a purely autistic production.

428

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Don D. Jackson. 'An Episode of Sleepwalking.'

A young man had an episode of sleepwalking a few days before the onset of an acute schizophrenic episode. These events occurred coincidentally with his return home, when he experienced tension in his relations with father and brother and seductive smothering from his mother. The somnambulistic episode and the psychosis had certain common features with regard to the forbidden impulses and the defences against them.

428

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Martin A. Berezin. 'Enuresis and Bisexual Identification.'

The enuretic act is conceived of as both active and passive, masculine and feminine. Enuresis may be a prototype of sexual experience so that later in life it acted in this case as a determinant in the phantasies connected with masturbation and sexual intercourse.

[[RunningHead]]

Samiksa, 8, 1954, No. 4

George Devereux. 'Belief, Superstition and Symptom.'

This paper is offered partly as an addendum to that of Dr. Judd Marmor, 'Some Observations on Superstition in Contemporary Life', read before the St. Louis, Mo., Annual Meeting of the American Psychiatric Association, May 1954.

Belief, superstition and symptoms are different channels for discharge of anxiety. 'Beliefs, and to a lesser extent superstitions are culturally and socially integrative. By contrast, symptoms are socially divisive.'

This difference and others are examined. It is emphasized that 'the anal component, nowadays so unfashionable in psycho-analysis, is quite conspicuous' in these problems.

responseSet.title: Canadian Journal of Psychology 7, 1953: Noel Mailloux, O.P.  'Psychic Determinism, Freedom, and Personality Development.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Canadian Journal of Psychology 7, 1953

Noel Mailloux, O.P. 'Psychic Determinism, Freedom, and Personality Development.'

A. Lussier

A. Lussier

A. Lussier  

The author studies the problem at the level of 'the conflict-free sphere of the ego'. Freud stated that the striving for ego freedom is inherent in psycho-analysis, but the problem is to see how free will develops from the archaic stages of indeterminacy and infantile automatism. An important first distinction is that indeterminism and not determinism is the alternative to free will.

In the first stage of development, the overcoming of primitive indeterminacy has to be achieved through 'automatization of psychic processes' which is no more than a rudimentary adaptability. Gradually, this narrow functioning has to give way to the 'autonomous determinism of freedom'. This process implies the continuous temptation to regress to earlier stages of automatism, in order to avoid anxiety, the 'emotion of the possible'. The unconscious, here, is not eliminated but integrated and surpassed.

Psychic determinism is seen as a preliminary condition for freedom. Free activity is a self-determined activity, with the highest capacity for flexibility in adaptation, for rational motivation and creativeness. Finally, neurotic attitudes are analyzed as inability and fear to reach freedom.

435

responseSet.title: Indian Journal of Psychology 28, 1953: L. C. Bhandari.  'New Horizons in Clinical Psychology.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Indian Journal of Psychology 28, 1953

L. C. Bhandari. 'New Horizons in Clinical Psychology.'

The author discusses the meaning and present-day status of this branch of psychology and the issues which face clinical psychologists in a field where their interests clash with those of psychiatrists. He considers that in order to promote further advancement in the methods of diagnosis and treatment in clinical psychology, emphasis should be laid upon the importance of close collaboration between psychiatrists and clinical psychologists in a task which requires their mutual cooperation and goodwill.

Psychosomatic medicine is discussed as representing the latest scientific discipline which has developed in the medical world since World War II. It provides the basic approach in applying facts of psycho-pathology to a number of functional and organic illnesses.

In the therapy of emotional disorders 'brief psychotherapy' is discussed as a methodological advance which may provide the benefits of psychotherapy for the masses. To serve the wider interests of individual and community mental health, the importance of training in techniques of 'brief psychotherapy' has been stressed.

Author's Abstract.

435

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Leopold Bellak, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>NEW YORK</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: An Ego-Psychological Theory of Hypnosis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Ego-Psychological Theory of Hypnosis

Leopold Bellak M.D.

Leopold Bellak

Leopold Bellak, M.D.
NEW YORK

Leopold Bellak  

Of the many functions of the ego (4), (2), (1) that relatively most recently discussed and least generally understood is the function of 'self-exclusion' (5), (6), (7). Under certain circumstances, the ego is able to exclude or to decrease some or nearly all of its functions. The cognitive function needs to be excluded in the process of falling asleep. In hypnagogic phenomena, especially in Silberer's functional phenomenon (10), we deal with a self-observation of the process of falling asleep and, as it were, a self-observation of the decrease and exclusion of perception. Varendonck (11) has cited a number of interesting examples which occurred to him when he was struggling to stay awake. A patient of mine, following a small operation, was nightly given codeine as a sedative and experienced a vision of a window being closed as the last thing before falling asleep. I have myself experienced the image of a closet door closing and darkness ensuing. Such phenomena are easily recalled either if one trains oneself, as Varendonck did, to awaken again, or when circumstances bring about a chance noise that penetrates the perceptual field and reverses the process.

Not infrequently, people report a final awareness of muscular relaxation as the feeling of falling in going to sleep, only to awaken with a start if they happen to be anxious and the muscular relaxation is incomplete, and if passivity is a threat. Similarly, the process of awakening from a deep slumber frequently involves a temporary disorientation with only gradual perception of the entire field.

I suggest that the hypnotic state—in the past frequently described as a 'state of narrowed consciousness'—can be better understood as a state of partial self-exclusion of the ego similar to the process of falling asleep, but not identical with it. In enumerating the differences, I hope also to contribute to a clarification of the longstanding discussion of comparisons between sleep and hypnosis.

Consciousness, and therefore ego activity, is not an 'all-or-none' proposition, but is a state ranging on a continuum from a hypothetical maximum to a hypothetical minimum. This is known both popularly and clinically, and finds its conceptual expression in psycho-analysis in the formulation of 'pre-consciousness' (mental processes that are not conscious but can with minimal intent be made conscious) and in such clinical psychiatric terms as 'cloudy states' (Dämmerzustand), depersonalization, etc.

responseSet.title: Notes on Menstruation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Notes on Menstruation11

Emilio Rodrigue

Emilio Rodrigue

Emilio M. Rodrigue
LONDON

Emilio Rodrigue  

SUMMARY

I have tried to show the continuity in a series of phantasies over three menstrual cycles. The main feature in this sequence was that the patient's anxiety, of a persecutory nature at first, diminished and became mainly depressive in quality. With the lessening of anxiety, the patient's symptoms of pain in menstruation disappeared, and she was able to allow herself to fall in love. During the first menstrual period

332

the patient felt herself to be permeated by a to and fro movement of shapeless objects which appeared as vague outlines, and which she described as 'someone', 'everyone', 'anything', etc. She felt herself split into a community of 'someones', whilst I was perceived as the double of the shapeless internal objects. Persecution was experienced during the second menstrual period, by a host of controlling and controlled objects. These objects—the duellists—had human characteristics. At the same time a less drastic control and an increasing concern for the good object could be observed in that a much earlier phantasy about a weed changed to a greater interest in good flowers. The third menstrual period ushered in an object-relationship characterized by a loving attitude and a sharing of experiences with an object while remaining at the same time comparatively free and independent of it.

Understanding of the changes in this patient is facilitated if we consider the process of assimilation described by Paula Heimann in her paper on sublimation.5 She suggests that the true capacity to sublimate entails a relationship with internal objects which are not overwhelmingly persecutory or idealized; for in either of these cases the work of sublimation carried out by the ego is primarily done out of fear—fear of the directly persecuting or intolerably exacting objects. Creating under these conditions is not a spontaneous and personal endeavour, but takes the form of a placatory or propitiatory act towards an object felt to be an alien to the self (an unassimilated object). In the present material it was seen that the patient had a relationship with idealized or persecutory objects. At one stage particularly they had a special characteristic: that of fascination. The duellists, the Weed, and the Negroes were examples of fascinating internal objects that made her feel at their mercy. During the last menstrual period, however, the patient was in sympathy with her object while not feeling spellbound or threatened by its power. This capacity for sympathy with an object—a feeling of oneness with it, in which she still retained her own free self—can be seen as the result of an internal relation with an assimilated object. This assimilation was possible only when the patient realized to what extent she had put her own impulses into her objects.

If the fact that the patient could have a good relationship with a man because she was menstruating is borne in mind, it follows that this event was a helpful experience. It was so, I think, because with the assimilation of the good object a certain balance in the mechanisms of introjection and projection was reached, in the sense that she achieved the unconscious belief that the bad object had been ejected whilst the good object remained in a safe inner world. By the third menstrual period described the patient felt that her inside, as a result of the genital bleeding, was empty, clean, and safe, ready to carry her future children. On this occasion she took a predominantly feminine rôle. Concurrently with this gain in femininity, with the diminution particularly of her persecutory anxiety and the lessening of control over her internal objects, her libido, which till then had remained fixated to the lost object, could now be transferred to a new object. Her new choice was significant because in the new man she now loved she had been able to retain some of the admired qualities of her lost sweetheart, viz. his interest in motor-cycling. This improvement although not fully maintained was significant. It must be assessed in relation to the fact that the patient later showed a good adjustment in her marriage and had a greater capacity for happiness.

I think it worth while pointing out that menstruation can be experienced as good and helpful, because in the analytical literature the positive aspect of menstruation has been considerably neglected. Most writers dealing with this topic have over-emphasized the traumatic aspect of menstruation, considering it almost exclusively as a revival of the female castration complex, as a cyclic abortion, as something dirty equated with faeces, etc. These are widespread anxiety situations to be found in the analysis of every woman, but I believe that a better perspective is gained if the constructive aspects of menstruation are contemplated as well. It is important to consider, as Melanie Klein has pointed out, that 'menstruation can also be experienced as positive in so far as it indicates fertility', 6 or as Helene Deutsch has

333

stated, 'menstruation becomes a decisive experience in this process of feminization'.

Finally, I should like to deal briefly with the feeling of 'uncanniness' that so often accompanies menstruation. Practically all papers on this subject refer to the taboos, superstitions, and prohibitions, old and new, that have gathered round the menstruating woman. The work of C. D. Daly in particular has given us a wealth of valuable information, drawn from literary and anthropological data.7 Several explanations have been put forward to account for the widespread horror and the creation of taboos that this biological phenomenon evokes. C. D. Daly considers menstruation as 'the most virulent of all the taboos …' and ultimately traces its 'virulence' to the repressed early incestuous drives of the boy to his mother.8 Another basis for this reaction is to be found in the castration anxiety which the genital bleeding stirs in the boy (and in the group).

We may also consider the way in which menstruation can be felt subjectively as uncanny by the menstruating woman herself. Leaving aside the importance of the time factor in this cyclic phenomenon, and the rôle it might have in producing the uncanny response to menstruation (for instance, in creating the suspense or expectation that something is about to happen), I have observed in this and in other female patients that the sinister feeling was related to an underlying state of confusion. It may be, therefore, that consideration of the earlier infantile persecutory and depressive phantasies may help to clarify the problem of the attitude of women towards menstruation. The problem is posed by Helene Deutsch when she states that it is not yet clear 'why so many women suffer menstrual depression before the menstrual period', adding that they forget from month to month that their periodic depression is caused by the approach of the menses and breathe relief when the onset of menstruation supplies a rational explanation of this depression (op. cit., p. 174). This forgetting the periodic nature of the depression might be due to the anxiety stirred up by the relatively fixed menstrual timing, provoking in the woman the feeling that the inside of the body is controlled by alien, powerful forces, following an immutable temporal law.

The findings of Melanie Klein9 regarding the sexual development of the girl suggest that a direct Oedipus complex alternates with an inverted Oedipus complex from early infancy onwards. Vaginal sensations associated with phantasies of a typically feminine receptive nature frequently accompany the young girl's heterosexual impulses. It follows from her views that when the first menstruation occurs in puberty, this event, important though it is, does not bring in its train a new, specifically genital conflict. Its deeper meaning has to be understood in the light of the phantasies and anxiety situations pertaining to the early stages of the Oedipus complex in the girl. Thus in the first two menstrual periods here described the idea of the uncanny appeared. The analysis of the anxiety-situations revealed that the patient felt that she was either invaded by or lost in an object, and she was unable in either case to draw a line between herself and the bad (fascinating, persecutory) object. This inner confusion, entailing a notion of unreality, must play a rôle in the 'secret and mysterious fears' that menstruation so often arouses.

responseSet.title: Isaac and Oedipus. A Study in Biblical Psychology of the Sacrifice of Isaac, the Akedah: By Erich Wellisch.  (London:  Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1954.  Pp. 131.  15 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Isaac and Oedipus. A Study in Biblical Psychology of the Sacrifice of Isaac, the Akedah

By Erich Wellisch. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1954. Pp. 131. 15 s.)

Eva Rosenfeld

Eva Rosenfeld

Eva Rosenfeld  

The author, who is well equipped with historical and biblical material, stipulates infanticide as the primal crime of humanity, of greater importance and of more historical significance than either parricide or fratricide. (This view has also been recently expressed by psycho-analytic writers, who have, however, not confined their attention to the crime of the father against the son.) He then proceeds to discuss the Oedipus myth in the light of his re-evaluation of the primal crime of humanity. He concludes that ideas about oedipal guilt feelings and unconscious conflicts should be replaced by the concept of 'moral masochism'. (Glover, 1949.)

Finally, the author turns to the biblical story of the 'Akedah', the binding of Isaac for sacrifice. He suggests that a change of heart in Abraham followed the binding of his son, which led to 'the creation of new heavens and a new earth'. Thus he looks on Isaac as the precursor of Christ, and upon Abraham's internal change from aggression to love as the birth of a new religion. A new phase in the evolution of civilization is, therefore, reached at the moment when the child is spared and the animal sacrificed instead.

Consequently, the author feels that the story of Isaac and Abraham is a more successful resolution of the problem posed in the full story of Oedipus, and is, therefore, of greater use as an analogy in clinical work.

Most psycho-analysts and anthropologists would agree with the stress the author lays on the importance of this step in the process of development of both civilizations and individuals. Analysts, however, would probably express it differently by saying that a new phase of human civilization is reached when the father turns from identification with the aggressor (God's voice commanding the slaughter of Isaac) to identification with the victim (the divine voice commanding him to spare the child).

A very fine selection of illustrations accompanies the book. We learn with much regret that the author died shortly before this work appeared.

responseSet.title: Dr. Douglas Noel Hardcastle 1892–1954
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dr. Douglas Noel Hardcastle 1892–1954

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer  

Dr. Noel Hardcastle, who was a member of the British Psycho-Analytical Society and Physician to the London Clinic of Psycho-Analysis, died after a short illness on 17 May, 1954, at Bishop's Stortford, where he had established himself in psycho-analytic and psychiatric practice.

Dr. Hardcastle's record of neurologic and psychiatric appointments suggests that his psychopathological experience must already have been of high standard in the late twenties. It was then that he decided to study the Child Guidance Services in the United States; spending in all three years there. Back in England, with a first-hand account of this growing branch of psychiatric practice and research, he founded and directed the Fulham Child Guidance Clinic, and, as a keen thinker and observer, found there a fertile field for his gift of searching into the conflicts of childhood and adolescence. But he also felt that he had to look for what was not on the surface and could not be discerned without employing the method of psycho-analysis. Uncompromising as he was, he underwent a thorough personal analysis and training at the London Institute of Psycho-Analysis. In spite of his age he insisted on seeing it through, and qualified just before World War II. As he had done in the First World War, he joined the Royal Army Medical Corps, serving as a psychiatric specialist. By his rather sudden death a long search and struggle for accomplishment has come to an end.

His devotion to the science of psycho-analysis was honest and deep; receptive and imaginative, he constantly found stimulation in our literature, in his patients, and in the meetings of the British Psycho-Analytical Society. He married Miss Kitty Duguid, who survives him with three children.

74

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Heinz Hartmann, M.D. 'Contributions to the Metapsychology of Schizophrenia', pp. 177–198.

This paper, read at the 18th International Psycho-Analytical Congress in London, applies the author's earlier formulations concerning the genetic and economic factors of ego organization to some aspects of the schizophrenic process. Some aspects of schizophrenia may be accounted for in terms of the impairment not so much of the ego as of specific ego functions. Emphasis is laid on the fact that this effort to integrate certain well-known data about schizophrenic defence, object relations, language, and reality testing is made on the understanding that one aspect can be fully meaningful only in interrelationship with others already studied. Metapsychological hypotheses are used to bridge the gap between the instinctual and ego aspects and to establish connections between them. Insight is gained into the economic aspects of aggression in the normal and schizophrenic individual and into deficiencies in the primary autonomous factors in the ego which may represent one etiological element in the predisposition to schizophrenia.

78

[[RunningHead]]

Civilisation, War and Death

By Sigmund Freud. Edited by John Rickman. Psycho-Analytical Epitomes No. 4. New and enlarged edition. (London: Hogarth Press and the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 1953. 8 s. 6 d. $1.00.)

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer  

John Rickman's second choice from the works of Freud comprises edited essays written twenty to forty years ago, among them Freud's letter to Albert Einstein on war and pacifism (1932). There is hardly anything in this collection which should be discarded as untimely or purely historical; Freud's reflections are as threatening and gloomy as if they had been conceived today. Still, in the interval between the two editions of this selection further

138

translations have made it possible to display another facet of Freud's insight into the nature of man and his societies. In the first edition the reader was struck by Freud's argument about the inevitability of internal aggression and the consequent probability of future wars. In this new and enlarged version John Rickman has added two further essays, of which one at least shows a somewhat different Freud; one who in spite of relentless and pitiless insistence on the dangers stemming from the aggressive drives reminds the reader of Eros and of his belief in it. How otherwise could he conclude his thoughts on Goethe, laid down in the essay 'On Transience' (1915), with the words: 'We shall build up again all that war has destroyed, and perhaps on firmer ground and more lastingly than before'?

139

[[RunningHead]]

Cybernetics: Circular Causal and Feedback Mechanisms in Biological and Social Systems

Transactions of the Eighth Conference. Edited by Heinz von Foerster. (New York: Josiah Macy, Jr. Foundation, 1952. Pp. xx + 240. $4.00.)

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom  

The present volume is the third of the series on Cybernetics to be published. It gives the impression of being even better than its predecessors. All volumes containing verbatim discussion must have weaknesses; the wonder is that the plan succeeds as well as it does. If we are willing to accept the defects inherent in such a scheme (and the present volume is unintelligible in several places), we shall be rewarded with a grand-stand view of acute minds grappling with difficult problems in borderland fields. The theme of the discussion is different kinds of Communication—between groups, between animals, and so on. Dr. Kubie's contribution on communication between the sane and the insane is perhaps the most striking part of the book.

139

responseSet.title: Heredity in Health and Mental Disorder: By F. J. Kallmann.  (New York:  W. W. Norton and Co., 1953.  Pp. 315.  $6.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Heredity in Health and Mental Disorder

By F. J. Kallmann. (New York: W. W. Norton and Co., 1953. Pp. 315. $6.00.)

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman  

In this book F. J. Kallmann presents the results of his extensive investigations into the role of hereditary factors in the causation of mental disease. All the important research carried out over the past seventeen years is here included. The work is divided into three sections. The first two deal respectively with 'Heredity in Relation to Mental Health' and 'Heredity in Relation to Mental Disorder'. In nearly all his investigations Kallmann has employed the method of comparative twin studies. In the first part of the book he not only describes the principles and methods of genetics but also produces an abundance of twin material to support his belief that 'The evidence of the over-all data presented in this first section in support of genetically determined variations in man's adjustive patterns and survival values is sufficient to indicate that human individuality and health are no chance phenomena reflecting only the degree of cultural pressures or the extent of instinctual drives. The capacities for health and adequate adjustment are fundamental biological properties with the common denominator of hereditary potentiality'.

While the second part of the book contains the results of investigations into the part played by heredity in convulsive disorders, neurological diseases, and mental defect, the interest of the psycho-analyst will be drawn to the reports on the functional psychoses. Kallmann has little to say about the psychoneuroses, regretting the difficulties of obtaining sufficient twin material for investigations equal in scope to that found for the psychoses.

Kallmann has observed from his 794 schizophrenic twin pairs that monozygotic twins are concordant for schizophrenia to the extent of 86.2 per cent., but dizygotic twins only 14.6 per cent. This, with other information, has convinced Kallmann that genetic factors are decisive in the predisposition to a schizophrenic illness. He does not believe that a mild attack of or freedom from the psychosis is due to a kind fate which protects the individual from severe environmental stresses. In Kallmann's opinion freedom from the illness results from a constitutional defence mechanism which is probably non-specific genetically.

The Mind and the Eye: A Study of the Biologist's Standpoint, with Bibliography and Index

By Agnes Arber. (Cambridge University Press, 1954. Pp. 146. 16 s.)

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

Psycho-analysts will enjoy this short book by a botanist. The author brings together her views on scientific method and aim, language in its relation to non-verbal thought, and the relation between the scientist's unconscious ideas and his scientific findings. Her bibliography is long and many new sources of earlier work may be suggested, especially to those whose interest is ego psychology.

357

responseSet.title: The Integration of Behavior. Vol. I. Basic Postulates; Vol. II. the Integrative Process in Dreams: By Thomas M. French, M.D.  (University of Chicago Press, 1952 and 1954.  Pp. xi + 272; xi + 367.  37 s. 6 d. and 49 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Integration of Behavior. Vol. I. Basic Postulates; Vol. II. the Integrative Process in Dreams

By Thomas M. French, M.D. (University of Chicago Press, 1952 and 1954. Pp. xi + 272; xi + 367. 37 s. 6 d. and 49 s.)

M. Brierley

M. Brierley

M. Brierley  

These books can be read independently, but they are the first two in a projected series of five volumes, whose aim is to study the 'process of integration of behaviour by a detailed analysis of the thoughts and acts of one person during two years of psycho-analytic treatment'. The patient was analysed by a colleague. Anticipating the query 'Can anything be proved by one case?' the author writes, 'Our aim, really, is not to prove anything. We intend, rather, to illustrate a method by which anyone may check our conclusions on as many cases as he cares to study in this way' (p. 40).

In his Acknowledgements the author says that he owes most to Freud but has also been much influenced by long collaboration with Franz Alexander and by the work of Kurt Lewin, Pavlov, Kohler, and Tolman. Some of his views have already been published in papers, but here we have the systematic presentation of what is essentially a revised metapsychology, reformulated in terms largely derived from, and consonant with, modern experimental physiology, and 'field' and Gestalt concepts. One of the aims of the re-phrasing is to render some of the findings of psycho-analysis more amenable to experimental investigation and validation, though in these books theory is formulated in close and intimate relation with clinical material and illustrated by simple tables and diagrams.

Basic Postulates. It is not feasible in a review to give a detailed account of the author's thesis and the arguments for it, but an attempt will be made to indicate the kind of approach and a few of the correspondences and differences between some of the suggested hypotheses and our more customary formulations. The method used, 'analysis by comparison', mainly of dream with dream, affords 'a strictly empirical method for reconstructing the pattern of a patient's personality. … As our working hypothesis, we assume that rational behaviour, neuroses and dreams can be analysed into component factors that are in part common to all three' (p. 5). There is nothing to question in this hypothesis since it is a re-statement of something that psycho-analysts take for granted. Leaving aside the value of 'analysis by comparison' as a therapeutic, as distinct from a research, technique, the method enables the author to bring out very clearly relationships and differences between normal and abnormal behaviour, the sequence of the patient's dreams and their recurring patterns, and the effects of intercurrent events between sessions on the transference situation.

responseSet.title: A Synthesis of Human Behavior: An Integration of Thought Processes and Ego Growth: By Joseph C. Solomon.  (New York:  Grune & Stratton, 1954.  Pp. xii + 265.  $5.50 or 38 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Synthesis of Human Behavior: An Integration of Thought Processes and Ego Growth

By Joseph C. Solomon. (New York: Grune & Stratton, 1954. Pp. xii + 265. $5.50 or 38 s.)

M. Brierley

M. Brierley

M. Brierley  

This book, by Dr. Solomon, Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry in the University of California, attempts a synthesis of human thinking from birth to old age. For the author 'the ego is a development of the "meaning of things", or in short, conceptual thinking' (p. 4). Perception being defined as sensation plus memory, perceptual thinking corresponds to non-verbal thinking and ego development depends upon the transition, via the acquisition of language, from non-verbal to conceptual thinking.

Many readers will question the adequacy of such an exclusively cognitive key to the many-sided problems of human behaviour, but, for the author, it is a clue that has led him to codify his views in a Table of Ego Organization (pp. 8–9). This table lists nine Maturation Phases from Infancy to Senescence; the typical characteristics of each phase are given in five columns: Psychosexual Stage, Principal External Objects, Mature Ego, Superego Reactions, Neurotic Ego Traits. The Psychosexual Stages of Infancy and Toddlerhood, Early- and Late-Perceptual and Early- and Late-Conceptual, here replace the old libidinal oral and anal stage classification. Unfortunately an undetected printer's error in the table gives the first Toddler phase, which should obviously be 'early-conceptual' as a second 'early-perceptual'. Since the table is intended for constant reference, it is rather a pity this mistake escaped notice in the proofs.

The most useful part of the book is probably not the table or the theory but the wealth of descriptive material and the sensible discussions of human problems at different age periods which are 'mainly an outgrowth of my own twenty-seven years of training and experience, not only in psycho-analysis but also in pediatrics, child guidance, and hospital psychiatry'. This should be useful to students in any profession directly concerned with human welfare.

The author has been considerably influenced by Hartmann, but is one of the few American psycho-analysts who seems to have made a serious study of the work of Melanie Klein, though he differs from her on many points. Thus, while he endorses the idea of preverbal superego development he does not think 'an elaborate system of phantasies that exist almost from birth' (p. x.) necessary to account for infantile anxiety, which he regards as perceptual in origin. For him, such phantasies are probably regressive and he suggests the term 'secondary perceptual thinking' for such re-clothing of primary sensations in later 'meaning'.

responseSet.title: Science in History: By J. D. Bernal.  (London:  Watts, 1954.  Pp. xxiv + 967.  42 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Science in History

By J. D. Bernal. (London: Watts, 1954. Pp. xxiv + 967. 42 s.)

M. Brierley

M. Brierley

M. Brierley  

This is a remarkable book but, unfortunately, its value is impaired by tendentious writing due to the author's strong ideological bias. This is amply illustrated by the account of Sigmund Freud and the social implications of psycho-analysis (pp. 815–17) from which the following are excerpts: 'Though Freud himself was always striving to express his findings in terms of objective realities, the actual entities he constructed were far more like the spirits, virtues, and faculties of a medieval morality play than the material entities of the physicist and chemist. … Just because these constructs had no possibility of material existence susceptible to any other form of demonstration, they tended to take on … an absolute character, and created a fixed and almost mythological inferno of evil influences. …' 'Because it stressed the hidden importance of the irrational and primitive aspects of the human mind, the new psychology provided an explanation, indeed practically an excuse, for man's inability to deal with the problems of society. The upshot of Freudian psychology was that man was effectively ruled by his own unconscious instincts, implanted before birth and carefully nurtured for evil in the early upbringing of children.' … 'The search for inner truth is a well-worn way to passive acceptance of outward evil.

408

responseSet.title: The Need to Believe. The Psychology of Religion: By Mortimer Ostow and Ben-Ami Scharfstein.  (New York:  International Universities Press, 1954.  Pp. 162.  $3.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Need to Believe. The Psychology of Religion

By Mortimer Ostow and Ben-Ami Scharfstein. (New York: International Universities Press, 1954. Pp. 162. $3.00.)

M. Brierley

M. Brierley

M. Brierley  

This short book is popular in the sense that it could be read without any previous knowledge of psycho-analysis, but it gives a lucid account of some of the more important aspects of the psychology of religion. The authors wish to present facts rather than to defend a special point of view, but they have definite views to offer. In their opinion, religion has positive psychic and society-protective functions. These include the gratification of otherwise unacceptable passive wishes, the dissipation of unconscious guilt, and the maintenance of the social conscience without which no society could remain stable. The similarity between ritual and compulsive behaviour can be over-emphasized because ritual 'is a practical instrument of ethics. When effective it not only curbs, but also helps to create the desire to serve the community …' (p. 101). While admitting that psychiatry cannot prove or disprove many religious claims, good and bad religions are distinguished mainly on the ground of their social usefulness. Moreover 'Believing is almost as necessary to humans as eating'. 'The particular metaphors in which we define our experience always change, but there is never any change in the need of society to enlist the conscience of its members, or of the individual to believe more than he can prove.' (p. 155)

408

responseSet.title: Handwriting: A Key to Personality: By Klara G. Roman.  (London:  Routledge & Kegan Paul, 1954.  Pp. xi + 382.  25 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Handwriting: A Key to Personality

By Klara G. Roman. (London: Routledge & Kegan Paul, 1954. Pp. xi + 382. 25 s.)

M. Brierley

M. Brierley

M. Brierley  

It seems reasonable to assume that handwriting is self-expressive. The difficulty is to establish 'scientifically' the correlation between specific writing habits and specific character traits. This book summarizes the history of the subject, discusses the developmental stages of handwriting and then reviews the techniques and methods used in its analysis and interpretation. The author gives a table of the 'graphic indicators' and their corresponding personality factors that she herself employs. She has had much experience and, as used by her, this method can produce results that have value for vocational guidance and even for diagnosis between serious and minor mental disorder. The 'Sample Analyses with the Use of Work Sheets' are of considerable interest and lend support to the view that graphology merits further research and is not a parlour game.

408

[[RunningHead]]

Progress in Neurology and Psychiatry: an Annual Review. Vol. VIII.

Ed. by E. A. Spiegel. (New York: Grune & Stratton, 1953. Pp. 591. $10.00.)

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

The fields covered by this review increase and some (e.g. electro-physiology and psychology) are now reviewed every second year. The brief review of psycho-analysis by R. L. Frank and collaborators (pp. 502–16) summarizes 46 articles and refers the reader to 11 others. Such a sketch will be of some use to non-analysts. The review as a whole continues to be of great value to analysts in being a very easily read summary of the fields of neurology and general psychiatry. A detailed index is included.

415

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A History of Psychoanalysis in America

By C. P. Oberndorf. (New York: Grune & Stratton, 1953. Pp. 280. $5.00.)

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

The late Dr. Oberndorf did many services for psycho-analysis. One of his last was this valuable personal summary of the development of psycho-analysis in the U.S.A. It will stimulate older analysts to remember their development and it will do much to help younger analysts and students to see the problems of the present in prospective. Psycho-analysis itself and the relationship of psycho-analysis

415

to medicine, psychiatry, neurology, psychology, and sociology are well covered, but many readers will notice minor inaccuracies and gaps. It is to be hoped that Dr. Oberndorf's literary executors will ask for and be able to make use of corrections and additions before a second edition is published. Every analyst and student should be unsatisfied until he has read this book. It is an excellent basis for a definitive history. The index is detailed.

416

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Third Revolution: A Study of Psychiatry and Religion

By Karl Stern. (New York: Harcourt, Brace & Co., 1954. Pp. 306. $4.75.)

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

Dr. Stern went to Canada from Germany just before the war. He published his biography The Pillar of Fire in 1951. In it he vividly described his conversion to Roman Catholicism. Dr. Stern is now Professor of Psychiatry at the University of Ottawa. In The Third Revolution he criticizes some modern trends in psychology and psychiatry which neglect the essential individuality of patients' lives and states that psycho-analytic theory and practice hold out most hope for a future to psychiatry in which religion is accepted and utilized. He closely argues his belief that science and Christianity must be integrated so that man may feel 'humbled, instead of humiliated'.

416

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Lawrence J. Friedman. 'Regressive Reaction to the Interpretation of a Dream.'

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman  

The patient reacted regressively after the interpretation of a dream. She described feelings and behaviour which reminded her of the feelings and behaviour of a very small baby. This regression had the purpose of avoiding the anxieties associated with the oedipal conflicts.

responseSet.title: The Doors of Perception: By Aldous Huxley.  (London:  Chatto and Windus, 1954.  Pp. 63.  6 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Doors of Perception

By Aldous Huxley. (London: Chatto and Windus, 1954. Pp. 63. 6 s.)

H. Hardenberg

H. Hardenberg

H. Hardenberg  

In this short book Mr. Aldous Huxley describes his own experiences after taking four-tenths of a gramme of mescalin. These, exhilarating and rewarding for him, are extremely interesting and vividly recounted, as one would expect from such a brilliant writer. For this alone the book is worth reading. There is, furthermore, a fascinating dissertation upon the folds of drapery; as well as an excellent account of the artist's relation to this subject. It is a pity that Mr. Huxley did not stop here and leave this as a statement of his own experiment. Mr. Huxley, however, is interested in mescalin itself, its history, its relation to schizophrenia, and especially its possible benefits to humanity. His subject is therefore wide and general, and not all the problems he touches upon can be referred to in this review.

He feels that previous investigators of mescalin have stopped at a point 'well this side of idolatry'. Whether he himself has, is open to question. It is not enough to generalize from one's own experience, and this is what he has done. Others, normal subjects, have taken comparable amounts of mescalin, and had quite different results from those of Mr. Huxley. Some have had extremely disturbing and unpleasant ones. Dr. Guttmann gave a comprehensive account of the drug's effects in 1936 (Journal of Mental Science, 82). This was based on personal observations on sixty subjects, mostly normal. Since then, there have been further accounts of the drug's effects on normal subjects, confirming the variable response. Dr. Guttmann, after mentioning the physical symptoms (such as mydriasis, rapid pulse, raised blood pressure, nausea, or vomiting) goes on to list the chief effects—the well-known hallucinatory and perceptual disturbances, the changes in mood, the splitting of the personality, and disturbances of volition (with lack of impulse and spontaneity), of thinking, and its anaphrodisiac effect—sexual ideas playing no special rôle in the experiences. Mr. Huxley's account of the drug's effects is incomplete and certainly misleading.

</artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Charles Odier
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Charles Odier

Raymond De Saussure

Raymond De Saussure

Raymond De Saussure  

Dr. Charles Odier was born in Geneva in 1888 and died, after a prolonged illness, on 28 July, 1954, at Lausanne.

After his medical studies, which he followed in Geneva, he worked as resident in neurology and psychiatry at Vienna. Thereafter he pursued his studies in medicine in Geneva and Paris and practised for a few years in Geneva.

While attending the lectures of Professor Théodore Flournoy, Dr. Odier took an increasing interest in the study of neurosis. In 1914 he wrote on symbolism in hysterical contractions. He went to Holland in 1922, and was analyzed by Ophuysen. Returning thence, he continued in general practice, but at the same time began work as a specialist. In 1925 he wrote his first book on the Oedipus complex, testifying to his wide understanding of the unconscious processes.

At the first conference of French-speaking psycho-analysts, held in Geneva in 1927, Dr. Odier presented an authoritative report on the various defence mechanisms used by obsessive compulsive and phobic patients. Finding that many French colleagues were becoming interested in his research, he decided to leave for Berlin, to complete his preparation at the Psycho-Analytic Institute of that city. Thereafter, settling down as a specialist in Paris, he practised in France from 1929 to 1939. He delivered several papers to the Psycho-Analytical Society in Paris, lectured at the Institute, and became co-editor of the Revue française de Psychanalyse. His leadership was at once appreciated. As a scrupulous and subtle exponent of the teaching of Freud and his pupils, he succeeded in clarifying to the French mind the often obscure ideas of his German colleagues: and he played a great part in providing a French vocabulary for the language of psycho-analysis.

In 1939 Dr. Odier returned to Switzerland and settled at Lausanne. His first years there were mainly devoted to preparing a number of books. In Les deux sources conscientes et inconscientes de la vie morale (The Two Sources, Conscious and Unconscious, of Moral Life), published in 1943 (a second enlarged edition was issued in 1947), Dr. Odier lays down a distinction between function and value. The principal aim of his work was to show the philosophers and theologians of Switzerland that psycho-analysis was not incompatible with morality; an aim explained by the many prejudices which were then felt against the teachings of Freud. In 1947, Dr. Odier published Angoisse et Pensée Magique (Anxiety and Magic Thought). In which he attempted to harmonize Freud's ideas with those of Jean Piaget. Among this book's merits were its many original considerations regarding problems of anxiety and the criteria of the mature ego.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Elements of Rorschach Interpretation

By Robert M. Allen. (New York: International Universities Press, 1954. Pp. 242. $4.00.)

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler  

This is a companion volume to the author's Introduction to the Rorschach Test. It is an up-to-date account of the conventional processes of evaluating the Rorschach protocol. Recent research has been integrated into the text, and a useful bibliography is given.

This is undoubtedly a good book for any student who wishes to become proficient in the test as it is currently used. One gains the impression that the author is sensitive to the problems of the test's reliability and validity, and his approach is, to some extent, a 'dynamic' one.

419

This is a second edition of a book reviewed in this journal six years ago.1 Since then the book has been considerably enlarged, and now includes T.A.T. pictures given to women. Psycho-analysts might be particularly interested in the detailed T.A.T. analysis of a male case before and after psycho-analysis.

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler  

This little book (with a foreword by Bruno Klopfer) is confined to the techniques of administering and scoring the Rorschach test. As its name suggests, it contains exercises (with answers), and the student who works through this book conscientiously will no doubt achieve a fair standard of scoring proficiency. It can be recommended to anyone who wishes to master the Klopfer and Kelley scoring methods.

419

responseSet.title: Psychological Testing: By Anne Anastasi.  (New York:  The Macmillan Company, 1954.  Pp. 682.  $6.75.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychological Testing

By Anne Anastasi. (New York: The Macmillan Company, 1954. Pp. 682. $6.75.)

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler  

New psychological tests are being produced almost daily, and Professor Anastasi has written a most readable account of the various tests in current use. The varieties considered include intelligence tests, aptitude and achievement tests, and instruments designed to measure personality. In particular, the author has stressed the theoretical principles underlying the tests, and includes references to useful sources of information.

The stress of this book is laid entirely on the tests as such, and there is no discussion of the tester—surely as important a variable as the tests themselves? This work was, as Anastasi herself points out, 'evolved in the classroom', and precisely because of this may present a rather idealized picture of the whole subject. One may gain the impression that psychological tests are, in general, as effective as biochemical tests used in medical work. In the field of personality at least, this is far from true.

The psycho-analyst who is interested in getting an academic bird's-eye view of psychological tests would be well advised to read this book.

420

<artinfo arttype="ART" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ART" id="IJP.036.0369A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1955</artyear><artvol actual="36">36</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">369-369</artpgrg><arttitle>To Sylvia May Payne on the Occasion of her Seventy-Fifth Birthday</arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Hoffer, W." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>W.</nfirst><nlast>Hoffer</nlast></aut>

responseSet.title: To Sylvia May Payne on the Occasion of her Seventy-Fifth Birthday
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

To Sylvia May Payne on the Occasion of her Seventy-Fifth Birthday

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer  

For reasons intrinsic in its approach psycho-analysis—unlike other branches of scientific organization—has been spared a struggle for the emancipation of our women colleagues. Thanks to the pioneering spirit of the early psycho-analysts there has emerged a galaxy of women analysts whose brightness radiates into every corner of our work.

Among British psycho-analysts there is no other woman member who has endeared herself to all her colleagues as much as Dr. Sylvia Payne has done. The advent of her seventy-fifth birthday offers an opportunity to give public expression to our feelings of admiration and gratitude for her work as a clinician, as a teacher of and contributor to the science of psycho-analysis, and above all as an organizer and conductor of our scientific and professional affairs.

For well over thirty years Dr. Sylvia Payne has been a devoted, tireless and wise counsellor to our Society. It was soon after the First World War, when she had relinquished the post of Commandant and Medical Officer in Charge of the Torquay Red Cross Hospital, and had just been honoured by the title of Companion of the British Empire, that she joined the British Psycho-Analytical Society and trained with James Glover and Hanns Sachs. Back in England and reunited with her husband and her growing children, she helped more and more actively to shape the British Psycho-Analytical Society and Institute, discharging in the course of time with exemplary skill, conscientiousness, and consummate tact all the offices the Society and Institute can entrust to its members.

Among her scientific contributions, 'A Conception of Femininity' and 'Some Observations on Ego Development of the Fetishist' are outstanding examples of her searching mind and its integrative qualities.

Called upon in 1944 to preside over the Society at a time of internal crisis, she soon proved her success in combining the traditional British realism, firmness, and genius for compromise with a deep appreciation of psycho-analysis both as a science and as a therapeutic discipline. In the British Psycho-Analytical Society Dr. Sylvia Payne represents stability, constancy, and noblesse; and yet whenever necessary she is an ardent advocate of freedom of thought. Although not much given to speculation she willingly lets herself be guided into it, never failing to show the way back from its darkness into the light of reality. Her clinical work is outstanding, and she enjoys the highest repute among students as well as among those who are in need of help.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Allan Strauss, Ph.D.</cs></ln><ln>LONDON</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Unconscious Mental Processes and the Psychosomatic Concept
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unconscious Mental Processes and the Psychosomatic Concept

Allan Strauss Ph.D.

Allan Strauss

Allan Strauss, Ph.D.
LONDON

Allan Strauss  

Of all the sciences, it is without doubt psycho-analysis which offers the best means of observing in the human being the constant interactions between mental and bodily events. To the analyst such interactions are a matter of everyday clinical experience, and his detailed findings concerning them are basic not only to psycho-analysis itself but also to the closely allied field of psychosomatic medicine. Yet one of the unsolved problems common to both fields is that of body-mind interaction as such, since the very concept of such interaction appears to conflict with the principle that the causes of any given physical event are themselves exclusively physical, and therefore that nothing which is defined as non-physical can in any way intrude into the closed circle of natural causation so as to exert a physical effect.

In might, of course, be claimed that this principle of the closed circle of physical causation has been clearly invalidated by just those above-mentioned clinical observations of body-mind interaction, and in general by the whole current psychosomatic revolution in medicine. But this would actually entail that in each of the vast number of these apparent body-mind interactions (and in every voluntary action, for that matter) something non-physical intervenes somewhere among the atoms and nerve-currents in the body and makes them do what they would not have done otherwise. And physicists and physiologists could not admit such a possibility, since as scientists they proceed on the assumption that any given bodily event could be explained completely in terms of other bodily events provided they knew enough about them.1 The principle of the closed circle of physical causation (with its corollary of the conservation of mass-energy) thus remains a necessary working assumption, and even those clinicians to whom the effects of mental events on bodily ones are undeniable facts are not inclined to question a principle so fundamental to natural science as this, especially since they themselves are natural scientists. Yet there is a very real contradiction between these two equally compelling generalizations: between, that is, the causal effect of mental events upon bodily ones, and the closed circle of physical causation.
