responseSet.title: Fundamentals of Psychoanalytic Technique: By Trygve Braatøy.  (London:  Chapman & Hall Ltd., 1954.  Pp. xii + 404.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fundamentals of Psychoanalytic Technique

By Trygve Braatøy. (London: Chapman & Hall Ltd., 1954. Pp. xii + 404.)

R. Markillie

R. Markillie

R. Markillie  

The title of this book suggests that it could be an important theoretical contribution to psycho-analysis, and just what students have been wanting. Unfortunately it is not. It has many merits, but as a whole it proves disappointing. It is unquestionably Braatøy's testament and most certainly reveals his personality, but it is hardly fundamentals of psycho-analytic technique. Many valuable personal experiences are imparted, and his enthusiasm is infectious, but his discursiveness makes the book difficult to follow, especially for a student. Fresh ideas and comments continually crowd in one upon another. The book needs pruning and shaping severely, but in fact the editing is poor and a number of errors get by.

The first rubric is 'Love the basis for personal psychotherapy'. Love can be sentimentalized so easily and used to justify wild or uncritical treatment, that the unconscious determinant of a therapist's attitude, the counter transference, needs to be well understood to handle it properly. On the first page he suggests that the term 'counter transference' should really be used for a patient's response to his therapist's love, but as this is not argued cogently it tends to leave a confusing impression rather than to suggest a new principle. The understanding and analysis of transference phenomena, especially in its negative aspects, is one of the weakest aspects of the book. The amount of reassurance which creeps into his treatment is quite blatantly not psycho-analytic. The sub-title, 'A Fresh Appraisal of the Methods of Psychotherapy', which appears only on the dust jacket, is a much truer title.

It is only too easy to pick up small points in criticism, but a book so titled should manifest a much greater critical faculty in discussing technique. Here are some examples: P. 103, 'If the patient blocks some words or expressions connected with his feelings one may say, "Why do you do that? You are fond of me, aren't you?" or "You are angry with me, aren't you?" It is not wise to say such things before you are sure that they are so.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Sylvan Keiser, M.D. 'A Manifest Oedipus Complex in an Adolescent Girl', pp. 99–107.

An adolescent girl with ordinary neurotic inhibitions quickly developed an intensely sexualized transference. The feeble repression of incestuous strivings was not symptomatic of a psychotic process. It was possible to recognize the sources leading to the failure of the superego's repressive function. The defective organization of the superego had its genesis in the special conditions surrounding the two parents. The father disappeared during the phallic period, but he frequently promised to visit the patient. His failure to carry out his promises reactivated the original disappointment over his disappearance and repeatedly evoked the false hope that he would return. This pattern kept alive the original sexualized oedipal relationship, which could be neither fulfilled nor resolved because of the physical separation. Furthermore she was burdened by the conscious knowledge that her birth had been intended to save the parents' marriage. Her failure to achieve this intensified her feelings of inadequacy and guilt.

The mother actually encouraged the girl to remain fixated on the oedipal level, while simultaneously seducing the girl and flaunting her own sexual superiority.

76

'Mother, God and Superego.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Freeman, Thomas" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Thomas</nfirst><nlast>Freeman</nlast></aut>
</artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association: James Clark Moloney.  'Mother, God and Superego.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

James Clark Moloney. 'Mother, God and Superego.'

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman  

Mothers who are not emotionally attuned to their infants are liable to disturb the natural infantile rhythms—the need for food, warmth, and protection. This can lead to a conflict originally between the child and mother and later between the individual and his superego (the internalized mother). Sometimes this struggle is given up and the individual may experience a theophany or kenosis—a blinding flash of inspiration which seems to point the way to a resolution of inner conflicts. The individual concerned becomes an advocate of the mother's authority which he originally attempted to destroy. Theophany is an expression of the submission of the self systems (as opposed to the mother systems [superego]). This is accompanied by a regression to the period of infantile dependence on the mother. After describing a method of experimentally induced theophany used in the training of Buddhist priests the author gives an account of instances from theological history of theophany and kenotic experiences. Nearly all contain the phenomena of light and/or a sudden loud noise. Both neurotic and psychotic patients undergo experiences very similar to theophany and kenosis. Although superficially different, there is an underlying similarity between psychotic, kenotic, and theophanic phenomena. Such experiences as theophany and kenosis must be traceable to some previous occurrence. The effect of this is such as to make the individual seek an explanation which will allay his anxiety. Spiritual meanings are therefore often ascribed to these experiences. To what extent these manifestations should be considered the product of a rational or irrational mind depends on the extent to which the ego renounces its contact with reality. Theophany represents a return to the precognitive period. There is a fusion of the precognitive mother with the superego forming a powerful component of the personality. The superego is an intrusion and an extension of the precognitive diffuse mother. It thereafter has an external form and a history of its own. In theophany the self is devoured by the internalized superego-mother-breast.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association: Karl A. Menninger.  'Psychological Aspects of the Organism Under Stress.  Part II:  Regulatory Devices of the Ego under Major Stress.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Karl A. Menninger. 'Psychological Aspects of the Organism Under Stress. Part II: Regulatory Devices of the Ego under Major Stress.'

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman  

Disease must be looked upon as a positive expression of the survival efforts of the organism. Whenever the ego is subjected to internal and external stresses of great intensity radical measures are introduced to maintain the homeostatic balance. Minor stresses are dealt with by relatively 'healthy devices', but these are inadequate in the face of major stress. The author describes five levels of stress-relieving devices which may be employed in the attempt to cope with the influx of stimulation arising from the aggressive and sexual instinctual drives or from environmental sources. Regulatory devices of the first order may be utilized in situations which overtax the ordinary or 'normal' devices. They consist essentially of exaggerations of normal functions, but are now accompanied by sensations of discomfort and unpleasantness. Amongst the most familiar are: hypersuppression, hyper-repression, hyperalertness, minor somatic dysfunctions, hyperkinesis, etc. The first order devices usually disappear once the pressure on the ego is removed. However, this is not invariable. When the first order devices fail to restore the homeostatic balance something further must be done by the ego. The ego is forced to accept a lowered level at which a new homeostatic equilibrium can be established, and it effects this by means of a second order of regulatory devices characterized by a partial detachment from the world of reality. Amongst these second order devices are: (1) dissociation, which includes amnesia, depersonalization, syncopes, etc.; (2) displacement, which includes prejudice, phobias, obsessions, etc.; (3) substitution of magic symbols and behaviour for more open hostility, e.g. compulsions, rituals, perverse sexuality, kleptomania, etc.; (4) substitution of self or part of self as object of displaced aggression. Regulatory devices of a third order are called into action by the emergence of uncontrollable instinctual impulses. The stress-relieving mechanisms are represented by episodic outbursts of aggressive energy which are more or less disorganized. They may take the form of homicidal or suicidal attempts, convulsions, panic attacks, etc. Fourth order devices result in the individual relinquishing his hold on reality. There is yet a fifth order of regulatory devices where the ego fails completely and self-destruction ensues. The author points out that although he has sharply differentiated one level of stress-relieving device from the other, there is in fact always some degree of overlapping.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Patterns of Psychosexual Infantilism

By Wilhelm Stekel. Edited and with an introduction by Emil A. Gutheil. (London: Peter Nevill, 1953. Pp. 412. 18 s.)

Noel Bradley

Noel Bradley

Noel Bradley  

Readers of this Journal will be little interested in this book. It was originally published in 1922, though only the date of the present translation is given, and shows many of the less desirable characteristics of the popularizing writings of that date which were inspired by psycho-analysis. There are no editorial footnotes to indicate developments in psycho-analytic thought since that time, so that the lay reader is faced with such statements as: 'The Freudians point incessantly at the Oedipus Complex (the son's love of the mother) and neglect the homosexual component in the boy's rebellion against the father' (p. 57) as though this represents current opinion, let alone being an accurate description of views held thirty years ago.

It is difficult to credit some of Stekel's utterances, as that 'I have nowhere seen evidence of any physical or psychological harm done by such early seductions of boys' (p. 62). Yet the next paragraph informs the reader that 'Very often the first experience determines the individual's choice of a partner'; which is illustrated by the case of a thirteen-year-old boy who was later sexually potent only with women dressed in the same style as his seductress. This is confusing and, more seriously, misleading.

362

responseSet.title: Human Development: By John P. Zubek and Patricia Anne Solberg.  (New York, Toronto, London:  McGraw-Hill, 1954.  Pp. vii + 476.  48 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Human Development

By John P. Zubek and Patricia Anne Solberg. (New York, Toronto, London: McGraw-Hill, 1954. Pp. vii + 476. 48 s.)

M. Brierley

M. Brierley

M. Brierley  

This is both a summary and a critical review of experimental research on human development from conception to old age. As the Preface states, 'The general orientation of this book is physiological', but it covers a great deal of relevant work in experimental psychology and sociology. The arrangement of material is by subject and each chapter provides a longitudinal section of a particular aspect of behaviour, which makes for ease of reference. Recent work on old age is most fully reported because much of this is still only accessible in monograph form. The extensive Bibliography is mainly American and Canadian with a few English references, e.g., to early work of Burt's. Some omissions, e.g. of Lewin and 'field theory', are presumably due to the fact that their approach is cross-sectional rather than genetic. Psycho-analysis does not appear in the Index and is mentioned only in passing, e.g. as a method of personality appraisal (p. 407). However, any psycho-analyst who wishes to inform himself rapidly of the 'present state' of experimental knowledge of human behaviour in North America would be well advised to consult this book.

402

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Five Copybooks

By Marie Bonaparte. (Four volumes of commentaries and a facsimile of the original copybooks.) (London: Imago Publishing Company, Ltd., 1951. Each volume pp. 300 approx. Per set £16.)

Michael Balint

Michael Balint

Michael Balint  

The history of these five copybooks is told in the introduction. The author found them after the death of her father in the spring of 1924. She was then 42 years of age, and although it was she who between the ages of seven and a half and ten had filled the books with stories and fantasies in her 'secret' language of English, she had lost all memory of their contents. Even after reading them she could not remember having written a single line, although she at once recognized the author as being herself.

412

responseSet.title: Hungarian and Vogul Mythology: By Géza Róheim.  (Monographs of the American Ethnological Society, No. XXII, edited by Esther S. Goldfrank.)  (New York:  J. J. Augustin, 1954.  Pp. x + 86.  $2.75.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hungarian and Vogul Mythology

By Géza Róheim. (Monographs of the American Ethnological Society, No. XXII, edited by Esther S. Goldfrank.) (New York: J. J. Augustin, 1954. Pp. x + 86. $2.75.)

Hilda Abraham

Hilda Abraham

Hilda Abraham  

This monograph is introduced by a foreword by Róheim's literary executor, Warner Muensterberger. Esther S. Goldfrank undertook the editing and bibliography of the work, completed before Róheim's death in June 1953, with the help of some eminent specialists in Hungarian, Russian, Latin, and anthropological sources.

The short work seems an excellent memorial to Róheim's painstaking method of research and his amazing erudition. This latter makes it difficult for a reviewer who is no anthropologist to do justice to his work. It makes fascinating reading, but if there is anything to criticize, it is that its pages contain so many intricate variations of the myths Róheim uses for analysis, and so many names of different tribes in exemplification of his theme, that the psycho-analyst needs a good deal of determination in order to read it right through and reach the conclusions which are of main interest to him.

Róheim begins with the question whether there is a Hungarian mythology to rescue. There are only the Hungarian Chronicles dating back mainly to the eleventh and twelfth centuries as set out in an Appendix by Prof. Dr. J. Lodz. Róheim proves that once there must have been much more, because the Hungarians are identical in origin with the Mansi or Vogul in Western Siberia who had an abundant and elaborate mythology. This fell into oblivion under the ruthless persecution of Hungarian kings newly converted to Christianity. Hungarian, Turkic, and Vogul singers used to relate their epic songs in the first person, and this custom 'projects back to the heroes of ancient times'. Róheim wonders whether the historical personages mentioned in the Chronicles were superimposed on mythical prototypes and thus could pass the censorship of Christianity; that is to say, real happenings may have been told in mythological style.

The first myth discussed is that of the Seven Magyars. They were originally the seven chiefs of the seven united tribes forming the Magyar nation. The seven Magyars also designate seven men left over from a defeated army sent back with their ears cut off and thereafter ostracized. This story may later have been confused with the fact that the Christian king treated heathen survivors as pariahs. To the question why just this myth among many should have survived the answer is that the Sky-God of the Vogul had seven sons.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Samiksa, 8, 1954, No. 1

T. C. Sinha. 'A Case of Aspermia.'

An outline is given of analytic work done with a medical practitioner suffering from aspermia and difficulties with study and examinations.

Analysis of his feminine wishes towards his father led to resolution of his symptoms, including a change from a nil spermatozoal count to a practically normal count.

434

responseSet.title: Samiksa, 8, 1954, No. 2: J. M. Dorsey.  'Some Considerations of the Psycho-Analytical Principle and Religious Living.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Samiksa, 8, 1954, No. 2

J. M. Dorsey. 'Some Considerations of the Psycho-Analytical Principle and Religious Living.'

The central note of this long paper is that only through development of 'the glorious capacities of self-perception and self-observation' can integration be achieved. All that is perceived as outside is but part of the self.

These feelings of 'adoration of our all, reverential feelings towards our total human being, ' when not repressed relate to the feelings towards divinity. 'To have the highest moral worth, an action must be motivated by fully acknowledged self-interest.'

Numerous religious and other quotations are given.

434

responseSet.title: The Abilities of Babies: By Ruth Griffiths.  (London:  University of London Press, 1954.  Pp. 229.  20 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Abilities of Babies

By Ruth Griffiths. (London: University of London Press, 1954. Pp. 229. 20 s.)

E. Anthony

E. Anthony

E. Anthony  

Many psychiatric and psychological workers either express scepticism at the possibility of measuring the infant's abilities reliably or doubt as to its usefulness in any field outside the developmental study. Reading Dr. Griffith's book will convince them of its wider applicability especially in the diagnostic sphere. The author's indebtedness to Gesell is everywhere apparent, but this in no way detracts from the individuality of her approach. The publication is an important event since it is the first British Baby Test, standardized on British babies, and providing developmental profiles that take many aspects of the child into account—but not the 'total child' as many of the organismic writers claim. Cultural loading is not a big factor in baby tests, and it will be the intrinsic merits of the Griffiths test that will lead to its replacing the American versions.

406

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953: Berta Bornstein.  'Masturbation in the Latency Period', pp. 65–78.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Berta Bornstein. 'Masturbation in the Latency Period', pp. 65–78.

This paper is primarily concerned with masturbation in the latency period, its varieties, and their consequences. A survey of the changing dynamics of the activity from the first to the fifth year is made in preparation for a further differentiation within the latency period itself. Early latency is marked by manifest behaviour difficulties and the formation of new neurotic symptoms. Psychic maturation aids the child in the second latency period. The formation of a less rigid superego, increased real gratification in the outer world, and successful sublimation of pregenital impulses are the main determinants for progressive instead of regressive development towards puberty and adolescence.

In the neurotic child the blocking of sexual satisfaction results in traumatic anxiety and break-throughs with paralysing guilt. Cases are quoted where the fear of climax or orgastic sensation led to a compulsive interruption of all pleasurable activity, thought, or speech, before its consummation. Illustrations are offered of the fight against compulsive masturbation and masturbatory equivalents with resulting insomnia, and the question of the isolation of phantasy from the masturbatory act is discussed. So long as the phantasy is attached to the act, it does not interfere with the child's behaviour and adjustment to reality. Where it has no bodily outlet, libidinal and aggressive energy left undischarged may permeate the ego activities. Compulsive talking and excited or quarrelsome behaviour may be the only visible substitute for the relinquished sexual activity.

Clinical difficulties in the elucidation of this highly defended material are discussed throughout the paper, and some helpful comments on upbringing are appended.

76

responseSet.title: Group Psychotherapy: Studies in Methodology of Research and Therapy.  By Florence B. Powdermaker, Jerome D. Frank, and others.  (Cambridge, Mass.:  Harvard University Press.  London:  Geoffrey Cumberlege, 1953.  Pp. 615.  42 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Group Psychotherapy

Studies in Methodology of Research and Therapy. By Florence B. Powdermaker, Jerome D. Frank, and others. (Cambridge, Mass.: Harvard University Press. London: Geoffrey Cumberlege, 1953. Pp. 615. 42 s.)

S. Foulkes

S. Foulkes

S. Foulkes  

This account of a research project is presented in two sections: the first is concerned with groups of neurotic patients at the Veterans Administration's Mental Hygiene Clinic in Washington, D.C., and the other with chronic schizophrenic patients at the Veterans Administration's Hospital at Perry Point, from July 1947 to May 1949. The research staff concerned with both parts consisted of four psychiatrists, five clinical psychologists, and two psychiatric social workers, apart from a further twenty psychiatrists in charge of groups.

The therapy was analytic in orientation, and its goals those of psychotherapy in general. The dynamics of group interaction in its relation to therapy was a special feature of the research. Each psychiatrist used his own approach, but as the project developed the different approaches tended to converge.

The groups lasted one and a half hours, and were attended by a (mostly silent) observer who prepared narrative reports of each meeting. This was found to be the most suitable form of recording, but wire recordings (with an unconcealed microphone) were also used. After discussion between the doctor and the observer, summaries of the meetings were prepared. The aim of developing an objective method of studying group therapy led to the isolation of specific problems as experienced by the therapist. A form of 'situation analysis' was devised to sift the vast material and to bring out cause and effect relationships.

The groups of neurotic clinic patients were open ones over relatively short periods (three to nineteen months). Attendances were rather irregular, averaging five patients. A large proportion received individual psychotherapy as well. In 27 per cent of the patients who improved under combined individual and group psychotherapy, the doctors considered that the main weight of the therapy had been carried by the group in spite of the more intensive contact in individual sessions. For another 13 per cent both types of therapy were considered of equal importance, and in about 40 per cent group therapy was judged to be an important factor in their improvement. Many patients left the group treatment.

responseSet.title: Ludwig Jekels 1867–1954
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ludwig Jekels 1867–1954

Edmund Bergler

Edmund Bergler

Edmund Bergler  

Dr. Ludwig Jekels died in New York City on Saturday, April 3, at the age of 87, while reading a literary journal. An hour earlier, Mrs. Jekels saw him in his favourite position on the couch, one arm resting on the pillow, looking through the journal; an hour later, the fact that he had not changed his posture aroused suspicion in Mrs. Jekels; she looked more closely and found him dead. The facial expression was calm; death must have been instantaneous, without suffering.

One of the oldest pupils of Freud, Jekels was for nearly fifty years engaged in psycho-analytic practice and in never-ending study of theoretical problems of our science. He became in later years something of a symbol and a mythological figure to the younger and middle-aged generation of analysts. He was conscious of this fact, and sometimes complained ironically, 'I don't belong to this age'.

71

responseSet.title: Group Work with the Aged: By Susan H. Kubie and Gertrude Landau.  (New York:  International Universities Press, 1953.  Pp. 214.  $3.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Group Work with the Aged

By Susan H. Kubie and Gertrude Landau. (New York: International Universities Press, 1953. Pp. 214. $3.50.)

Marjorie Franklin

Marjorie Franklin

Marjorie Franklin  

This book gives an account of the first nine years of the Wm. Hodson Community Centre (in U.S.A.), a day recreation club for elderly men and women.

It is of considerable interest, and the style is

359

clear, restrained, and conveys a sense of warmth and affectionate respect for the subjects described, combined with the obvious sincerity which has enabled the workers to succeed in their pioneer field. It is written in non-technical language that can appeal to the general reader, but contains much thought and psychological understanding.

Although one of the most interesting chapters describes the group feeling that influences the 'non-participating members' (stimulating a crippled and frail old woman to make the arduous journey regularly just to sit among her fellows), nevertheless most of the book is concerned with describing a multitude of activities—craftwork, poetry, music, drama, discussions, self-government, etc. The contention is well supported that 'change and growth have no age limits'.

The Centre has the aim of 'restoring to the old the right to live life to the end with the fullest opportunity to use one's capacity'. At this Centre aged men and women learn new skills and form new relationships and social adjustments at their own pace, under the influence of interacting forces among a group of their contemporaries, combined with the skilled help of younger trained social workers. The power to learn, to change, and to develop was observed not only in the well educated of wide experience but also, and very strikingly, in men and women of poor education who had lived narrow lives and were suffering deprivation of familiar relationships (immigrants, etc.).

Many examples are given to elucidate the descriptions, and the same names recur in different sections. Unfortunately the absence of an index prevents one following any case through. This book should be recommended to all concerned in group work with the elderly, and it is to be hoped there will be new and expanded editions including an index.

responseSet.title: Memoirs of my Nervous Illness: By Daniel Paul Schreber.  Translated, edited, with introduction, notes and discussion, by Ida Macalpine and Richard A. Hunter.  (London:  Wm. Dawson & Sons, 1955.  Pp. 416.  35 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Memoirs of my Nervous Illness

By Daniel Paul Schreber. Translated, edited, with introduction, notes and discussion, by Ida Macalpine and Richard A. Hunter. (London: Wm. Dawson & Sons, 1955. Pp. 416. 35 s.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

Macalpine and Hunter have rendered a valuable service in furnishing a competent and carefully edited translation of Schreber's famous memoirs which played such an important part in the history of psycho-analysis. They are critical of some of Freud's interpretations and do not share his views on the etiological significance of the conflict over

399

homosexuality. They stress the importance of pregenital factors in the origin of the psychosis. They do not accept the concept of transference as evolved by Freud. They interpret Schreber's psychosis as 'a reactivation of unconscious archaic procreation fantasies concerning life, death, immortality, rebirth, creation including self-impregnation, and accompanied by absolute ambisexuality expressed in doubts and uncertainty about his sex'. In their view homosexual anxieties were secondary to the fantasy of having to be transformed into a woman. They believe Schreber's psychosis to have been precipitated by his wife's childlessness which reactivated his unconscious procreation fantasies.

Whatever we may think about the translators' views, we shall agree with them that Freud's interpretation should not be taken as the last word on the origin of paranoid illness.

400

responseSet.title: The Psycho-Analyst and the Artist: By Daniel E. Schneider.  (New York:  International Universities Press. 1950.  Pp. 303.  $4.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psycho-Analyst and the Artist

By Daniel E. Schneider. (New York: International Universities Press. 1950. Pp. 303. $4.00.)

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

In a book of popular orientation the author sets out to tackle the question of the essential nature of the artistic gift, of what makes great art. This is a bold enterprise, and if he succeeds in adding some original views of his own to an adequate and relevant summary of existing views on the psychology of the artist and of art he is to be congratulated. Dr. Schneider does both of these.

His main theme is that the artist's work is the precursor and equivalent of that of the psycho-analyst. The artist is also the interpreter of unconscious themes. With this he formulates something more explicit than has been implied by the phrase 'The artist is in touch with his unconscious'. He shows—notably in a study of Oedipus Rex—how the artist consciously or unconsciously provides specific interpretations of the unconscious material he presents. He continues by developing the thesis that the relationship of the public to the artist is analogous to a transference-relationship. In a drama, for instance, by means of identification with the protagonists, the members of the audience submit their fantasies to the dramatist for interpretation, and are thereby made more aware of the contrast between these fantasies and reality. Some of this may have been implicit in views generally held; Dr. Schneider has certainly elaborated it.

If the artist is primarily an interpreter, it is his style, or the form he gives to the basic contents, which becomes the hallmark of artistic achievement. Dr. Schneider attacks the problem from an original angle. Taking a clue from Freud's remarks in his book on Wit on the relationship between physical motions and their quantitative psychic reflection, Dr. Schneider links the problem of artistic form with the ablity to capture physical motion and reflect it in the operations of the psychic economy. It is this power which gives a work of art its quality of aliveness. It is through this capacity that the artist unites motion with emotion. This is a valuable suggestion. It might well be that there is a special type of employment of the perceptual apparatus and organization in its memory-traces which is specific for the genesis of artistic talent. A bridge might here be found between psycho-analytic and Gestalt psychology. Such a special organization might also relate, not only to the ability of the great artist to innovate technically, but also to the relationship of this ability to the achievement of a new view of nature. It also links with the question of the different use made of their interest in nature by the artist and the scientist, a problem mentioned by Dr. Schneider but not explored.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 2: Lawrence S. Kubie.  'The Fundamental Nature of the Distinction between Normality and Neurosis.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 2

Lawrence S. Kubie. 'The Fundamental Nature of the Distinction between Normality and Neurosis.'

This paper is an attempt to formulate more exactly the concept of the essence of neurosis which Kubie has approached in many previous writings. His idea is that a nuclear, as opposed to a holistic, definition is possible. He finds this possibility in the type of determination of any individual act of behaviour or mentation. In so far as the alliance of conscious and preconscious mental systems is dominant the act is normal; in so far as unconscious determination is dominant it is neurotic. This must be so because domination by the conscious-preconscious alliance means the possibility of flexibility of response, whilst domination by unconscious motivation implies domination by unmodifiable repetitive responses. It is just this rigidity of response which characterizes the neurotic process.

Kubie deals in detail with numerous misunderstandings and criticisms of his theory.

423

responseSet.title: The Unconscious Origin of Berkeley's Philosophy: By Oulton Wisdom.  International Psycho-Analytical Library No. 47.  (London:  Hogarth Press and Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 1953.  Pp. xii + 244.  25 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Unconscious Origin of Berkeley's Philosophy

By Oulton Wisdom. International Psycho-Analytical Library No. 47. (London: Hogarth Press and Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 1953. Pp. xii + 244. 25 s.)

J. Flugel

J. Flugel

J. Flugel  

Of the various attempts to illumine psycho-analytically the lives of historical figures few have dealt with philosophers, though some attempts in this direction are to be found in the earlier volumes of Imago. The author of this book, who is exceptionally qualified for the task, has already shown his interest in the subject in short studies of Descartes and Schopenhauer already published in this Journal. The present work on Berkeley is undoubtedly the most outstanding effort in this field. Indeed, in its scholarship, thoroughness and detailed clarity of presentation, it ranks among the most important applications of psycho-analytical insight to biographical and literary material. True, as the title indicates, it is a study of Berkeley's philosophy rather than of his life. Nevertheleys, since it endeavours to show how this philosophy was largely determined by the deepest strata of his psychic being, much light is thrown also on Berkeley the man, that remarkable combination of ecclesiastic, philosopher, mathematician, economist, tar-water enthusiast and would-be reformer of higher education.

The analysis of Berkeley's philosophy is recorded in 55 short 'interpretations', each summarizing in a few lines the results of the immediately preceding discussion. Berkeley's fundamental conflict, according to the author, lies in an ambivalent attitude to faeces, which he regarded as both 'good' and 'bad', though the bad aspects, felt as 'poison', predominated at the more conscious levels. The badness of faeces extended to 'matter' in general, and matter, it seemed, could be abolished, or at least discounted, by a subjectivist philosophy of esse est percipi. Through this philosophy the external world could be subjectivized in the waking state, in much the same way as happens in the sleeping state in dreams. Matter, furthermore, according to this philosophy, was inert, could exercise no force or power. Active power which gives unity and order to our presentations must be sought in spirit. Pure solipsism seemed inadequate for this task, and therefore the ultimate power must lie in God, who in this capacity is the very antithesis of evil 'matter'. Such, however, was the underlying attraction to, and fear of, matter that its existence had to be strenuously denied, lest God himself should become material and poisonous. This ambivalence shows itself also in Berkeley's hostility to deists and mathematicians, whom he secretly envied for their ability to handle dangerous things and ultimately to turn God himself into poison. Later, as a still more desperate measure, he tried to identify himself with God, this being symbolized on an earthly plane by his attempt to act as founder and inspired teacher of a College in Bermuda—a less contaminated place than could be found in Europe. When, largely owing to the failure of Sir Robert Walpole to forward the promised money, this scheme fell through, Berkeley, who had been impatiently waiting in Rhode Island, returned to the British Isles and succumbed increasingly to a psychosomatic disorder, a 'nervous cholic', for which he sought a remedy in tar water, a 'deobstruent' which he hoped would free him from the internal poison which he was unable to keep at bay. In a chapter on 'childhood reconstructions' it is inferred that Berkeley suffered in infancy from fluid stools. His desire to replace 'flux' by orderly separated faecal masses led, among other things, to his objection to Sir Isaac Newton's 'fluxions', as the newly discovered calculus was called.

responseSet.title: Clinical and Theoretical Observations on Male Homosexuality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical and Theoretical Observations on Male Homosexuality

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman
GLASGOW

Thomas Freeman  

SUMMARY

The clinical material presented here confirms Freud's views that there is nothing specific about the psychological factors to be found in cases of male homosexuality. The material also suggests that the 'masculine protest' of homosexual patients has a defensive function protecting them from an underlying passive femininity of which they are afraid. In this they differ in no way from certain patients suffering from potency difficulties. It is unlikely that there is a specific 'homosexual ego'. The more disturbed the early childhood experiences the more abnormal will the ego be, and this need not be accompanied by manifest masculinity. It is equally possible, as has been seen, for homosexuality to occur in the presence of a relatively intact ego.

responseSet.title: Siegfried Bernfeld 1892–1953
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Siegfried Bernfeld 1892–1953

Hedwig Hoffer

Hedwig Hoffer

Hedwig Hoffer  

At the turn of this century a growing group of educational reformers with strong liberal leanings made themselves felt in Central Europe. They dimly sensed the approaching disaster and tried to ward it off by more enlightened education, more social justice, and better psychological insight into the needs of the coming generations. In this movement Gustav Wynecken was an outstanding figure. Impressed and stimulated by the English Public School system, he founded the Landerziehungsheim Wickersdorf, where he tried to uproot the usual authoritarian school system and give life and reality to the cultural and democratic ideals of the progressive intellectuals in Imperial Germany. Co-education and the sharing of administrative responsibilities between teachers and pupils were among the novel features of Wickersdorf.

In his foreword to Kinderheim Baumgarten Bernfeld names Maria Montessori, Berthold Otto and Gustav Wynecken as the educators whose ideas he tried to synthesize in his own experiment with new forms of education. More will be said about Kinderheim Baumgarten in its proper chronological place, since the experience which Bernfeld gained in connexion with this experiment marks a turning-point in his emotional and intellectual development.

Bernfeld was a prolific writer, and his first paper was written when he was eighteen, an undergraduate at the University of Vienna. Biology and psychology attracted him from the start. A review of William Stern's Psychological Methods of Intelligence Tests and their Application to School Children was his first publication. In the following year came his first paper in the 'Zeitschrift' (Internationale Zeitschrift für Psychoanalyse) on 'Unconscious Determination of the Thinking Process. A Self-Observation'. 'Psycho-analysis and Psychology' followed next year in the same journal, to mention only the psycho-analytically oriented papers of the years preceding World War I.

The bibliography of Bernfeld's writings at the end of this obituary notice enables us to trace the different steps of his intellectual development; more than this, they tell us also of his reactions to the outbreak of the 1914 war and the emotional hardships of the years that followed. Resigned to the fact that the time was uncongenial to educational reforms, he turned to specific war problems and tried to contribute something to their solution.

responseSet.title: Walter Schmideberg
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Walter Schmideberg

Edward Glover

Edward Glover

Edward Glover  

Walter Schmideberg died in Switzerland on 22 September, 1954, of heart failure following a severe operation. He was sixty-four years of age.

With his death one more link is broken with the pioneering phase of psycho-analysis which came to an end only a few years after the First World War; and, by the same token, psycho-analysis loses one of the more colourful personalities that from time to time have added some glamour to its otherwise conventional professional façade. That he was better known and appreciated among an older generation of psycho-analysts was due partly to his retiring disposition and partly to the fact that, once a new and at first unconventional science becomes organized along professional lines, its younger members have less need of and rate less highly the qualities of mind that sustain the pioneer. In those earlier times psycho-analysis attracted to its ranks a number of adventurous spirits who had already gained distinction through their personal character and their work in other fields. Walter Schmideberg had a special claim to recognition in this respect; for he broke with an aristocratic tradition, to say nothing of an adventurous and distinguished army career, to become and remain for the rest of his life a devoted student of psycho-analysis.

He was born in Vienna on 5 April, 1890, of an American father and Austrian mother and his training and education were designed to fit him for the life of a gentleman and a professional army officer. At an early age he passed into the old Imperial Austrian Army and, at a time when Hungary was still part of the Empire, rose to the rank of Rittmeister (Captain) in a crack regiment of Hungarian Hussars. Already as a young subaltern he was recognized as a fine athlete and sportsman, and, before war broke out, was one of the most daring steeplechase riders in Austria.

Even during this first and strenuous phase he devoted much time to intellectual pursuits.

213

'Notes on the Theory of Transference.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1: Heinrich Racker.  'Notes on the Theory of Transference.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1

Heinrich Racker. 'Notes on the Theory of Transference.'

Racker adds the following considerations to Freud's formulation of the dynamics of the transference. (1) Free association abrogates the dominance of those ideas which reject impulses. Though superficially rejected ideas may be projected upon the analyst, at base the ideas projected are always the rejecting ones—moral, affective, and instinctive. Feelings of destruction and persecution do not form an exception; they must be regarded as primitive rejecting ideas directed against impulses. This phenomenon is probably a consequence of the identification of the primitive ego with its impulses. Interference originally came from the outside. Thus the rejecting ideas are the surrogates of the early rejecting objects who are one and the same as the first libidinal objects. Racker notes that if it is the giving up of rejection which is responsible for this transference, then it is due not to a resistance, but to the partial relinquishment of a resistance. (2) Since the rejecting objects are also those who are most needed, the more they are projected on to the analyst the more he is needed. Hence an intensification of the need for love already noted by Freud as basic to the transference. (3) A reverse process is also at work. The understanding of the analyst, taken as affection, increases the patient's capacity for loving. But the more the analyst is loved the more the patient's archaic paranoid and depressive anxieties are stimulated. (4) Analytic transference is characterized not only by its intensity but also by its depth. The analysis of the transference of the rejecting superego uncovers the transference of the rejecting internal libidinal objects derived from the genitally frustrating parents of the oedipal phase with whose rejecting action the patient's ego has become linked.

The author next considers what light these considerations throw on the rôle of the transference in therapy.

Freud regarded the negative and sexual transferences both as a resistance to remembering and as the field on which infantile experience could be rectified. The reconciliation of these views has exercised other analysts. But if Racker's view is correct, that the giving up of rejection in free association ultimately threatens the ego with the re-emergence of unbearable object-relations, then it is against this that the resistance must be directed. Their repetition and correction, not the search for memories, must therefore be the aim of treatment. Actually this repetition is memory, since the past is felt as present in the unconscious. In so far as it is felt in the analysis as past, its remembrance is, broadly speaking, a resistance against the present.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 3

Stanley Steinberg and Joseph Weiss. 'The Art of Edvard Munch and Its Function in his Mental Life.'

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

The character of the work of Edvard Munch changed after a psychotic illness. Before it his pictures were dominated by his fears of object-relationship—of visual over-stimulation, destructive incorporation and loss. After it they became more tranquil. This was possible because his pictures themselves became human objects for him which he treated in such a way as to protect himself against these fears.

424

'Ego and Super-Ego in Obsessional Character and Neurosis.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4: Philip Weissman.  'Ego and Super-Ego in Obsessional Character and Neurosis.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4

Philip Weissman. 'Ego and Super-Ego in Obsessional Character and Neurosis.'

Weissman differentiates between the operations of the pre-genital, or archaic, and the genital super-ego. The archaic super-ego is formed by the internalization of objects to whom aggressive energy adheres in unneutralized form. These internalizations are more transient than the introjections which form the genital super-ego, and they are undone not so much by modification as by projection. Less

425

differentiated from the external world and from the id they may be said to form an 'intro-ego' or 'undifferentiated ego-conscience' rather than a super-ego. The intro-ego threatens with loss of objects and loss of love rather than with annihilation, castration and loss of self-esteem, as does the mature super-ego.

The particular vicissitude in super-ego formation responsible for the obsessional neurosis is that the archaic internalized objects shift their position in relation to the more mature objects so that they operate alongside them. Retaining their cathexis they have priority in governing phallic and anal-sadistic drives. Weissman examines several classical obsessions to differentiate the influence of the introjects of each stage.

The appearance of obsessional symptoms in latency has been accounted for by the over-rigidity of a newly formed super-ego. While not denying that this plays a part, the author points to the failure in repression of the Oedipus complex as a prominent feature of the disorder. He considers the symptoms of obsessional neurosis, characterized by magic and pseudo-morality, to be the attempt of an immature super-ego to protect against the Oedipal onslaught, expressed in terms of an anal regression. When the regression has occurred after the establishment of a mature super-ego the outcome is not obsessional neurosis at all, nor its equivalent a compulsive character, but a true anal character as described by Freud. Symptoms are minimal, and the super-ego is occupied with problems of self-esteem and of real morality.

Obsessional symptoms occurring in the pre-Oedipal period Weissman similarly relates to the loss of the parents as allies, which leaves the immature ego only able to organize a bewildering world by means of archaic parental identifications. Whether a pre-Oedipal neurosis has always preceded a post-Oedipal one deserves investigation.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Andrew Peto</cs></ln><ln>SYDNEY</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: On So-Called "Depersonalization"
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On So-Called "Depersonalization"11

Andrew Peto

Andrew Peto

Andrew Peto
SYDNEY

Andrew Peto  

SUMMARY

I assume that depersonalization phenomena, in clinical experience, in the transference neurosis, in the course of normal development and as illustrated in folklore, all refer to a common basic process. This is a phase of defusion of the two basic instincts—as postulated by Freud—that always occurs during mental activity that is not in the service of immediate drive gratification. I suggest that this is a repetition of a universal human experience; that of the baby who loses the nipple from his mouth. I suggest that this experience represents the inevitable transitory phase between the trauma of losing the object and the defensive stage of coping with this loss through integrating the lost object into the ego with the help of some archaic defence mechanism.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer  

At the conclusion of this, the 36th volume of the INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, it should be recorded that in the year 1955 the Journal has changed from a quarterly periodical to one published in six parts, one part succeeding the other at a two-months' interval only. This change was initiated by Dr. W. Clifford M. Scott and agreed upon by the other members of the Editorial Committee as well as by the Board of the Institute of Psycho-Analysis in London, who at the same time did not insist on raising the subscription rate. The Editorial Committee is convinced that the increase of administrative work and responsibility will be outweighed by the more frequent and closer contact with the readers and that by such relationship the quality of our publication can be affected favourably.

The shortening of the interval between publications does not necessarily imply an increase in the number of pages, but this can easily be effected, of course, if the necessary conditions can be met.

It was eventually decided to publish the two parts covering the months July to October as one issue as it was thought the more compact form would facilitate reading during the summer vacation. This need not become a custom, but most probably 'double numbers' will prove necessary from time to time in the future. Readers' and subscribers' comments on that or any other topic will be appreciated.

It should further be put on record that Dr. Charles Rycroft (London) has joined the Editorial Committee as one of the Assistant Editors and has thus filled the vacancy created by Dr. W. Clifford M. Scott's change of domicile from London to Montreal; Dr. Scott in return has filled the vacancy due to the death of Dr. Clarence P. Oberndorf (New York) and together with Dr. Robert P. Knight (Stockbridge) will represent the Editorial Committee in the Western Hemisphere.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4: Bertram D. Lewin.  'Sleep, Narcissistic Neurosis, and the Analytic Situation.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4

Bertram D. Lewin. 'Sleep, Narcissistic Neurosis, and the Analytic Situation.'

Lewin attempts to formulate first ecstasy, hypomania and depression, then the analytic situation, in terms of the metapsychology of the dream.

He has already interpreted both ecstasy and blank dreams in terms of the wish for fusion with the breast of the satiated nursling. Since in Freud's formulation dreaming is the exception to the narcissism of sleep, Lewin argues that the nearer a dream is to a blank dream the fewer are the impurities in the narcissism. Ecstasy and the blank dream may therefore be equated metapsychologically. But the sense of reality in ecstasy is similar to that in dreams in general. Since in ecstasy it comes from the energic cathexis of condensed oral experiences, the origin of the sense of reality in dreams may therefore have been oversimplified by Freud when he attributed it to the cathexis of visual memory-traces; the oldest oral elements may always contribute.

Blank dreams may occur at the conclusion of a period of ecstasy and usher in a period of hypomania. The same narcissistic wish as blank dreams and ecstasy express must therefore persist into hypomania. Hypomania may therefore be likened to that part of the manifest content of a dream which denies the latent thought. The euphoria is in fact derived from the wish, but its cathexis is displaced to later (mainly motor) memory-traces and the regression is disguised in the manifest picture.

In depression the first step is a similar narcissistic regression. This is equally true of toxic and psychogenic depressions, since the toxin induces a state resembling sleep. In depression, however, the manifest picture is one of blatant unpleasure which Lewin equates with painful weaning and waking. The super-ego is the derivative not only of the father, but of all early wakers, such as light and noise. In depression wish for sleep is expressed by the ego which hears the call but fails to respond.

Lewin next turns his attention to the analytic situation.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs style="AuthorName">M. Woolf, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>TEL-AVIV</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: On Castration Anxiety
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Castration Anxiety11

M. Woolf M.D.

M. Woolf

M. Woolf, M.D.
TEL-AVIV

M. Woolf  

In the libidinal development of the child, the castration complex plays a rôle secondary in importance only to the Oedipus complex, but as yet much of it is obscure and undelineated.

Almost all our knowledge of the masculine castration complex is contained in the works of Freud, according to whom it is the fusion of the following two circumstances which contributes most to its establishment:

1. Castration threat as a punishment for masturbation and bedwetting, and

2. The discovery of the female genital which lends reality to this threat.

The boy enters the Oedipus phase; he begins to manipulate his penis, and simultaneously has phantasies of carrying out some sort of activity with it in relation to his mother; but at last, owing to the combined effect of a threat of castration and the spectacle of women's lack of a penis, he experiences the greatest trauma of his life, and this introduces the period of latency with all its attendant consequences.2

Only one of these instances does not seem to have the same severely traumatic effect:

In the course of these investigations [of sexual curiosity—M.W.] the child makes the discovery that the penis is not one of the possessions common to all creatures who are like himself. The accidental sight of the genitals of a little sister or a little playmate is the occasion of this. … We know how they [the boys] react to their first perception of the absence of the penis. They deny its absence, and believe they do see a penis all the same; the discrepancy between what they see and they imagine is glossed over by the idea that the penis is still small and will grow; gradually they come to the conclusion, so fraught with emotion, that at least it had been there and had at some time been taken away. The absence of the penis is thought to be the result of a castration and then the child is faced with the task of dealing with the thought of a castration in relation to himself.3

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association: Annie Reich.  'Early Identifications as Archaic Elements in the Super-Ego.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Annie Reich. 'Early Identifications as Archaic Elements in the Super-Ego.'

A distinction is made between identifications with parents seen in a glorified light and identifications resulting from the breakdown of the Oedipus complex. The former represent the ego-ideal, the latter the superego. The reader is reminded that Freud's original conception of the ego-ideal was much wider than the later concept of the superego. Most frequently the superego is equated with the conscience. Superego identifications are differentiated structures within the ego, while earlier identifications are usually thought of as being completely absorbed into the ego (ego identifications which do not lead to a differentiation within the ego). The paper aims to demonstrate that early identifications do not always result in a complete integration within the ego. Early identifications may occur as an attempt to compensate for a narcissistic injury. They may result in a longing to be like the idealized parent (the ego-ideal). The ego is measured against these longings. Self-esteem depends upon the relationship between them and the ego. Depending upon the degree of ego maturity, the individual will or will not attempt in reality to fulfil the standard imposed by the ego ideal. The less developed the ego the more will these ideals express themselves by imaginary wish fulfilments—as narcissistic phantasies. This is the most primitive method of restoring wounded narcissism. The origin of accentuated narcissistic ideals appears to be twofold: (1) sudden threats to narcissism, e.g. strong castration anxiety; (2) the early identifications which are of an essentially primitive nature. The child imitates whatever momentarily attracts his attention—he is the object. The later wish to be like the object presupposes some degree of realization of the self and object as distinct entities. Normally these passing identifications develop into permanent ones. The increase in reality-awareness leads to the parental figures being divested of their grandiose, infantile aspects. A clinging to the original conception of the parental imagos indicates that reality testing has not developed adequately. Omnipotence can be attained for the self by identification with these parental images. In more abnormal cases normal identifications do not occur; imitative ones more appropriate to early childhood remain. Often there is little desire or insufficient ability to achieve the narcissistic ideals. Concurrently there is little capacity to tolerate tension; gratification of desires must be instantaneous. The narcissistic phantasies remain unattainable with a resulting state of dissatisfaction characterized by feelings of inferiority and moods of depression. Two case histories are described to illustrate the points mentioned.

Medical Psychology

By Paul Schilder. Trans. and ed. by David Rapaport. (New York: International Universities Press, 1954. Pp. 418. $7.50.)

H. Klein

H. Klein

H. Klein  

This is the first English translation of a book written in 1923. In his preface Schilder says that he is attempting to integrate phenomenology, experimental psychology, brain pathology, and psycho-analysis, and in this, to some extent, he succeeds; although he may convince the psycho-analyst of the unity of the whole nervous and psychic apparatus more easily than his other readers.

The book may be divided into two main parts, the first of which is concerned with the theory of perception, action and language, and memory.

responseSet.title: Aggression and its Interpretation: By Lydia Jackson, D.Phil., B.Sc. (Oxon.).  (London:  Methuen, 1954.  Pp. 237.  21 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Aggression and its Interpretation

By Lydia Jackson, D.Phil., B.Sc. (Oxon.). (London: Methuen, 1954. Pp. 237. 21 s.)

Frank Philips

Frank Philips

Frank Philips  

This book records the author's attempt 'to discover, through the study of aggressive behaviour in children, how the self-assertive urge, on which the survival of the individual depends, becomes deflected from creative to destructive ends, and how it is again re-directed into constructive channels'. Dr. Jackson claims to have successfully split the concept of aggression into positive and negative forms. Numerous examples of aggressive and destructive behaviour in neurotic, delinquent, and normal children are given, compared, and presented statistically. The author has devised a pictorial projection test of family attitudes, which is also described. She relies on this material to substantiate her conviction that there exists in the child a single instinctual urge, that of 'aggressive self-realization'. In her view there exists no dualism of instincts, and presumably therefore no possibility of the mitigation of hatred by love. Dr. Jackson believes that the child is not innately 'negatively' aggressive but becomes so through rigid and unsympathetic handling, i.e. if its self-assertive urges are misdirected, especially owing to the conscious or unconscious negative attitudes of the mother. Notwithstanding all this the author's psychological vocabulary embraces many psycho-analytical terms and concepts. In view of this and since she is so interested in the study of aggression in children it is surprising that, although she refers to the ideas of many psychologists and psycho-analysts, she ignores the writings of Melanie Klein.

362

responseSet.title: Psychological Reflections: An Anthology of the Writings of C. G. Jung.  Selected and edited by Jolande Jacobi.  (London:  Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1953.  Pp. xxvii + 342.  30 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychological Reflections

An Anthology of the Writings of C. G. Jung. Selected and edited by Jolande Jacobi. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1953. Pp. xxvii + 342. 30 s.)

W. Ronald Fairbairn

W. Ronald Fairbairn

W. Ronald Fairbairn  

This volume consists of a careful selection of some of the more pregnant passages in Jung's numerous works, and will thus appeal to such as find many of his writings tiresome to read owing to the unsystematic nature of his thought. The selected passages are more concerned with his conclusions than with the arguments upon which these are based; and they present a very good picture of his general point of view, philosophical no less than psychological. The selections are arranged in sections under comprehensive headings such as 'Consciousness and the Unconscious' and 'Doctor and Patient'. The sections are of unequal value; and some are full of what appear to be arbitrary pronouncements—the prevailing atmosphere being rather that of the master instructing his pupils. That some of these pronouncements contain much wisdom is not to be gainsaid; and many provide a challenge to thought. Doubtless readers who hold the view that Jung's influence chiefly resides in his personality will be confirmed in their opinion. Be that as it may, all will agree that Jolandi Jacobi has performed a most efficient editorial task.

362

<artinfo arttype="ART" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ART" id="IJP.036.0043A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1955</artyear><artvol actual="36">36</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">43-52</artpgrg><arttitle>Homicide During a Schizophrenic Episode<ftnx r="F001" type="PG">1</ftnx></arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Sheehan-Dare, Helen" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Helen</nfirst><nlast>Sheehan-Dare</nlast></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs style="AuthorName">Helen Sheehan-Dare</cs></ln><ln>LONDON</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Homicide During a Schizophrenic Episode
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Homicide During a Schizophrenic Episode11

Helen Sheehan-Dare

Helen Sheehan-Dare

Helen Sheehan-Dare
LONDON

Helen Sheehan-Dare  

The aim of this paper is to put before you some facts for study and discussion which might contribute something to the understanding of the nature of what is sometimes called a 'schizophrenic episode'. Questions concerning the relation between consciousness, memory and ego-control arise naturally out of the consideration of these.

They are taken from the case of a man, to whom I shall refer by the initials K. M., hanged at the age of twenty-six for the murder of his father. In fact, he murdered both parents and threw their bodies over a cliff. He then took his father's car, which he had hoped to borrow for this purpose, and drove to London during the night, making the journey of 245 miles, with several stops, in seven hours, in order to reach a girl, whom I shall call Jennifer, by whom he had become attracted. There was an element of jealousy of a boy of sixteen in this relationship. On the evening of that day, about twenty-four hours after the crime, he was arrested outside the house in which the girl lived.

I was asked to be present at his trial because, as a boy, he had been a patient of mine. The plea was insanity, and two psychiatrists, in evidence for the defence, gave it as their opinion that he was schizophrenic and that the crime had been committed during a 'schizophrenic episode'.

My patient was of rather less than average height. He had a mop of unruly brown hair, was abnormally pale, and looked years younger than his age. By nature he was gentle and rather passive and had never committed any act of violence (except one upon himself, recorded in adolescence) until the evening when he killed his parents in their home.

I will quote from his statement to the police and from another, made to a psychiatrist two months later, and will supplement these from evidence heard in Court.

I will then give a short account of his life and illness gathered from reports sent in to the solicitors for the defence and from other sources.2 A brief description of his analysis, and of the circumstances in which it was carried out, will take its place chronologically.

responseSet.title: On Determinism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Determinism

Hans Lampl

Hans Lampl

Hans Lampl, Dr.
AMSTERDAM

Hans Lampl  

SUMMARY

I have tried to demonstrate: (i) that the law of probability does not refute the problem of the determinism of psychic phenomena; (ii) that there are, however, psychic phenomena which are unjustly considered by us to be exclusively psychically determined, whose determining factors we have been unable so far to disclose completely. Thus we can only investigate them, for the time being, along the lines of the laws of probability.

<artinfo lang="en" artsubtype="ART" j="IJP" artsource="A1V2" arttype="ART" id="IJP.036.0289A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1955</artyear><artvol actual="36">36</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">289-297</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">Entropy, Organization and the Problem of the Economy of Human Relationships</arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Szasz, Thomas S." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Thomas</nfirst><nmid>S.</nmid><nlast>Szasz</nlast><ndeg dgr="OTHER" other="M.D."/></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln type="name"><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Thomas S. Szasz, M.D.</cs></ln>
<ln type="address">CHICAGO, ILLINOIS</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Entropy, Organization and the Problem of the Economy of Human Relationships
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Entropy, Organization and the Problem of the Economy of Human Relationships

Thomas Szasz M.D.

Thomas Szasz

Thomas S. Szasz, M.D.
CHICAGO, ILLINOIS

Thomas Szasz  

SUMMARY

The purpose of this paper is to examine two related problems. One is the influence of certain physical concepts, particularly that of entropy, on psycho-analysis. The effect of this concept on our views of the economy of human relationships is noted. The second problem is the need for new theoretical concepts regarding the economy of object relationships, based not on notions borrowed from physics, but on the operational method of psycho-analysis.

The concept of entropy has gained renewed popularity in biology, and is being invoked in attempts to clarify the nature of human mental activity and of interpersonal processes. It is

295

noted that while the process of metabolism is characterized by a reciprocal change in the biological complexity of the organism and the ingested foodstuff, respectively, similar directions of change do not always characterize processes leading to psychological growth. In other words, in some types of human interaction, it is possible for both participants to undergo a simultaneous increase in the degree of their psychological organization (here measured in terms of 'learning'). Illustrative of this is the communication between teacher and student or analyst and patient, an interaction that may result in learning for both. Our usual frame of reference, according to which development of one (biological) system occurs at the expense of some other, does not allow for this possibility.

It is generally assumed that an increase in the biological organization of a system is equivalent to a decrease in the entropy of the system. Similarly, increased 'information' of a system is held to be paralleled by a decrease in the entropy of the system. It is shown that use of the concept of entropy in connection with both 'biological organization' and 'psychological organization' is misleading. This conclusion is based partly on a recent paper by Martin J. Klein, who demonstrated on the basis of thermodynamic arguments and reasoning that 'the degree of order in a system which is measured by the entropy (low entropy corresponding to high order) is not the same thing as the degree of organization of the system in the sense used by the biologist.' Psychological observations concerning learning are cited to show that similar conclusions apply to the essential unrelatedness of psychological complexity and entropy.

The problem of the economy of human relationships is briefly considered. The need for new theoretical models, based on a psychology of object relationships, is stressed. The problem is approached by trying to answer the questions, 'Who gives what to whom?' and 'Who gets what from whom?' In other words, the problem of what it is that is being 'exchanged' in a human relationship comes to the centre of our attention. We attempt a provisional explanation along the lines of (i) the intent (wish of the participants in relation to each other), and (ii) the need-satisfactions of the participants as a result of the interaction. This is a very complex problem, principally because the meaning of the total situation of interaction must be understood and evaluated in order to arrive at a relevant inference concerning the 'gains' and 'losses' of the participating persons. Social arrangements are often based on a moral evaluation of the interaction (e.g. by focusing on the officially avowed intentions of one or the other participant). This may colour our psycho-analytic orientation. A scientific (operational) view of the economy of object relationships must be based largely on the assessment of the need-satisfactions of the participants in processes of human interaction.

[[RunningHead]]

International Psycho-Analytical Association

Ruth Eissler

Ruth Eissler

Ruth S. Eissler, Dr., Hon. Secretary
International Psycho-Analytical Association.

Ruth Eissler  

ANNOUNCEMENT

The Celebration by the International Psycho-Analytical Association of the Freud Centenary will take place in London on May 6th, 1956. In the morning a commemorative plaque put up by the London County Council on the Freud House, 20 Maresfield Gardens, will be unveiled. Following this ceremony, a reception at No. 20 Maresfield Gardens will be held by Miss Anna Freud. In the afternoon Dr. Ernest Jones will address a meeting to which all psycho-analysts present in London at the time will be invited and also representatives of scientific societies of which Freud was a member; space permitting the meeting will be open also to the general public.

We have not been informed of the dates of all the celebrations of the Component Societies of the I.P.A. As for those of the American Association and the British and Dutch Societies, dates have been selected that will allow their members to attend also the celebration of the International Association on May 6th.

I am glad to be authorized by the British Society to tell you that analysts attending our celebration of Freud's birthday will be welcome also as guests of theirs which is to take place on the two preceding days.

440

responseSet.title: The Ritual Origin of Shakespeare's 'Titus Andronicus'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ritual Origin of Shakespeare's 'Titus Andronicus'

William Desmonde Ph.D.

William Desmonde

William H. Desmonde, Ph.D.
NEW YORK

William Desmonde  

The survival of ritual forms in literature and in the drama is well known to scholars. Lord Raglan has conjectured that such traditions as those of Robin Hood, the Norse Sagas, Hengist and Horsa, Cuchulainn, and the story of Troy have originated in religious ceremonies (11), and Jessie Weston has offered the hypothesis that the Holy Grail stories originated in Mithraism (16). It has been shown by Dieterich (3), Ridgeway (13), Jane Harrison (6), Gilbert Murray (10), and Cornford (2) that theatrical forms are traceable to the religious rituals of ancient Greece.

G. Wilson Knight has suggested that Shakespearian tragedy is essentially a re-working of ancient religious ceremonies connected with the sacrifice of the mass. Shakespeare's kings, he states, are to be compared with the ritual kings of pagan cultures (7). This essay seeks to show the existence of a ritual pattern in the tragedy Titus Andronicus.

In a recent article on the Eleusinian Mysteries1 I discussed the psycho-analytic significance of primitive puberty rites. They represent, according to the hypothesis offered, a social institution devised, no doubt over a long period of time, by the older men. The purpose of this institution was both to rid the adolescents of their oedipal ambivalence, freeing their energies for higher cultural activities, and to provide an outlet for the hostility of the tribal fathers against their sons.

The essential feature of these rituals consists of being shown the tribal mysteries, dramatic portrayals of the killing and resurrection of the neophyte. Originally, according to Freud's hypothesis, the separation of the son from the mother was carried out by the killing, castrating, or driving out of the male offspring. Frequently, puberty rituals for girls consist of a defloration ceremony.

In the course of social development, these mysteries often survive under the direction of organizations of priests, who conduct the dramatic-magical rites of the group. The religious mysteries of ancient Greece arose from the barbaric rituals of the primitive Aegean, according to Webster (15), Andrew Lang (8), Van Gennep (14), and Jane Harrison (6); and it is well known that Greek ritual had a great influence upon the Roman drama, from which Shakespeare derived many of his plots.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953: Anna Freud, LL.D.  'Some Remarks on Infant Observation', pp. 9–19.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Anna Freud, LL.D. 'Some Remarks on Infant Observation', pp. 9–19.

This account of the observations it is possible to make on an infant in the first year of its life and the significance to be attached to them was delivered as an address to the first-year medical students in Cleveland, Ohio, the first group to receive their training under the new medical curriculum instituted at Western Reserve University in 1952. These students were to begin their medical education not in the dissecting room but in the antenatal clinic, initiating a contact with the expectant mother, which it was planned they should carry through the various stages of pregnancy, confinement, and postnatal care, and which should continue for the term of their medical studies.

In the simple and sensitive description of observable phenomena the analyst will readily recognize that the students are being introduced to the earliest manifestations of the operation of instinctual forces, to processes leading from part to whole object relationship, and to the differentiation of the body self. The account covers the functioning of the primitive pleasure ego and the earliest developments of reality testing. Needless to say, the complete absence of analytic terminology proves entirely compatible with the presentation of an exact description of early developmental processes.

75

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Rose Coleman, M.D., Ernst Kris, Ph.D., and Sally Provence, M.D. 'The Study of Variations of Parental Attitudes. A Preliminary Report', pp. 20–47.

This report from the Child Study Centre of the Yale University School of Medicine is based on the work of a research service which offers healthy and sick baby care to mothers chosen in the early months of pregnancy to participate in the project. The service covers all the vicissitudes of antenatal and postnatal care as well as the provision of nursery education. The present stage of the investigation focuses on the changes which can take place in a mother's attitude to a single child at different stages of its growth. The investigators found they were able to predict certain of these changes in advance, and are now aiming to assess the range of predictability which this type of study may make possible.

Four cases are presented in detail. In two of them marked improvement in maternal attitude was apparent and in two deterioration set in, changes in all cases being coincident with the child's initial strivings for independence. In both sets of cases, the mothers identified with their children on the basis of archaic id aspects of their personalities. The authors draw attention to the fact that when identification has become autonomous, and its energies neutralized within the ego, it opens the way for understanding of the object, and becomes a part of object relationship. Much of the ability of the mother to handle the small child and much of the difficulty in adjusting to its growth depends on her own capacity to make the shift from one type of identification to the other.

75
