responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953: Margaret Fries, M.D., and Paul J. Woolf, M.S.  'Some Hypotheses on the Role of the Congenital Activity Type in Personality Development', pp. 48–62.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Margaret Fries, M.D., and Paul J. Woolf, M.S. 'Some Hypotheses on the Role of the Congenital Activity Type in Personality Development', pp. 48–62.

The Congenital Activity Type, a descriptive term referring to the amount of activity a new-born infant shows in response to certain stimuli, is a concept covering neuromuscular excitability due in part to inherited, intra-uterine, and birth factors, and in part to temporary body changes and emotional states as well as to emotional attitudes of the parents. Grouping is into five types, ranging from pathological extremes of hyper- and hypo-activity through active, and moderately active, to quiet types. The impact of environmental experiences occurring before the differentiation of the ego from the id gains importance because the effect is as lifelong as though they were constitutional factors. The Congenital Activity Type therefore ranks in the authors' view as one of the primal ego variations to which Freud referred in 'Analysis Terminable and Interminable.'

On the basis of extensive observation of cases in analytic treatment and direct observation of some two hundred newborn babies, the authors have explored the role of the Congenital Activity Type in determining the set of the parent-child relationship; as a factor in psychosexual development, e.g. in the choice of areas of libidinization; in its influence on the child's ways of testing reality and his form of mastery over his environment; in the choice of defence mechanisms; and finally in the choice of symptom and the predisposition to pathology. While every condition is overdetermined, the conclusion is reached that the Congenital Activity Type is one of the many aetiological factors in personality development.

75

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953: Christine Olden.  'On Adult Empathy with Children', pp. 111–126.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Christine Olden. 'On Adult Empathy with Children', pp. 111–126.

Empathy is a process oscillating between feeling as the object feels and recapturing the standpoint of one's own ego as observer and judge. Empathy with the infant and the child in the latency period

76

calls for a capacity to regress with the child's ego to manifestations of the primary process or the first stages of sublimation. It is a process which is independent of intellectual understanding and of liking or not liking. It has the capacity to pass through the object's defences to the real state of emotional affairs. It is to be distinguished, however, from various forms of regressive interplay with children originating in a narcissistic disturbance in the adult. Examples from the author's work with parents and teachers illustrate the confusion to which some pseudo- or partial-empathy subjects children when it is used for adult instinct-gratification or as a defence against adult anxieties, immediate or archaic. Equally the child's proclivity for living partly in the primary process may be felt as a threat to the mature psychic acquisitions of some adults. The adult's ability to feel with the child corresponds to the degree to which he has assimilated and been reconciled with his own childhood experiences and his own relation with his parents.

77

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953: Victor Rosen, M.D.  'On Mathematical "Illumination" and the Mathematical Thought Process.  A Contribution to the Genetic Development and Metapsychology of Abstract Thinking', pp. 127–154.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Victor Rosen, M.D. 'On Mathematical "Illumination" and the Mathematical Thought Process. A Contribution to the Genetic Development and Metapsychology of Abstract Thinking', pp. 127–154.

A child's ability to operate reliably with numerical concepts appears in connection with certain stages in the maturation of the perceptual apparatus during the oedipal period. The ability to exclude purely sensory aspects of data in favour of their quantitative value seems decisive. The process is therefore one of selective countercathexis. Observations of hypersensitive infants with a low threshold of the stimulus barrier suggest that the need to erect secondary protective barriers against excessive external stimulation may result in the precocious maturation of some ego functions. It is suggested that these secondary barriers are precursors of the process of selective countercathexis and by their means an ego process may become available precociously for the development of quantitative conceptualization. In those with a special mathematical gift, it is probable that this maturational sequence takes place at an earlier period of ego development, so that along with the precocious concepts of number there remain archaic defence mechanisms which are later utilized in the creative aspects of the process.

In the case reported, a young mathematician experienced spontaneous mathematical insight while undergoing analysis, in connection with repressed feelings of disappointment and rage around objects with which he had previously had positive libidinal ties. The patient dealt with the disturbing, real situation by negation. The creative act, involving momentary 'controlled regression' (Kris, 1950) to the preconscious level, dealt with the repressed archaic conflict by a continuous substitution of symbols for images. By confronting himself at this juncture with a world which was internally consistent within the framework of pure mathematics, the patient was enabled to avoid facing what was inner and what was outer reality. The conflict was resolved in a satisfying aesthetic experience. In this way the mathematician avoids the ambivalent conflict inherent in all other sciences which deal with external objects. The material indicates the substitution of mathematical thinking for aggressive feelings. The author concludes: 'The sublimatory fixing of the whole process is ensured by the high degree of "neutralization" of both libidinal and aggressive drives. Thus the creative act in mathematics ("illumination") has its counterpart in the arts while the secondary elaborative process is continuous with those of the sciences.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953: Samuel Ritvo, M.D., and Sally Provence, M.D.  'Form Perception and Imitation in Some Autistic Children:  Diagnostic Findings and their Contextual Interpretation', pp. 155–161.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Samuel Ritvo, M.D., and Sally Provence, M.D. 'Form Perception and Imitation in Some Autistic Children: Diagnostic Findings and their Contextual Interpretation', pp. 155–161.

Six children were studied, ranging in age from 22 to 39 months. All belonged to the autistic or symbiotic types of deviant (Mahler), and showed gross disturbance in human contact and absence or near-absence of speech, with general features which had led to a diagnosis of mental defect. Two features of their test performance are used as corroborative evidence for Mahler's formulations and for advancing suggestions for early diagnostic criteria of the symbiotic type.

All children of both types achieved age-adequate or superior performance on form board items. All moreover showed poor imitation of the examiner in tests where the imitation of drawings, vocal sounds, and movements was a feature. The poor imitation of these children is said to reflect the tenuous nature of their object relations, and the insufficient differentiation of the self from the not-self. In imitation the transformation of the self is carried out according to an image of the object and appears to be a part process of identification. Mahler stresses that these children have difficulty in cathecting the periphery of the organism with consequent defect in the body image and difficulty in establishing ego boundaries. The high performance on the form board presupposes a strong interest in and ability to perceive boundaries of shapes experienced through the hands, an aptitude which, it is suggested, stems from the forcing value of anxiety in their special area of conflict.

Early diagnosis of the symbiotic child might find some help in the study of nascent identificatory processes as manifested in the earliest imitative elements. It would seem likely that symbiotic cases would show disturbances in this area.

77

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Sara Kut. 'The Changing Pattern of Transference in the Analysis of an 11-year-old Girl', pp. 355–378.

In her paper entitled 'Clinical Notes on the Nature of Transference in Child Analysis' (Study of the Child, vol. 6) Selma Freiberg concludes that the term 'transference neurosis' cannot be applied to the analysis of children and adolescents, because 'the original neurosis is still centred to a large extent on the sphere of the home and the infantile objects'. In contrast, this paper attempts to show that the transference in child analysis does indeed exist in different forms and can, at certain times, assume the appearance of a transference neurosis. Three stages of the transference are described against the background of the libidinal development of the girl.

In the first stage the libidinal cathexis remained at home, and the transference paved the way towards the acceptance of aggressive and hostile wishes and thoughts directed towards members of the family. It was in the second stage that one could speak of a transference neurosis, because the regressed conflicts, once they had been worked through in relation to the original objects, were repeated in the analysis with the therapist as the central object. They were resolved after they had been interpreted and worked through. In the third stage the transference shifted once again, and now 'contemporary' and current conflicts were simultaneously worked through in many different places in relation to the different objects at home, at school, and with friends, as well as at the clinic.

80

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 8, 1953

Géza Róheim, Ph.D. 'Fairy Tale and Dream', pp. 394–403.

In this posthumous publication two fairy stories, Little Red Riding Hood and the Tale of Fearless John, are analysed in terms of a new view of mythology and the folk tale. 'It seems that dreams and myths are not merely similar but that a large part of mythology is actually derived from dreams. In other words we can not only apply the standard technique of dream interpretation in analysing a fairy tale but actually can think of tales and myths as having arisen from a dream which a person dreamed and then told to others, who retold it again, perhaps elaborated in accord with their own dreams.

80

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Scientific Study of Personality

By H. J. Eysenck. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1952. Pp. xiv + 320. 30 s.)

J. Flugel

J. Flugel

J. Flugel  

This book has come somewhat late into the reviewer's hands, late, that is, especially in relation to the author's energy and creativity, which have already enabled him to produce two volumes since the present one appeared. As is now well known, Dr. Eysenck is a firm believer in the strictly 'scientific' approach as the only one that will ensure real progress in psychology and psychiatry; and in the field of personality this approach, in his view, is best served by the administration of simple objective tests giving definitely quantitative measures which can be treated by factor-analytical methods so that personality can eventually be described in terms of a limited number of 'dimensions'. In this approach, as he envisages it, there is no use for procedures which involve a large element of subjectivity or the accumulation of material which is difficult or impossible to put into quantitative form. Indeed, such half measures as projection tests, questionnaires, and ratings are regarded with almost as much suspicion as are psycho-analytic sessions or other forms of psychiatric interview.

Psycho-analysts will no doubt continue to feel that this insistence on rigid rules of experimentation and quantification is only too likely to blind the investigator to important aspects of the human mind which in the present state of our knowledge do not readily lend themselves to such procedures; and in this they can find support in the fact that the admittedly less rigid and more intuitive methods of psycho-analysis enriched psychology with a long series of working concepts (since proved of value in illuminating many other fields) which 'brass instrument psychology', for all its attempt at exactitude, had completely failed even to adumbrate. Dr. Eysenck remains unimpressed, and retaliates by pointing out that even after half a century of practice by psycho-analysts and other psychotherapists there is no clear proof that on the therapeutic side any measurable success has been achieved. Even as regards 'experimental psycho-analysis', or 'experimental psychodynamics' as some prefer to call it, in which attempts are being made to use psycho-analytic findings in such a way that they can be experimentally confirmed, refuted, amended or enlarged—a field where Dr. Eysenck might perhaps have been expected to exercise greater tolerance or even to exhibit some approval—he tends to remain sceptical, appearing to hold that the inspiration derived from psycho-analytic sources and the apparent confirmation of psycho-analytic findings in a good many cases are of doubtful value. Thus in his appraisal of Dr. Goldman-Eisler's work on oral character traits, which he admits is good of its kind and which appears at first sight to be a remarkable vindication (within limits) of Abraham's and Glover's clinically derived views, he points out that the psycho-analytic explanation in terms of a causal relation between early weaning and certain character qualities may be capable of an alternative interpretation in terms of hereditary factors, in the sense that introverted mothers may tend both to have introverted offspring and to wean them earlier than their more extravert sisters. Such a view seems indeed to be much less likely to be true than the psycho-analytic—environmentalist one (especially when we take into consideration the apparently confirmatory evidence from anthropology and from experiments with animals), but it has the merit of being: (a) possibly correct; (b) capable of proof or disproof by further appropriate investigation. The more obvious interpretation and the one which is most in harmony with already known facts is not always the correct one: and this exemplifies the value of Dr. Eysenck's sceptical attitude, even though it may appear strained and unappreciative to those who think primarily along psycho-analytic lines.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly: Robert Fliess.  'The Hypnotic Evasion:  A Clinical Observation.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

Robert Fliess. 'The Hypnotic Evasion: A Clinical Observation.'

Fliess believes that certain patients exploit the residual hypnotic characteristics of the psycho-analytic situation as a resistance. The patients he describes were all marked in ordinary life not only by a tendency to hypnotic responses but by an intense unconscious need for punishment. When they used this escape from analysis, sometimes very transitorily, it was into the first degree of hypnosis—a state often difficult to detect but accompanied by an experience of lassitude. This evasion was designed to counteract the mobilization of forbidden impulses by paralysis, and its detection was on occasion indispensable to their successful analysis. The patients' reaction to the analyst as hypnotist proves to be the ego's reaction to the superego projected upon the analyst: its metapsychology and relation to the archaic past of the species are discussed.

219

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly: Joel J. Friedman.  'Psychology of the Audience in Relation to the Architecture of the Theater.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

Joel J. Friedman. 'Psychology of the Audience in Relation to the Architecture of the Theater.'

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

Friedman links the increasing separation from Greek times of the actors from the audience, and the architectural developments of the theatre which accompanied it, to the increasing repression of the themes of incest and parricide which dominated Greek tragedy. This increasing repression involved the increasing individuation of the problems enacted and therefore the necessity for an increasing realism of technique. Thus the theatre of convention came to be replaced by the present theatre of illusion. The watching by a modern audience of the outside world from the darkness of the mother's womb involves a fundamental shift in the method of alleviating basic anxieties.

220

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association: Stanley L. Olinick.  'Some Considerations on the Use of Questioning a Psychoanalytic Technique.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Stanley L. Olinick. 'Some Considerations on the Use of Questioning a Psychoanalytic Technique.'

Questioning, though a basic therapeutic tool, has been taken for granted and given little attention as a subject for study. The judicious use of questioning may facilitate the analyst's function of free floating attention. A question may be less harmful than a blunt statement. The former avoids the definiteness and dogmatism of the latter, and suggests the possibility of a joint exploratory effort. Systematic enquiry may be regarded as a parameter or deviation from the basic model of technique (Eissler). Questioning may interfere with the transference, and this effect may not be abolished by interpretation and working through. Under ordinary circumstances questioning is self-eliminating once a relevant answer has been obtained. Extensive questioning is often due to counter-transference influences. Questioning may be resented, feared, and resisted by the patient, or it will be welcomed. The reaction will depend upon the predominant psychical constellations. Questioning is indicated in the following instances: (1) inarticulate borderline neurotics; (2) the patient in an acute state of anxiety; (3) enquiry into obsessional data; (4) with the psychotic patient, particularly the schizophrenic; (5) in the interpretation of resistance. The author takes pains to point out that questioning is only a preliminary manoeuvre and a preparation for interpretation and subsequent working through.

221

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association: 2, 1954, No. 2
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

2, 1954, No. 2

Ralph R. Greenson. 'The Struggle Against Identification.'

This paper begins by differentiating between the concepts of incorporation, introjection, and identification. While incorporation is an oral instinctual activity which has as its aim the taking in by the mouth of a piece of the external world, introjection is the psychic phenomenon parallel to incorporation and is modelled after it. Identification is more complex than the other two. Introjection is the principal mechanism leading to identification. Introjection is an instinctual aim towards an object, while identification is a process which results after introjection has taken place. Identification influences more than the ego of the subject, it involves the entire self. The term 'self' is used here as distinct from the term 'ego'. There appear to be many different kinds of identification—e.g. total and partial. Four patients are described who introjected and identified with a hated parental figure. They tried to remain unaware of this identification. They attempted to deny any resemblance to the hated parent in behaviour or thought. Simultaneously there was a need to find new objects. This was very apparent in the transference situation. The patients were conscious of a hatred towards the parent of the same sex while the parent of the opposite sex was idealized. Throughout their lives the patients repeated behaviour more appropriate to the hated parent. Experience in no way altered this pattern. These patients were prone to disturbances of mood which were essentially due to a reactivation of old identifications. The men did not merely become angry; they were their angry fathers. They were also given to bouts of 'acting out' when they unconsciously repeated the behaviour of the consciously hated parent.

The author reviews the developmental history of the superego and points out that before the superego proper is established introjections and identifications have occurred. He refers to the presence of 'good' and 'bad' introjects which eventually fuse into a composite introject. This fusion occurs during the anal-retentive phase of libidinal development. The ego similarly demonstrates a capacity to fuse 'good' and 'bad' internal object representations. In infancy introjection and identification cannot be separated because perception implies a transformation of the self (primary identification). As the ego matures perception and introjection no longer lead automatically to identification. The author suggests that the reason why the patients struggled against an awareness of their identification with the hated internalized parent is to be found in the primitive and regressive nature of the introjection. The patients intuitively felt the oral-sadistic nature of these early introjections. The view is advanced that there is a hierarchy of introjections, both self and object representations, which begin in infancy and continue throughout life. The earlier introjections are characterized by oralsadistic trends, while the later introjections arise from less primitive sources.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Journal of Mental Science

100, 1954, No. 418

W. H.

W. H.

W. H.  

Ernest Jones. 'The Early History of Psycho-Analysis.'

This paper was read as a Maudsley Bequest Lecture before the Royal Medico-Psychological Society. Its personal, in part autobiographical approach does not lend itself easily to the formal process of abstracting and it will more profitably be read in extenso. From the little anecdotes starting with an experience during the author's internship and concluding with his rise to ascendancy in 'the psycho-analytic movement,' the reader will learn many facts, unknown or forgotten, about the friendly and adverse relationships between psychiatry and psycho-analysis. An evolutionist throughout, the author struggles hard to make his audience see the emergence of psycho-analysis in psychiatry in true historical perspective, quoting, for instance, Hughlings Jackson, who said: 'If you want to find out about insanity you must first find out about dreams.

224

responseSet.title: The Troubled Mind: By Beulah C. Bosselman.  (New York:  Ronald Press, 1953.  Pp. 206.  $3.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Troubled Mind

By Beulah C. Bosselman. (New York: Ronald Press, 1953. Pp. 206. $3.50.)

J. Jacobs

J. Jacobs

J. Jacobs  

This book presents to the lay reader an account of the disorders of personality, their origin and treatment.

The work falls into three parts. The first deals with emotional development from birth to maturity, and with the further adjustments thereafter until old age is reached. The second, clinical, part is devoted to accounts of the various forms of emotional maladjustment, neuroses, psychosomatic disorders and psychoses. In the third and final part the problems of treatment are discussed.

The book is a readable and succinct exposition of its subject. In the clinical section added interest is provided by a number of illuminating cases which are briefly described, though it is felt that these could have been fuller, as could also the sections dealing with the psychiatric disorders of the pre-adult years. In the chapter on treatment little mention is made of the very considerable amount of work being done in the psycho-analytic treatment of the psychoses, and the reader is left with the impression that physical methods of treatment (insulin coma and electro-shock) are the only ones available in these conditions.

There is a useful bibliography which might have been expanded with advantage.

361

(Cambridge, Mass.:  For the Commonwealth Fund by Harvard University Press; London:  Geoffrey Cumberlege, 1954, Pp. 619.  $8.50 or 68 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Studies in Schizophrenia. A Multidisciplinary Approach to Mind-Brain Relationships

By the Tulane Department of Psychiatry and Neurology. Reported by Robert G. Heath. (Cambridge, Mass.: For the Commonwealth Fund by Harvard University Press; London: Geoffrey Cumberlege, 1954, Pp. 619. $8.50 or 68 s.)

Robert Corday

Robert Corday

Robert Corday  

This is an ambitious work which attempts to attack the problem of schizophrenia and its treatment in toto by physiological means. All medical disciplines, from surgery to psycho-analysis, combine to pool their resources.

The authors were led to believe that the septal region of the forebrain was the influencing area in schizophrenia and severe emotional disease. The book contains a thorough documentation based on electrode stimulation, with extensive studies on animal and human subjects, complete with laboratory data. Briefly, they think that certain pathways constitute a hypothetical circuit that facilitates mental and bodily processes, while others suppress activity. It must be remembered that the report is a preliminary one.

The authors at first accept psycho-analytic psychology as the psychology of mental processes, and then ignore it, especially in evaluating their results. In a series of twenty patients, eleven were described as improved where a control series would only produce four. In the case histories offered, there seems to have been a remission in libidinal regression, the more normal part of the ego seems to have a tenuous hold, but the modified psychotic ego is in evidence. The improved patients live a marginal existence.

Psycho-analytic literature has described the modified, deformed ego of the psychotic. Although 'social cures' are very important, it would have been of inestimable value if the authors had described the superiority of their method over others in terms of structural change that had occurred.

400

responseSet.title: Adrenal Cortex: Transactions of the Fifth Conference, No. 4, 5 and 6, 1953.  Edited by Elaine P. Ralli.  (New York:  Josiah Macy Jr. Foundation, 1954.  Pp. 187.  $3.75.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adrenal Cortex

Transactions of the Fifth Conference, No. 4, 5 and 6, 1953. Edited by Elaine P. Ralli. (New York: Josiah Macy Jr. Foundation, 1954. Pp. 187. $3.75.)

S. Lucas

S. Lucas

S. Lucas  

This record of the fifth and final conference on the adrenal cortex completes a series of informal discussions by a limited group of investigators and guests. All five volumes are published at cost, and are designed for the advanced technical reader.

The recent brilliant discovery of Electrocortin (now known as Aldosterone) by Reichenstein and his associates is discussed by Dr. H. L. Mason who had, interestingly enough, crystallized the same compound. Electrocortin is a mineralo-corticoid found in the human adrenal cortex which has an activity of from 50 to 100 times that of desoxycorticosterone and its discovery is an important advance in adrenal physiology.

There are discussions on the salt and water factor of the adrenal cortex, the metabolism of adrenal steroids, and on whether ACTH is a single substance or a mixture of hormones.

There are references to psychologically disturbed patients. On p. 60 Dr. Thorn describes an alteration in the normal diurnal variation of steroid secretion in severe psychological stress. On pp. 61 and 62, Dr. Pincus describes work on psycho-neurotics who show no diurnal variations, even though they sleep well, and states 'the psychiatrists suggest that these are people who have rather interesting dreams.'

On p. 63, Dr. Dorfman describes increases in steroids following electroshock treatment in a 24-hour period.

No psychiatric data are given in these cases and this could not be expected at a conference of this kind. The psycho-analyst would certainly be very interested in further informal discussions which had as their aim the correlation of psychological data with physiological findings and comparative studies of such cases.

403

responseSet.title: Olive Schreiner. A Study in Latent Meanings: By Marion V. Friedmann.  (Johannesburg:  Witwatersrand University Press, 1954.  Pp. 68.  8 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Olive Schreiner. A Study in Latent Meanings

By Marion V. Friedmann. (Johannesburg: Witwatersrand University Press, 1954. Pp. 68. 8 s. 6 d.)

Barbara Low

Barbara Low

Barbara Low  

To those still alive who were young (or youngish) men and women in 1883, when The Story of an African Farm was first published, the impact of that remarkable book will probably never fade from memory. It opened up an entirely new vista in regard to background, types of character presented, and its passionate atmosphere.

Some of those early readers will be glad, I think, that the centenary of her birth (24 March, 1854) has been celebrated by the B.B.C. and by a crowded and successful meeting of the literary club, P.E.N., and by a new book published 24 March, 1954, by Daisy Hobman, containing much research and new light on the strange and tragic character of Olive Schreiner.

The small book here reviewed is written with insight, much sympathy and restraint. The author, obviously, has made some study of psycho-analytic theory, and in her section entitled 'Latent Meanings', after a sketch of Olive Schreiner's childhood and girlhood and the beginning of her writing career, she goes on to apply some of her psycho-analytical knowledge.

408

responseSet.title: Colour and the Human Soul: By Gladys Mayer.  (London:  New Knowledge Books, 1954.  Pp. 33.  2 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Colour and the Human Soul

By Gladys Mayer. (London: New Knowledge Books, 1954. Pp. 33. 2 s. 6 d.)

M. Dale

M. Dale

M. Dale  

This little book is one of a series called 'New Knowledge Books'. It contains an elaboration and interpretation of Rudolf Steiner's teachings and doctrines. A nomenclature is applied which is taken neither from everyday language nor from any other coherent terminology. The words 'soul' and 'spirit' seem to be code terms for emotions

409

and intelligence and consciousness respectively. The reader is presented with a dithyrambic adoration of colour, with symbolic comparisons, i.e. 'body breathes air, soul breathes colour', with semi-scientific thinking, with allegorical and animistic thoughts. With a crescendo of emotions the author describes an inner experience resembling that of an ecstasy in a spiritual seance. All ideas, feelings, and sensations merge into one; soul and colour then give way to Light and Darkness, and the little book ends with words from the Gospel of St. John.

410

responseSet.title: The Psychology of the Criminal Act and Punishment: By Gregory Zilboorg, M.D.  (New York:  Harcourt, Brace, 1954.  Pp. 141.  $3.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychology of the Criminal Act and Punishment

By Gregory Zilboorg, M.D. (New York: Harcourt, Brace, 1954. Pp. 141. $3.50.)

W. Joffe

W. Joffe

W. Joffe  

In writing this book the author had two main objectives: (1) To emphasize that in matters of law and psychiatry it is not enough to be correct or accurate, it is mandatory to fuse our legal and psychiatric functions with a morality that is practised and not merely given recognition; (2) to make the reader aware of the immensity of the problems involved in the conflict that exists between law and psychiatry. He contends that this conflict prevails because of the existence of an eternal struggle between the striving to punish and the yearning to understand and forgive within the same community of men, and more often than not, within the same person. The book largely deals with this theme as it operates in the framing of the law, in the dispensation of justice in court and in the implementation of punishment. He considers that the true and effective aim of punishment is to awaken, not stifle, the will to resocialization or reintegration or the reincorporation of social standards in the criminal. Using interesting case material he illustrates the discrepancy between this aim and the purpose punitive law achieves in its present form. The book presents an eloquent appeal for the greater utilization of psychological insight in the future, in order to create a rational penological system.

Dr. Zillboorg has a vast knowledge of his subject and has put a great deal of thought and moral indignation into the writing of this book. If one is to be critical at all, it would be about the fact that although he implicitly makes use of structural concepts, he tends to neglect them in his final assessment of the phenomena he describes. An illustrative example of this can be found in the chapter entitled 'Suggestions about Psychiatry and Psychiatrists' where he cogently and critically demonstrates that the court room procedure creates an atmosphere conducive to the generation of hostility between judge and psychiatric witness, prosecutor and defending counsel, one psychiatric witness and another—an atmosphere which does not permit objective psychiatric testimony. He then says, 'I hope no one will be misled by the apparent harshness of my critical attitude. It is not that the judge, the lawyers, the psychiatrists, erring and non-erring, are guilty of not doing the right thing. Rather, we are dealing here with a stubborn psychology of human reactions against the evildoers of one's own tribe. The stubbornness of this psychology is deeply rooted in the primitive layers of our poorly domesticated primitive instincts, and it is difficult for us to be different.

responseSet.title: The Six Schizophrenias. Reaction Patterns in Children and Adults: By Samuel J. Beck, Ph.D.  (New York:  American Orthopsychiatric Association Inc., 1954.  Pp. xii + 238.  $5.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Six Schizophrenias. Reaction Patterns in Children and Adults

By Samuel J. Beck, Ph.D. (New York: American Orthopsychiatric Association Inc., 1954. Pp. xii + 238. $5.00.)

W. Ronald Fairbairn

W. Ronald Fairbairn

W. Ronald Fairbairn  

This book is Research Monograph No. 6 of the American Orthopsychiatric Association; and it describes the nature and results of a joint study of schizophrenia in terms of the three disciplines of psychology, psychiatry, and social study. The actual material studied was that provided by two groups of patients referred to the Michael Reese

414

Hospital, Chicago, and considered on clinical grounds to display evidence of schizophrenic involvement—viz. a group of children and one of adults. The contribution of the psychiatrists was to supply, in the case of each patient, numerical ratings on a scale of 1–11 for 120 selected traits regarded as constituting 'a trait universe'. These traits were selected on the basis of clinical observation interpreted psycho-analytically in terms of (a) defences, (b) ego-functions, (c) emotions, and (d) restitutive forces. The contribution of the psychologists was to provide a Rorschach assessment for each patient in terms of clusters of significant traits and significant combinations of such clusters; and it is interesting to note that among the clusters isolated were (1) preoccupation with genitality and bodily functions, (2) perceptual inaccuracy with low mental drive, (3) withdrawal from the outer world, (4) indifference to familiar and conventional stimuli, and (5) emotional indifference. The data obtained by the psychiatric (clinical) and psychological (experimental) investigations were then correlated—a task rendered easier by the fact that the psychiatric approach pivoted upon an ego-psychology and the Rorschach approach upon the theory of an ego-affect balance. Attention was thus primarily concentrated upon the functioning of the ego. The statistical analysis to which the Rorschach results were subjected was effected by means of Stevenson's Q-technique, which possesses the advantage that it does not depend upon the establishment of norms derived from the study of large samples of the population, but is capable of yielding valid inferences from the study of individual cases. Indeed it is characteristic of the whole investigation that, in spite of its statistical nature, it is focused essentially upon the study of the individual. As a result of the investigation six types of schizophrenia were delineated. Three of these were identified by the Rorschach tests alone and could not be discriminated clinically. Two types occurred only in children and were interpreted as representing transitional states; and two others were found to represent advanced states with a poor prognosis. No attempt was made to relate the six types isolated to the fourfold Kraepelinian classification. Nor was any attempt made to determine the essential nature of schizophrenia; for, since the concept of schizophrenia adopted was purely 'transactional', it was considered sufficient to describe the schizophrenic person by 'operational trait data', and to submit hypotheses regarding the dynamic organization of traits into behaviour identifiable as 'schizophrenic'. Nor yet was any attempt made to establish any broad aetiological generalizations. At the same time, the contribution of social study to the investigation (based, like the psychiatric contribution, upon the assessment of selected traits in a 'trait universe') was to isolate such environmental patterns as appeared to have aetiological significance. Four such patterns were found to emerge—patterns characterized respectively by (1) rejection on the part of parents, (2) parental inconsistency, both spatial and relational, (3) parental indifference and inadequacy, and (4) parental over-protectiveness. Apart from the delineation of schizophrenic types, with which the investigation was primarily concerned, some observations of interest are recorded. Thus, whilst the presence of a schizophrenic pattern in childhood is not regarded as necessarily a cause for pessimism, the formulation 'once schizophrenic, always schizophrenic' is considered justified; and among other such general formulations are—'Schizophrenia is never an all or none condition', and 'We are all capable of some schizophrenia. Our dreams so show.' So far as clinical psychiatry is concerned, it seems doubtful whether the classification of types of schizophrenia resulting from the investigation described in this book will be regarded as an adequate substitute for the Kraepelinian classification. However, the aim of the investigation was not a clinical classification, but one orientated towards prognosis and the therapeutic possibilities. In general, psychopathological studies conducted on a statistical basis find little favour among psycho-analysts; but the present investigation can hardly fail to be of interest to psychoanalysts in that it describes a method, mediated by the Q-technique, whereby valid theoretical conclusions may be drawn from the intensive study of individual cases. It thus describes a method which offers some prospect of being satisfactorily applied to the testing of the validity of psycho-analytical concepts on a statistical basis.

415

Robert Blank.  'Depression, Hypomania and Depersonalisation.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1: H. Robert Blank.  'Depression, Hypomania and Depersonalisation.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1

H. Robert Blank. 'Depression, Hypomania and Depersonalisation.'

The author tries to interpret these phenomena from his study of a case as related elements in a pattern of defence.

The patient's major attacks of depersonalization were always preceded by hypomanic episodes. They may therefore be regarded as an emergency defence after failure of the hypomanic defence. Since minor episodes of depersonalization occurred without anxiety, while in the major attacks the anxiety developed as the feelings of unreality increased, it is likely that the depersonalization was a defence against anxiety. This anxiety would be most simply explicable as the precipitating anxiety of the attack. Such anxiety could, however, also be regarded as due to the threat of disintegration to the ego implicit in feelings of unreality. Perhaps this second view would be correct where depersonalization precedes schizophrenia.

The author goes on to consider the relationship of depersonalization to confusion, and to the phenomena of normal sleep.

421

'The Discriminating Function of the Ego.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1: Louis Linn.  'The Discriminating Function of the Ego.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 1

Louis Linn. 'The Discriminating Function of the Ego.'

Perceptual stimuli may be regarded as relatively structured or unstructured according to the complexity of conceptualization which they evoke. But in any conceptualization there is always an element of ambiguity. In effect one perceives not an object but a cluster of related ideas. Because the elements of a cluster vary in importance according to its context, the ego must scan the cluster for the most pertinent meanings. This implies that the ego discriminates reality in two stages.

The author confirms his description of the process by means of examples of its breakdown in patients with brain injuries. He also shows that the mechanisms of the primary process obtrude themselves into the faculty evaluations of reality which follow.

Although the scanning process must operate in the preconscious its effective reverberations must affect the unconscious. Since experimentally a visual stimulus is more quickly recognized if it corresponds to a wish, the author puts forward the hypothesis that the wished-for elements in a cluster are scanned first.

He then uses this theory to explain the phenomenon of prägnanz or 'closure' in Gestalt psychology, and conjectures that the wish on these occasions is the product of the conflict-free sphere of the ego.

421

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 3: Alfred J. Siegman.  'Emotionality—A Hysterical Character Defence.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 3

Alfred J. Siegman. 'Emotionality—A Hysterical Character Defence.'

Hysterical affects make a qualitatively different impression from other affects. Siegman believes this to be due to their defensive utilization by the ego. Among the defensive operations he notes (1) the offer of 'appropriate' emotions to the super-ego; (2) their use to establish an apparently convincing link with an impulse while the real affect remains unconscious; (3) the smuggling in of hidden libidinal gratifications; (4) the secondary eroticization of the defensive affects; (5) the active seeking out of stimuli by means of which the neurotic gratifications involved in the defence can be repeated. Parallels may be drawn with the obsessional neurosis and moral masochism.

The loud clamour of shallow emotions reminds one of children in the phallic phase hypocritically protesting 'appropriate' emotions to curry favour with their parents. This suggests a fixation at the period when the super-ego has been modified in its original severity but not yet firmly internalized. Fixation at this stage may help to account for the suggestibility of hysterics.

424

Carroll.  'Acting Out and Ego Development.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4: Edward J. Carroll.  'Acting Out and Ego Development.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4

Edward J. Carroll. 'Acting Out and Ego Development.'

The paper deals with acting out in the sense of repetitive compulsive gratification of unconscious impulses as seen in impulse neuroses rather than with the acting out which begins in therapy as a regressive defence. Like Greenacre, Carroll relates it to disturbances in the second year and emphasizes the failure to develop adequate means of linguistic communication.

This failure he attributes to participation in acts of gross duplicity on the part of the parents or guardians, so that the real facts have seemed to the child to differ from what was said about them. An example would be the passive witnessing of sexual scenes in which a nurse is involved and the child rewarded for secrecy. As a result orientation to reality through action and through speech develop independently of one another. The failure to assimilate language as a concise method of conceptualization means that whole dramatic sequences have to be acted out instead of details being selected for symbolic representation. Related to this there is a chasm between reality and fantasy, which cannot be integrated with reality by such means as artistic creativity.

Treatment requires an extensive preliminary period of ego education. Considerable tolerance must be given to the patient's motor behaviour, and the relevance of verbal communication must be constantly checked against it. Standard analytic procedure can only be introduced when the acting out tends to be replaced by psychosomatic anxiety equivalents and verbal communication.

425

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3: Robert A. Savitt.  'Extramural Psychoanalytic Treatment of a Case of Narcotic Addiction.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 2, 1954, No. 3

Robert A. Savitt. 'Extramural Psychoanalytic Treatment of a Case of Narcotic Addiction.'

A nineteen-year-old male patient, addicted to marijuana and heroin, was under analysis for thirty-eight months. A tolerant attitude was maintained towards the addiction and no demand was made on him to give it up in the initial stages of analysis. This enabled him to gain insight into the oral problems behind the addiction. This facilitated a self-imposed weaning process. Acting out set a difficult technical problem. The patient progressed sufficiently to graduate from college, leave his parents' home, and enter work of his own choice.

428

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Samiksa, 8, 1954, No. 1

H. Racker. 'On the Confusion between Mania and Health.'

The risks in analysis of developing hypomanic defences (the denial of dependence and of guilt feelings) are outlined. These stem from the analyst's encouragement of independence and instinct liberation, based on his own inner anxieties.

434

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Samiksa, 8, 1954, No. 3

Geza Roheim. 'Wedding Ceremonies in European Folklore.'

This was intended as a chapter in a book on Hungarian folklore published by the author in 1925, but was not then included. Numerous rites are discussed in detail and seen to be fundamentally similar from country to country, but in some of them the meaning, which in essence is the overcoming of the Oedipus complex, is particularly clear.

434

responseSet.title: The Thematic Apperception Test and the Children's Apperception Test in Clinical Use: By Leopold Bellak, M.D.  (New York:  Grune & Stratton, 1954.  Pp. x + 282.  $6.75.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Thematic Apperception Test and the Children's Apperception Test in Clinical Use

By Leopold Bellak, M.D. (New York: Grune & Stratton, 1954. Pp. x + 282. $6.75.)

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler  

The T.A.T. is a psychological test which should appeal to psycho-analysts. The patient is asked to construct a story about each of 20 pictures, and these stories can be studied in the same way as the material brought by a patient to the analytic session.

Bellak views the T.A.T. stories from the standpoint of psycho-analytical ego-psychology, and provides a detailed scheme of assessment. This is a far more rewarding approach than many which have been attempted, and analysts will find themselves on quite familiar ground. Bellak devotes a special section to the study of character and defences, but rightly points out the difficulty of basing psychiatric diagnoses (e.g. of schizophrenia) on the test. There is also a section on the use of the T.A.T. in psychotherapy.

The Children's Apperception Test (C.A.T.) is a delightful one. Pictures with animal figures are used, and typical conflict-situations are represented. Children, being on the whole more naïve than adults, can produce ego-syntonic stories about the cards which show more clearly than in adults their anxieties, defences, and object-relations.

The C.A.T. is an excellent method for eliciting useful and relevant phantasy material from children and should prove a useful tool. The pictures also have a place alongside the toys in the analyst's playroom, where stories can be produced as part of the process of play, and can be dealt with in the same way.

As with all psychological tests, it is possible to be too enthusiastic, and the danger of 'wild' interpretations is always present. There is also the fact that the degree of apparent disturbance on the test will often bear little relation to the amount of actual disturbance, although the nature of the conflicts may be indicated. Quite normal subjects can produce the most bizarre and 'psychotic' phantasy material, and it is only too easy for an inexperienced psychologist to be misled.

420

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4: Don D. Jackson.  'Some Factors Influencing the Oedipus Complex.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 23, 1954, No. 4

Don D. Jackson. 'Some Factors Influencing the Oedipus Complex.'

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

This paper is designed to underline the rôle in shaping the Oedipus complex of the parents' interaction on one another at a critical period of the child's life. The similarities of the clinical picture presented by six women patients are related to a specific type of inter-parental relationship.

The mothers were unloving in early childhood, though they permitted a third person to be tender to the child. As their daughters grew older they manifested strong dependence needs. The fathers on the other hand lost interest as the girls grew older. They behaved in an alienating way, but were also, sporadically, harshly seductive. Both sets of parents used the daughters vicariously to express their hostility to each other without overt quarrels. The fathers deprecated the mothers' sexuality by their acknowledgement of the daughters'; the mothers encouraged the seductive exploitation of the fathers.

The patients were thus trained to feed on triadic involvement. In adult life they had to create triangular situations. Beneath an apparently strong sexual attachment to the father lay an unresolved longing for the mother which prevented genuine relationships with either sex. The overcompensatory attachment to the father made them despise their femininity. Their main devices for escaping their dilemma were pregnancy fantasies and childbearing. Most of them had occupations or activities which enabled them to minister to 'helpless' women.

426

responseSet.title: Margarete Steinbach, Madrid
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Margarete Steinbach, Madrid

Carl Müller-Braunschweig

Carl Müller-Braunschweig

Carl Müller-Braunschweig  

An unexpected loss was suffered by the German Psycho-Analytical Association through the sudden death in Madrid of Mrs. Margarete Steinbach on 9 April, 1954. When the Association began to function again in 1950, Mrs. Steinbach was one of the six analysts who re-established it under the direction of Dr. Carl Müller-Braunschweig.

Margarete Steinbach was born on 12 July, 1894. She passed her teacher's examination in 1914, and from 1921 to 1926 she was working at the German School in Madrid. She returned to Germany in 1926 and studied academic psychology at Berlin University under Köhler and Lewin. At the same time she began her training at the Berlin Psycho-Analytical Institute, and was analysed by Dr. Bally. In 1939 she became a member of the German Psycho-Analytical Association, which was then known as a study group of the German Institute for Psychological Research and Therapy. At the end of the 1939 war, she worked exclusively as a member of the German Psycho-Analytical Association within the framework of the Training Council of the Berlin Psychotherapists. She also lectured at the Institute for Psychotherapy, and taught at several schools, including the Teachers' Training College in Berlin. In March 1951 she was invited to undertake the training of psycho-analysts in Spain. In the short space of three years she was not only training analyst of nine doctors (mostly psychiatrists), two psychologists and one headmistress, but also undertook the control of cases and taught the theory of psycho-analysis. In addition she carried out a number of therapeutic analyses.

Margarete Steinbach had a thorough knowledge and understanding of the theoretical and practical aspects of psycho-analysis; she had a lively intelligence and tremendous drive which was of much help to her in her work of establishing the 'Asociacion Psicoanalítica Española'. News of the recognition of this association by the Spanish Government reached her a few hours before her death.

We shall always remember her as a friend and fellow-fighter in the cause of psycho-analysis, but she will be especially remembered for her work in laying the foundation for psycho-analysis in Spain.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Construction of Reality in the Child

By Jean Piaget. Translated by Margaret Cook. (New York: Basic Books, 1954. Pp. xiii + 386. $6.00.) The Child's Construction of Reality. (London: Routledge & Kegan Paul, 1995. Pp. xiii + 389. 25 s.)

E. Anthony

E. Anthony

E. Anthony  

The world outside our minds (said William James) is chaotic. Every moment threatens to submerge us beneath a welter of sensory experiences that compete for our attention and concentration. As a pre-condition for sanity, we must become selectively imperceptive, and suppress or inhibit a large part of this perceptual bombardment. The immature brain of the infant and the damaged brain lack the capacity to do this, responding in a distracted way to every passing stimulus. Their experience is of a 'booming, buzzing confusion'. To bring law and order into such chaos is one aspect of what Eddington has called 'world making'.

Another aspect is the creation of permanent objects. This implies that a certain genetic point of view postulates an 'objectless' phase in early infantile development that precedes the establishment of a substantial object organized in time and space. This so-called empirical belief considers that the object is gradually created through the warp and woof of maturation and experience. The nativists on the other hand conceive of an object apprehensible from birth. The pre-eminent and most cogent representative of the empirical viewpoint is Piaget and it is with his object concept that this work deals. It is his eleventh book to be translated into English, so that the developmental psychologist (academic or psycho-analytic) has now less excuse for overlooking this formidable system on the grounds of a language barrier. At this 'darkest of all ages' as Miss Freud has termed it, before the concept of a repressed unconscious begins to dichotomize the psychologies, data gleaned from the experimental-observational approach of the non-analyst may find an important niche in psycho-analytical formulations. It may also serve to temper the over-fanciful interpretation. Even the most encapsulated theorist will find it difficult to restrain his interest in this methodical description of the earliest relation to 'things' when the infant is apparently engaged in creating a world of external objects—no doubt as important for him as the postulated world of internal objects.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Survey of Clinical Practice in Psychology

Edited by Eli A. Rubenstein and Maurice Lorr. (New York: International Universities Press, 1954. Pp. 363. $6.00.)

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler  

419

The title of this book is somewhat misleading, especially to those who use the term 'psychology' in a fairly broad sense. It is an account of the work carried out by non-medical clinical psychologists in the United States. The first emotion aroused on reading it is one of surprise that there are so many wide and varied fields in which clinical psychologists are working. There are 27 chapters, each written by an expert, describing the work of psychologists in their own institutions or specialities. University and psychiatric clinics, the armed forces, clinics for alcoholics, the aged, and for student counselling; courts and prisons, rehabilitation centres, schools, and clinics for hearing, reading, and speech difficulties are all described. There clearly can be no such thing as a 'general practitioner' psychologist. He is inevitably a specialist, and must very often reach a high degree of professional competence.

The book gives a useful indication of the immense amount of work—including therapy—undertaken by psychologists in their various fields. Psycho-analysts will be disappointed at the very small amount of analytic training of any sort undergone by psychologists working with clinical material. The wealth of organizational detail given makes the work very useful for anyone who has to organize a department or clinic in any of the specialities discussed.

420

responseSet.title: Clinical Essay Prize
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Essay Prize

Ilse Hellman

Ilse Hellman

Ilse Hellman, Honorary Scientific Secretary
Institute of Psycho-Analysis
63 New Cavendish Street,
London, W.
 1.

Ilse Hellman  

Members and Associate Members of the International Psycho-Analytical Association are reminded that competitors for the Clinical Essay Prize must send in their work to the Hon. Scientific Secretary of the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 63 New Cavendish Street, London, W.1, by 31 March of the year in which they wish to enter the competition.

The conditions governing the competition are the following:

A prize of (not exceeding) 30 guineas is offered.

Requirements for the Essay

The essay shall consist of a clinical record of a case investigated by psycho-analytical methods. It should illustrate clearly the events and changes in the mental life of the patient and their relation to external environment. In awarding the prize, the Judges will pay attention to acuity of observation and the clarity with which the facts are stated. If the writer wishes to draw theoretical conclusions, he must bear in mind the necessity of making the evidence for such conclusions carry conviction.

It is recommended that the length of the essay should not exceed 20, 000 words.

Date of Sending in Essays: Language: Format, etc.

Essays must be submitted on or before the 31st day of March in any year, in the English language. They must be typescript on quarto paper with ample left-hand margin. They must be in triplicate and be sent to the Hon. Scientific Secretary of the Institute. All copies of essays submitted become ipso facto the property of the Institute (or its successor) while it has the appointment of the Trustees for the Prize Fund.

No Award

If no essay of merit worthy of a prize is submitted in any year, no award shall be made for that year.

Joint Award

In the event of the Judges regarding the essays of two or more competitors as of equal merit, they may divide the prize money into equal parts and award it to such competitors jointly.

Eligibility

Any person of either sex, who is not a member or a past-member of the Board of the Institute, shall be eligible for the competition.

Tenure

The prize shall be given to the writer of the best essay in the opinion of the Judges submitted in any year, but the prize may be awarded to the same person twice, provided that he submits a second essay of sufficient merit in a later competition, and that the prize shall not be awarded more than twice to the same person.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Maternal Dependency and Schizophrenia

Mothers and Daughters in a Therapeutic Group. A Group-Analytic Study. By Joseph Abrahams and Edith Varon. (New York: International Universities Press Inc., 1953. Pp. 240. $4.00.)

S. Foulkes

S. Foulkes

S. Foulkes  

This is the report of a highly original research carried out at St. Elizabeth's Hospital, Washington, as part of the Group Psychotherapy Project sponsored by the Veterans Administration. The authors conducted over a period of two years a group of eight, made up of four pairs of mothers and daughters. The daughters were young or adolescent women, chronically ill with schizophrenia, and in-patients of the hospital. They met, as a group, once a week for one hour, with their visiting mothers and the doctor and a research psychiatric social worker. The events of each session were thoroughly discussed by the psychiatrist and the P.S.W. and were recorded at the time. The technique used in utilizing the deep emotional involvement of therapist and observer is bold and exemplary.

The most important result is the emotional significance of the parents, especially the mother's dependence on the child for the genesis of the illness. Two fathers died from heart attacks during the period concerned.

The authors devote their first chapter to their approach to the investigation. Chapter II describes the mother-daughter pairs, and Chapter III, the longest chapter in the book, presents the development of the group through its various phases. There follows an analytical summary of the group's development and the mother-daughter relationship, and finally the authors give their conclusions.

This is a piece of work of the utmost interest for the problems of schizophrenic illness and at the same time a beautiful illustration of the value of group-analytic observation. The presentation is quite excellent.

358

responseSet.title: Essays on Sociology and Social Psychology: By Karl Mannheim.  Edited by P. Kecskemeti.  (London:  Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1953.  Pp. 310.  25 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Essays on Sociology and Social Psychology

By Karl Mannheim. Edited by P. Kecskemeti. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1953. Pp. 310. 25 s.)

P. Turquet

P. Turquet

P. Turquet  

With this the second volume, we now have in volume form all the shorter papers written by Professor Karl Mannheim. It contains a number of papers published elsewhere, but also four unpublished lectures on 'Planned Society and the Problem of Human Personality', originally given at Oxford. The psycho-analyst will find much to interest him, particularly in these lectures and more generally throughout the volume.

A major aspect of Mannheim's contribution to sociology concerns the sociology of knowledge, the main thesis of which is that ideas emerge and develop in response to, and are determined by, their socio-historical situation. Mannheim stressed that a particular perception of social reality will be determined by the social position from which it is viewed, and will vary with different social and historical settings. Part I of this volume with the important chapter on 'Conservative Thought' illustrates this type of sociological approach.

It is thus relevant to note than Mannheim himself changed after 1933 when he left Frankfurt for England. Like many of the Weimar intellectuals, he had seen himself as part of a 'socially unattached intelligentsia', analysing history but never part of it. His approach was historical, theoretical, and aloof, though the intellectual liberal in him believed he was part of a creative social force for the future. Meanwhile, history was to show that if the intelligentsia are to act as 'night watchmen' as Mannheim wished, and since night watchmen are usually the first to detect the presence of intruders, they must learn to grapple with their assailants until help comes. This situation, once described by Julien Benda as 'La Trahison des clercs', Mannheim experienced to the full and sought later to remedy. England taught him to be more pragmatic, and with this direct experience of social crisis he came to think more in terms of how to influence history. His experience in Germany gave an urgency to his teaching, and since he saw England moving towards a social crisis, he drew attention to those methods German sociologists, as a result of their longer experience of social crisis, had devised for the interpretation of complex general situations. The present volume contains three fruitful papers dealing with this aspect of his work with a comparison between German and Western sociology. Clinically speaking, his experience of a social crisis forced him to a 'here and now' approach, with a consequent enrichment of his work.

responseSet.title: The Analysis of an Obsessional: By R. W. Pickford.  (London:  Hogarth Press, 1954.  Pp. 223.  21 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analysis of an Obsessional

By R. W. Pickford. (London: Hogarth Press, 1954. Pp. 223. 21 s.)

M. Masud Khan

M. Masud Khan

M. Masud Khan  

In his book Mr. Pickford has set himself a twofold task, namely 'to present a full account of the treatment of a single patient', and 'to reveal fully the mental processes and functions involved in an individual's development from the earliest stages of infancy'. By all requirements it is a laudable undertaking, the fulfilment of which would have earned everyone's gratitude. But it is only fair to state that so far, within the analytic literature, no one except Freud has ever succeeded in doing justice to case histories, and unfortunately Mr. Pickford is no exception.

Mr. Pickford has succeeded in giving us a very succint and dynamic report of the phantasies and symptoms, dreams and behaviour patterns of a young man of 20 who had sought treatment mainly for obsessional symptoms and phobias. This report, however, is bound to be more helpful to those who are not engaged in daily practice and somewhat obvious to those who are.

The second part of the undertaking, namely the technique of handling the case, was what one had hoped to see more of in such a full report. Here, although Mr. Pickford's sympathy and understanding of the patient are always in evidence, the clarity of his own therapeutic handling is rather dubious. The treatment is conducted largely in terms of dream analysis, though transference and its interpretetion are allowed a fair share. But one begins to have doubt about Mr. Pickford's real understanding of the transference when one reads the following:

'The technique of dream analysis was adopted forthwith, and in the second interview the patient was invited to sit in a chair, facing the fire with the analyst, and to recount his dreams. This position is less usual than that in which the patient lies on a couch, but it was preferred at the start in this case, because the patient showed some resistance against any formalities which suggested medical treatment, and was afraid of lying on a couch or bed because that was the position in which he had been examined and had received injections which had much disturbed him. At later interviews he lay on the couch, with a large table placed between him and the analyst to reduce any impression of personal interference by the latter. In the first interviews, during which the patient was sitting, the analyst took advantage of a square column supporting the ceiling, and placed the chairs with it between them.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 2, 1954, No. 4
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 2, 1954, No. 4

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman  

I. THE WIDENING SCOPE OF INDICATIONS FOR PSYCHOANALYSIS

Arden House Conference, Harrison, New York, May 1954, sponsored by the New York Psychoanalytic Society.

Leo Stone. 'The Widening Scope of Indications for Psychoanalysis.'

Psycho-analysis, thanks to the new knowledge obtained from the character studies of Freud, Jones, and Abraham, and Anna Freud's The Ego and the Mechanisms of Defense, has gradually expanded its fields of interest. To-day in the United States almost any illness which has a significant emotional component in its aetiology has become a possible indication for psycho-analysis. There is an unfortunate tendency amongst the American public to ascribe a magical power to psycho-analysis and a trend amongst medical practitioners to find psychogenic factors when the physical aspects of the disease are more important. Psycho-analysis should not be undertaken for trivial ailments. Mild incipient neuroses need not be an indication for analysis if the personality deviation is minimal. Stone describes the basic elements of the analytical process and discusses the technical alterations which may be required when it comes to applying the method to severe illnesses.

Analysis must be differentiated from other psychotherapies, particularly from interpretative psychotherapies. Some such general concept as 'modified psycho-analysis' would be useful to distinguish a method which utilizes many of the operational concepts of psycho-analysis. How far can the classical analytic method be modified and still be regarded as psycho-analysis? In Stone's opinion any number and degree of parameters (Eissler, 1953) can be introduced as long as they are directed towards the aims of the analytical effort. The effect of these parameters on the transference can be minimized as long as they are genuinely therapeutic, i.e. that they are actions bound to the immediate reality.

The suitability of psychotic illness for 'psycho-analytic office practice' depends upon the patient's awareness of his abnormal state. Similar considerations apply to the perversions and addictions. In current practice character disorders are frequently encountered and many of them approximate to psychosis. The 'border-line' case provides numerous problems for the analyst—whether or not to treat being the most important. This account of therapeutic possibilities is followed by a discussion of narcissism and transference. Stone distinguishes between the transference love of the patient with hysteria and the primitive phenomena of narcissistic transferences. The psychotic patient's transference is liable to engulf his whole personality. The fear of such emotions may be the cause of the patient's extreme detachment. When the transference does break through insatiable demands may be made or amongst other possibilities the transference may be literally narcissistic, i.e. the therapist does become confused with the self or is like the self in all respects. After reviewing some of the methods which have been recommended for the borderline case, Stone expresses his own views on the matter. For him the decisive factor is the capacity to tolerate the powerful and tormenting transference and potential countertransference situations. Looking back on his experience he believes that the best plan with these patients is to encourage a build-up of positive transference in the sense of an actual personal relationship which will stand the strain of hostility. The paper concludes with problems of the diagnosis and prognosis of 'borderline states'.

responseSet.title: A General Selection from the Works of Sigmund Freud: Edited by John Rickman.  Psycho-Analytical Epitomes No. 1.  New Impression.  (London:  Hogarth Press and the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 1953.  10 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A General Selection from the Works of Sigmund Freud

Edited by John Rickman. Psycho-Analytical Epitomes No. 1. New Impression. (London: Hogarth Press and the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 1953. 10 s. 6 d.)

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer  

The first volume of John Rickman's Psycho-Analytical Epitomes was published in 1937. During his lifetime the founder and editor of the series had the satisfaction of seeing it grow, though discrimination in the choice of works and the war years made this growth a slow one. Quite recently the publishers announced that the four volumes so far published are to be re-issued and that further works will be added in the near future under the editorship of Dr. W. H. Gillespie and Dr. W. Clifford M. Scott.

It should be noted that these newly printed volumes are bound very attractively in linen of bright blue colour.

The present volume contains selected chapters from Freud's books and papers written between 1910 and 1926, and it calls upon the reader to let himself be guided through some of the most difficult parts of psycho-analytic theory. After he has found his way through he will enjoy the satisfaction of having acquired not only knowledge of a cross-section of Freud's work but also some glimpses of its development. The latter he could not have achieved merely from the study of Freud's last summary, An Outline of Psycho-Analysis (London: Hogarth Press) published only three years after this anthology. If the reader should desire to learn with ease more of the clinical side, as undoubtedly he will, this reviewer would still recommend the reading of Freud's The Question of Lay Analysis (London: Imago Publishing Co.) which apart from the discussion of the problem laid down in the title contains Freud's most concise presentation of what really happens in psycho-analytic therapy: a description in miniature of the neuroses and their cure.

All these publications will however one day be outdated when, thanks to the devotion of Mr. James Strachey and the support of our colleagues in America, the Standard Edition of the Complete Works of Sigmund Freud is completed. The full range of Freud's work will then be at the reader's disposal from which to make his choice. Mean-while Epitome No. 1 will have to be taken as an hors d'oeuvre which should stimulate and prepare the reader for a full course of Freud.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly: Melitta Sperling.  'Food Allergies and Conversion Hysteria.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

Melitta Sperling. 'Food Allergies and Conversion Hysteria.'

Sperling uses the analysis of a patient showing both food allergies and hysterical conversion symptoms to support three propositions.

1. Food allergies may be the product of specific unconscious fantasies about specific items of food. Such specificity has been little stressed in psycho-analytic literature and is contrary to the usual emphasis in psychosomatic medicine on physiological responses and conscious attitudes.

2. The outcome of a conflict in conversion is

219

influenced by the relation between ego strength, adequacy of repression, and the possibility of conscious control of the threatening impulse. Sperling illustrates this from the following series of reactions in the patient: (a) Before analysis, the immediate conversion of impulses into somatic symptoms without anxiety. (b) During analysis, when repressed impulses were about to emerge into consciousness, the experience of anxiety but no conversion. (c) In later analysis the patient experienced only disgust with neither anxiety nor conversion. The author suggests that the immediate somatic expression of an impulse prevents it from being acted out, and this saves the patient from the feared 'break' with reality.

3. Anal eroticism is a specific genetic and dynamic factor in allergic conditions. The anal meaning of food was clear in this patient whose pseudo-genital satisfactions depended in fact on unconscious pregenital fantasies. Though the importance of anal eroticism in pregenital conversions has been emphasized by other writers, Sperling is doubtful if the distinction between hysterical and pregenital conversion has not been over-emphasized. She suggests that early genital stimulation and seduction may play a role in a confusion of genital objects and aims with pregenital ones.

220

[[RunningHead]]

Samiksa, 8, 1954, No. 1

M. W. Brody. 'A Psycho-analytical Interpretation of Rapunzel.'

Grimm's fairy-tale 'Rapunzel' portrays the endless cycle of life to death in relation to Mother Earth. This theme repeats itself in the tale, and, it is suggested, is finally symbolized in the birth of twins.
