responseSet.title: A Note on the Compatibility of Psychic Determinism and Freedom of will
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Note on the Compatibility of Psychic Determinism and Freedom of will

Samuel Lipton M.D.

Samuel Lipton

Samuel D. Lipton, M.D.
CHICAGO, ILL.

Samuel Lipton  

Although the concept of psychic determinism is generally accepted without qualification as an aspect of scientific causality, it sometimes appears difficult to reconcile it with the feeling of freedom of will. One of Freud's (1) statements about this was to the effect that the belief in psychic freedom is deep-rooted but unscientific, and that 'it must give ground before the claims of a determinism which governs even mental life'. This was written before he introduced the structural point of view, and the purpose of this brief note is to mention that the recognition of the subsequently-developed concept of intra-systemic differentiation of function within the ego permits an understanding of the concurrent existence of both concepts without either giving one up, as Freud suggests, or making any compromise with determinism, such as, for example, Glover's (2). The latter writes that the 'concept of psychic determinism is one that usually provokes discomfort in the minds of laymen as well as metaphysicians', and goes on to suggest that the difficulty can be overcome by remembering that in so far as the ego gains mastery over unconscious impulses, 'it may rightly claim it has acquired some "freed-will"'.

The feeling of freedom of will seems to be one of the subjective sensations of the normal ego, originating, presumably, in the awareness of the ego's mastery over unconscious impulses. The feeling persists despite the intellectual acquisition of the principle of causality; even the subsequent analysis of a specific action felt to be freely chosen does not dispel the feeling. For example, one may choose a number at random, analyse its determinants by means of free association, yet still retain the feeling that the number was chosen at random, and, of course, retain the feeling that one is again free to make a random choice. Conversely, reactions outside the range of the feeling of freewill are commonly felt as symptoms or other types of ego-dystonic intrusions.

It follows that the concept of psychic determinism is foreign and repugnant to the aspect of the ego which senses its own functioning and integrity as foreign as the id, of which determinism is the intellectual representative, and subject to a similar repression. The concept can be accepted only by the intellectual faculty of the ego, which has the capacity to stand apart from its sensation of its own functioning; in other words, the concept can command only intellectual, and not emotional, validity. In other fields of science there are many such concepts which depend exclusively on intellectual validation and cannot be felt, but in psycho-analysis we know that that type of validation is ordinarily insufficient. It is perhaps, uniquely, determinism which we can experience no more deeply. A feeling of determinism, in distinction to an intellectual conviction, may indeed exist in various forms, but only as an ephemeral or lasting disturbance in ego function.

Some Developmental Aspects of the Body Image

Louis Linn M.D.

Louis Linn

Louis Linn, M.D.
NEW YORK

Louis Linn  

SUMMARY

The work of Bender and his co-workers concerning double simultaneous cutaneous stimulation is reviewed. Theoretical implications of their findings are presented, particularly as they impinge on the developmental history of the body image.

responseSet.title: Dr. Georges Parcheminey, Paris
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dr. Georges Parcheminey, Paris

The death of Dr. Georges Parcheminey in September 1953 is a great loss to the Société Psychanalytique de Paris.

Not long before his death Dr. Georges Parcheminey had been elected President of the French Psycho-Analytical Society for a second term of office when that Society was dealing with grave internal difficulties. Born in Haute-Saône, he had come to Paris to study medicine and subsequently had become a teacher in the pre-clinical subjects. His intellectual curiosity made him turn towards psycho-analysis some thirty years ago and he founded with some young psychiatrists a Society for Psychiatric Evolution which later became the French Psycho-Analytical Society. He devoted himself to the study of Freud's work and his familiarity with German made it easy for him to acquire an intimate knowledge of our literature. He accepted and practised Freud's technique with the same care that had already characterized his work as a pathologist and medical practitioner. His judgement, tact, and diplomatic skill helped greatly in re-establishing harmony and tolerance within French psycho-analysis. No sacrifice or personal suffering was too great for him, and he thoroughly disliked conflicts rooted in the craving for power. The same ideal of self-control and sensitiveness made itself felt in his personal conduct with patients, colleagues, friends, and last, but not least, in his piano playing.

His psycho-analytic publications are numerous and cover many aspects, predominantly those of hysteria and conversion symptoms.

(From the Obituary by Michel Cenac, Revue Française de Psychanalyse, tome 17, October-November, No. 4, 1953.)

71

responseSet.title: Emergence of Sleep During Psycho-Analysis—A Clinical Note
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Emergence of Sleep During Psycho-Analysis—A Clinical Note

Alan Parkin

Alan Parkin

Alan Parkin
LONDON

Alan Parkin  

This is a report of two consecutive hours from the early part of the second year of the analysis of a 36-year-old physician.

His main symptom is his belief that various people infect him by throwing or squirting gonorrheal pus over him and sometimes by spitting or urinating upon him. During such periods of infection, which sometimes occur several times a day, he shuts himself away and for hours attempts to disinfect himself by desperate and frantic methods which are always accompanied by an overwhelming sense of guilt. In the interim periods he is rarely free of the fear of being infected by others, and his thoughts are concerned almost constantly with ways in which he can detect his potential enemies and forestall their attack. In spite of these severe handicaps, he is able to discharge his responsibilities as a husband, father, and busy G.P. He is rarely early in arriving for his analytic hours, partly to avoid remaining in the potentially dangerous waiting room, and if he finds the consulting room to be empty, he often waits there.

At the beginning of the first of these two hours I approached the consulting room and observing it to be empty through the half-open door, I went to the waiting room. Not finding him there, I returned to the consulting room and saw him through the doorway waiting there for me. After reclining upon the couch he spoke at some length of his dissatisfaction with his ability to associate freely. He had resolved that that day, being not only the first day of the week, but also the first day of the month, he would make a determined effort to give his associations in the way he believed was expected of him, namely by reporting words or phrases while in a state of reverie. As he talked he seemed to drowse and after a brief pause said 'I didn't know you were coming.' After a longer pause, during which he seemed to continue to drowse, I replied, 'I didn't know you were here.' Immediately he became attentive and said he had visualized a pregnant patient of his who had made an unexpected professional call upon him. He then considered the remark I had made and said that on his arrival he had observed at the foot of the couch a blanket which he had been accustomed to see upon a nearby chair. He had been on the point of removing it when he was struck by the belief that it was dangerously infected, and in his sudden indecision felt that his overcoat, which he had just hung up behind the door, was also infected. The latter feeling, being the more imperative, dispelled the former, and he had quickly placed the blanket upon the chair in his hurry to turn his attention to the overcoat. While frantically rubbing at the latter he had heard me approach the consulting room unexpectedly, and then disappear. Knowing that I would return in a moment and not wanting to be discovered in an act which was for him accompanied by such guilt, he quickly stepped from behind the door to wait for my return. His feeling of guilt about these events which had prevented him from mentioning them at the beginning of the hour was verbalized, and he related it to the feeling which followed masturbation.

A Note on Blathering11

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford M. Scott
BETHLEM ROYAL HOSPITAL AND MAUDSLEY HOSPITAL
LONDON

W. Clifford Scott  

SUMMARY

Blathering may be a useful word to apply to a specific early, oral, libidinal activity and its connected fantasies. During interpretation of the transference situation, when blathering anxiety is unconsciously present, blathering, not as a word but as an act, may be useful.

responseSet.title: The Rôle of the Superego and Ego-Ideal in Character Formation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Rôle of the Superego and Ego-Ideal in Character Formation

Samuel Novey M.D.

Samuel Novey

Samuel Novey, M.D.
BALTIMORE

Samuel Novey  

SUMMARY

i. The development of character analysis and of the modern concept of the ego has made necessary a change in our concept of the superego and ego-ideal.

ii. The superego is best looked upon as a functional pattern of introjection rather than as a fixed institution.

iii. The separation of superego and ego-ideal

offers the possibility of the further clarification of the rôle of introjection in character formation.

iv. The ego-ideal has its roots in primitive parental identifications.

v. The ego-ideal in the mature individual is dependent on these early identifications as well as upon later significant persons, but not on either of these alone.

vi. The ego-ideal is a distinct psychic institution related to the ego and superego, and it makes essential contributions to the process of sublimation bound by an inner reality making for inspired performance.

vii. The technique of psycho-analysis includes not only the tracing of these later ego-ideals to their genetic prototypes, but the analysing of them as significant contributors to character formation.

responseSet.title: Notes on Parapsychology and Parapsychological Healing
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Notes on Parapsychology and Parapsychological Healing

Michael Balint

Michael Balint

Michael Balint
LONDON

Michael Balint  

I

I think it is fair to say that a number of experienced analysts have met episodes in their practice which have struck them as probable cases of telepathy or extrasensory perception (called E.S.P.).1 Quite often these apparent cases of E.S.P. when subjected to a searching scrutiny reveal possibilities of other mechanisms, such as the emergence of a forgotten piece of memory already communicated; an interpretation of the analyst's behaviour, sentiments, or thoughts, by the patient based partly on consciously observed and partly on not quite consciously observed facts; the unexpected appearance in consciousness of identification with the analyst, based on intensive transference and counter-transference; the analyst projecting his own emotional involvement into the patient's material and interpreting it in this sense; analyst and patient who share experiences far beyond what can be expressed in words, coming unexpectedly to identical conclusions, etc.

To show what I mean I wish to report an event which, although almost certainly not a case of telepathy or precognition, illustrates some of the complications involved in clinical observations of this kind. In 1938, when the Szondi test was still in its experimental stages, and only very few people knew about it, I became interested and experimented with it myself. One evening when there was a party in my house we came to talk about this new test, and two sisters, not very closely acquainted with me, volunteered to be guinea-pigs. In an adjoining room I first tested the younger of the two, aged twenty-three and unmarried. After she had done the test, we began to chat, and she mentioned that it would be quite easy to indicate which pictures another person whom one knew fairly well would choose. (The test makes use of forty-eight photographs which are exposed in six sets of eight pictures each. The subject is asked to choose from each set two pictures that he likes and two that he dislikes, making a total of twelve likes and twelve dislikes.) I challenged her to do the test as her sister would do it, and she readily agreed. After we had finished I asked the elder, married, sister aged twenty-seven, to do the test, and found that of the twenty-four pictures she chose eighteen had been indicated correctly by her younger sister. After her test was finished I asked her whether she would be interested in doing the test a she thought it would be done by her younger sister, but she quite casually refused.

responseSet.title: On the Motivation of Human Relationships—A Preliminary Study Based on the Concept of Sublimation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Motivation of Human Relationships—A Preliminary Study Based on the Concept of Sublimation11

Barbara Lantos M.D.

Barbara Lantos

Barbara Lantos, M.D.
LONDON

Barbara Lantos  

THE HISTORICAL PERSPECTIVE

Before the social sciences emerged, some 200 years ago, human relationships were the domain of religion, ethics, and philosophy. The social sciences, being born in an already scientific age, looked, to further their researches, for something like a basic science of the mind. By building itself on observations, experiments, and statistics, psychology—after breaking away from philosophy—offered itself as such a science, trying to reach close proximity to the natural sciences. The natural sciences, however, are still suspicious of the new 'in-law', while the growing demand by modern anthropologists, sociologists, and economists to include psycho-analysis within their training syllabus indicates that academic psychology did not quite satisfy their search for this basic science of the mind.

Psycho-analysis did not start out as a philosophy or even as a general psychology to establish the basic dynamics of the mind. It began quite modestly in the neurological clinic, and the status of psycho-analysis as a tenet basic to the social sciences shows a curious example of Hegelian dialectic, looked at from the point of view of its founder. We know from Freud's autobiography that from early youth he was more curious about human concerns than about natural objects. Throughout his schooldays he wanted to study law and engage in public activities. It was under the influence of Darwin and of Goethe's beautiful essay on Nature that he suddenly decided to study medicine. The change from research work in anatomy and physiology of the nervous system to practical work in nervous diseases seems to have been made under external pressure. That this study, starting out with the physical symptoms of hysteria, should lead him ultimately to the discovery of the basic dynamics of the human mind was certainly not expected by him; but perhaps it was not altogether haphazard.

We know that parallel to the clinical studies on the neuroses (see his 'Early Papers', 1892–1906) he was, without being at first aware of the connection, interested in dreams. Quite privately he jotted down notes—long hidden—on the work of the mental apparatus. After 13 years of detailed clinical observation, however, the Three Essays(1905) emerged, a work which, in my view, is the basis for a genetic and dynamic understanding of human relationships. Not that Freud claimed this. He remained the clinician, drawing his conclusions of a more general and theoretical nature from the very field of everyday and clinical observations. The papers dealing more explicitly with problems of human development into civilization and of civilized society ('Civilized Sexual Morality and Modern Nervousness, ' 1908);(Totem and Taboo, 1912); (Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego, 1921);(The Future of an Illusion, 1927, and Civilization and its Discontents, 1930) and those dealing with artistic or religious problems (on Leonardo da Vinci, 1910; Michael Angelo, 1914; Goethe, 1917; Dostoyevsky, 1928; his Moses book, 1937, and many others) are all based on and linked up with clinical observations.
